config.title: Understanding LLMs by Building One
config.tagline: Build a GPT from scratch, one concept at a time, from pure Python to production serving.

labs.title: microGPT, Pure Python
labs.tagline: Karpathy's original microGPT, unmodified except for the dataset path. The complete algorithm in one file.
labs.description:
This is where it all starts — Andrej Karpathy's microGPT, a complete GPT implementation in a single file of pure Python with zero dependencies.

The code builds a scalar autograd engine (the Value class), implements a character-level transformer with multi-head attention, RMSNorm, and a linear learning rate schedule, then trains it on ~32,000 human names. Every operation — every multiply, every addition — is tracked in a computation graph so gradients can flow backward automatically.

This is the most educational implementation: you can literally trace every number through the network. It's also the slowest, because Python loops over individual scalars. The next labs will show how frameworks eliminate that overhead while keeping the same algorithm.
learning_objectives: Trace scalar values through a computation graph
Explain how automatic differentiation computes gradients
Identify the core components of a transformer architecture

exercises.prompt: Try changing the number of attention heads in the model and observe how it affects the generated names. What happens with 1 head vs. 4 heads?
exercises.hint: Look for the n_head parameter near the top of the file. Run the model with different values and compare the output after the same number of training steps.

exercises.prompt: Add a print statement inside the backward pass of the Value class to trace how gradients flow through a single addition operation. What order are the gradients computed in?
exercises.hint: Find the _backward closure defined inside __add__. Print self.grad before and after the backward call to see the chain rule in action.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — pure-Python scalar autograd edition.

A minimal decoder-only GPT built entirely from scratch in Python, with no
external libraries. The scalar autograd engine is inspired by Andrej Karpathy's
micrograd (https://github.com/karpathy/micrograd), and the character-level
language model follows his makemore (https://github.com/karpathy/makemore).

The transformer architecture originates from "Attention Is All You Need"
(Vaswani et al., 2017, https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762), though this
implementation uses only the decoder half rather than the full encoder-decoder
design. Normalization follows "Root Mean Square Layer Normalization" (Zhang &
Sennrich, 2019, https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.07467), replacing the original
paper's LayerNorm with the simpler RMSNorm.
"""

import os
import math
import random

random.seed(42)

input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), '..', '..', 'data', 'input.txt')
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request
    names_url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/988aa59/names.txt'
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(names_url, input_path)

docs = [line.strip() for line in open(input_path) if line.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set(''.join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

class Value:
    __slots__ = ('data', 'grad', '_children', '_local_grads')

    def __init__(self, data, children=(), local_grads=()):
        self.data = data
        self.grad = 0
        self._children = children
        self._local_grads = local_grads

    def __add__(self, other):
        other = other if isinstance(other, Value) else Value(other)
        return Value(self.data + other.data, (self, other), (1, 1))

    def __mul__(self, other):
        other = other if isinstance(other, Value) else Value(other)
        return Value(self.data * other.data, (self, other), (other.data, self.data))

    def __pow__(self, other): return Value(self.data**other, (self,), (other * self.data**(other-1),))
    def log(self): return Value(math.log(self.data), (self,), (1/self.data,))
    def exp(self): return Value(math.exp(self.data), (self,), (math.exp(self.data),))
    def relu(self): return Value(max(0, self.data), (self,), (float(self.data > 0),))
    def __neg__(self): return self * -1
    def __radd__(self, other): return self + other
    def __sub__(self, other): return self + (-other)
    def __rsub__(self, other): return other + (-self)
    def __rmul__(self, other): return self * other
    def __truediv__(self, other): return self * other**-1
    def __rtruediv__(self, other): return other * self**-1

    def backward(self):
        topo = []
        visited = set()
        def build_topo(v):
            if v not in visited:
                visited.add(v)
                for child in v._children:
                    build_topo(child)
                topo.append(v)
        build_topo(self)
        self.grad = 1
        for v in reversed(topo):
            for child, local_grad in zip(v._children, v._local_grads):
                child.grad += local_grad * v.grad

n_layer = 1
n_embd = 16
block_size = 16
n_head = 4
head_dim = n_embd // n_head

matrix = lambda nout, nin, std=0.08: [[Value(random.gauss(0, std)) for _ in range(nin)] for _ in range(nout)]

state_dict = {'wte': matrix(vocab_size, n_embd), 'wpe': matrix(block_size, n_embd), 'lm_head': matrix(vocab_size, n_embd)}

for i in range(n_layer):
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.attn_wq'] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.attn_wk'] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.attn_wv'] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.attn_wo'] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.mlp_fc1'] = matrix(4 * n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f'layer{i}.mlp_fc2'] = matrix(n_embd, 4 * n_embd)

params = [p for mat in state_dict.values() for row in mat for p in row]
print(f"num params: {len(params)}")

def linear(x, w):
    return [sum(wi * xi for wi, xi in zip(wo, x)) for wo in w]

def softmax(logits):
    max_val = max(val.data for val in logits)
    exps = [(val - max_val).exp() for val in logits]
    total = sum(exps)
    return [e / total for e in exps]

def rmsnorm(x):
    ms = sum(xi * xi for xi in x) / len(x)
    scale = (ms + 1e-5) ** -0.5
    return [xi * scale for xi in x]

def gpt(token_id, pos_id, keys, values):
    tok_emb = state_dict['wte'][token_id]
    pos_emb = state_dict['wpe'][pos_id]
    x = [t + p for t, p in zip(tok_emb, pos_emb)]
    x = rmsnorm(x)

    for li in range(n_layer):
        x_residual = x
        x = rmsnorm(x)
        q = linear(x, state_dict[f'layer{li}.attn_wq'])
        k = linear(x, state_dict[f'layer{li}.attn_wk'])
        v = linear(x, state_dict[f'layer{li}.attn_wv'])
        keys[li].append(k)
        values[li].append(v)

        x_attn = []
        for h in range(n_head):
            hs = h * head_dim
            q_h = q[hs:hs+head_dim]
            k_h = [ki[hs:hs+head_dim] for ki in keys[li]]
            v_h = [vi[hs:hs+head_dim] for vi in values[li]]
            attn_logits = [sum(q_h[j] * k_h[t][j] for j in range(head_dim)) / head_dim**0.5 for t in range(len(k_h))]
            attn_weights = softmax(attn_logits)
            head_out = [sum(attn_weights[t] * v_h[t][j] for t in range(len(v_h))) for j in range(head_dim)]
            x_attn.extend(head_out)

        x = linear(x_attn, state_dict[f'layer{li}.attn_wo'])
        x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_residual)]

        x_residual = x
        x = rmsnorm(x)
        x = linear(x, state_dict[f'layer{li}.mlp_fc1'])
        x = [xi.relu() for xi in x]
        x = linear(x, state_dict[f'layer{li}.mlp_fc2'])
        x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_residual)]

    logits = linear(x, state_dict['lm_head'])
    return logits

learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 0.01, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8
m = [0.0] * len(params)
v = [0.0] * len(params)

num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    keys, values = [[] for _ in range(n_layer)], [[] for _ in range(n_layer)]
    losses = []

    for pos_id in range(n):
        token_id, target_id = tokens[pos_id], tokens[pos_id + 1]
        logits = gpt(token_id, pos_id, keys, values)
        probs = softmax(logits)
        loss_t = -probs[target_id].log()
        losses.append(loss_t)

    loss = (1 / n) * sum(losses)
    loss.backward()

    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)

    for i, p in enumerate(params):
        m[i] = beta1 * m[i] + (1 - beta1) * p.grad
        v[i] = beta2 * v[i] + (1 - beta2) * p.grad ** 2
        m_hat = m[i] / (1 - beta1 ** (step + 1))
        v_hat = v[i] / (1 - beta2 ** (step + 1))
        p.data -= lr_t * m_hat / (v_hat ** 0.5 + eps_adam)
        p.grad = 0

    print(f"step {step+1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.data:.4f}", end='\r')

temperature = 0.5
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")

for sample_idx in range(20):
    keys, values = [[] for _ in range(n_layer)], [[] for _ in range(n_layer)]
    token_id = BOS
    sample = []

    for pos_id in range(block_size):
        logits = gpt(token_id, pos_id, keys, values)
        probs = softmax([l / temperature for l in logits])
        token_id = random.choices(range(vocab_size), weights=[p.data for p in probs])[0]

        if token_id == BOS:
            break

        sample.append(uchars[token_id])

    print(f"sample {sample_idx+1:2d}: {''.join(sample)}")

17.text: This is the irreducible version of the whole project: no tensor library, no autograd framework, just enough Python to expose every moving part of a tiny GPT.

18.text: The math toolkit is deliberately tiny. That is part of the lesson: underneath all the framework machinery, a language model is still just arithmetic, exponentials, and the chain rule.

19.text: Randomness enters in three places that matter later too: how weights start, what order the data arrives in, and which token gets sampled at generation time.

21.text: Fixed seed so every run is identical. Essential for debugging when something goes wrong.

23.text: Locate the shared corpus. Even in the pure-Python version, we want the exact same learning problem the later labs will use.

24.text: Fetch the corpus if needed so the tutorial can be run from a fresh checkout with no manual setup.

25.text: Only import the downloader on demand. The code stays focused on the model until the dataset actually has to be fetched.

26.text: A tiny but rich dataset: enough spelling structure to learn real patterns, small enough that we can inspect the whole training story by eye.

27.text: One HTTP call and we have our training data. No pip install, no API key.

29.text: Each document is one name. Strip whitespace, skip blanks. The model will learn the statistical patterns of how names are spelled.

30.text: Shuffling prevents the model from memorizing the alphabetical order of the dataset.

31.text: Quick sanity check. Expect around 32K names.

33.text: The vocabulary is just the unique characters across all names, typically a-z. Sorting gives us a stable char-to-ID mapping.

34.text: A special token marking where names begin and end. Its ID is one past the last character, so it never collides with a real letter.

35.text: Total vocabulary: 26 letters + 1 BOS/EOS token = 27 symbols. That's the entire language.

36.text: Print it to confirm. 27 is a beautifully small number to debug with.

38.text: The heart of the autograd engine. Every Value wraps a single scalar and knows how to compute its own gradient.

39.text: __slots__ saves memory by preventing Python from creating a __dict__ per instance. Matters when you have thousands of Values in the computation graph.

41.text: Each Value stores four things: the number itself, its gradient, what produced it, and the local derivatives connecting it to its parents.

42.text: The forward pass fills data; the backward pass fills grad.

43.text: Starts at zero because gradients accumulate. Consider a simple example: x=2, y=3, z=x*y, loss=z+x. Here x is used twice — once in the multiply and once in the addition — so it receives gradient from both paths. That's why we start at zero and use += later.

44.text: The computation graph is built implicitly: every operation creates a new Value that remembers its inputs.

45.text: Local gradients are the partial derivatives of this node with respect to each child. The chain rule's building blocks.

47.text: Operator overloading is the trick that makes autograd invisible. Writing a + b secretly builds the computation graph.

48.text: If you add a plain number to a Value, wrap it first. This lets you write x + 1 naturally.

49.text: Addition: result is a + b, and both local gradients are 1 because d(a+b)/da = d(a+b)/db = 1. In our example loss = z + x: loss.grad=1 flows equally to both z and x — each gets 1*1 = 1.

51.text: Multiplication builds the graph and stores local gradients. The derivative of a*b w.r.t. a is b, and vice versa.

52.text: Same wrapping trick as addition. Consistency means you can mix Values and raw numbers freely.

53.text: Product rule: d(a*b)/da = b and d(a*b)/db = a. In our example z = x*y with x=2, y=3: local_grads are (y=3, x=2). When z.grad=1 arrives from backprop, x gets += 1*3 = 3 and y gets += 1*2 = 2.

55.text: Power rule as a one-liner: d(x^n)/dx = n*x^(n-1). The exponent must be a plain number, not a Value, which is fine for division and square roots.

56.text: Natural log with its gradient 1/x, all in one line. This appears in cross-entropy loss where -log(p) measures prediction quality.

57.text: Exponential function: the only function that is its own derivative. Essential for softmax, which converts logits into probabilities.

58.text: ReLU in one shot: pass positives through, kill negatives. The gradient is a simple gate, 1 if positive and 0 otherwise.

59.text: Negation reuses multiplication: -x is just x * -1. No new backward logic needed.

60.text: Right-add handles 3 + value by flipping to value + 3. Python calls this when the left operand doesn't know how to add a Value.

61.text: Subtraction: a - b becomes a + (-b). Composing existing ops means the chain rule handles it automatically.

62.text: Right-subtraction for when a plain number is on the left: 3 - value. Flip and negate.

63.text: Right-multiply: 3 * value becomes value * 3. Multiplication is commutative, so the graph-building in __mul__ handles it.

64.text: Division is multiplication by the reciprocal: a / b becomes a * b**-1. The power rule gives us division's gradient for free.

65.text: Right-division: 3 / value becomes 3 * value**-1. Every arithmetic operation on Values is now covered.

67.text: Backpropagation: walk the computation graph in reverse topological order, applying the chain rule at each node.

68.text: Topological sort ensures we process each node only after all nodes that depend on it.

69.text: Track which nodes we've already visited to avoid processing the same node twice.

70.text: Recursive depth-first traversal to build the topological ordering. The classic algorithm.

71.text: Only process each node once, even if it's used in multiple operations.

72.text: Mark as visited before recursing into children.

73.text: Recurse into every input that produced this node.

74.text: Depth-first: process all ancestors before this node.

75.text: Post-order append: this node goes after all its children in the list.

76.text: Start the sort from the loss node. It's the root of the entire computation graph.

77.text: The seed: dloss/dloss = 1. In our example (loss = z + x = x*y + x): this 1 flows backward through the addition, then through the multiply, accumulating at each parameter.

78.text: Reversed topological order means we visit the loss first, then work backward toward the parameters.

79.text: For each node, pair up children with their local gradients.

80.text: The += is where multi-path gradients merge. In our example: x gets 1 from the addition path AND 3 from the multiply path, so x.grad = 1 + 3 = 4. Check: d(x*y+x)/dx = y+1 = 3+1 = 4. The chain rule works.

82.text: Just 1 transformer layer. Real GPTs stack 12-96+ layers, but one layer already captures interesting patterns.

83.text: 16 dimensions per token embedding. Tiny by modern standards (GPT-3 uses 12,288), but enough to learn name patterns.

84.text: Names longer than 16 characters get truncated. Most names fit easily.

85.text: 4 attention heads, each seeing a 4-dimensional slice. Multiple heads let the model attend to different types of relationships simultaneously.

86.text: Each head gets n_embd / n_head = 4 dimensions. The heads work in parallel on different subspaces.

88.text: A helper to create a 2D list of random Values. This is how we initialize weight matrices without NumPy, just nested list comprehensions.

90.text: The three core weight matrices: token embeddings (what each character means), position embeddings (where it sits), and the output head (predicting the next character).

92.text: For each transformer layer, allocate the attention and MLP weight matrices.

93.text: Query weights: "what am I looking for?"

95.text: Value weights: "what information do I carry?"

96.text: Output projection: recombines the multi-head attention outputs back into a single vector.

97.text: MLP first layer expands to 4x the embedding dimension. A wider hidden layer gives the network more capacity.

98.text: MLP second layer projects back down to the embedding dimension.

100.text: Flatten all weight matrices into one list so the optimizer can iterate over every parameter uniformly.

101.text: Print parameter count for sanity. With these settings, expect a few thousand parameters, a truly micro GPT.

103.text: A linear layer as a pure function: matrix-vector multiply. No classes, no frameworks, just math.

104.text: Each output element is a dot product between one row of the weight matrix and the input vector. This is one row of Wx.

106.text: Softmax converts raw scores (logits) into a probability distribution that sums to 1.

107.text: Subtract the max for numerical stability. Prevents exp() from overflowing. Doesn't change the output probabilities.

108.text: Exponentiate to make everything positive. The subtraction ensures the largest value becomes exp(0) = 1.

109.text: Sum of exponentials becomes the normalizing constant.

110.text: Each probability is its exponential divided by the total. Now they sum to 1 and represent a valid distribution.

112.text: RMSNorm: a simpler, faster alternative to LayerNorm. No mean subtraction, no learned parameters, just scale by the root mean square.

113.text: Mean of squared values. Measures the "energy" of the vector.

114.text: Inverse square root scaling factor. The 1e-5 epsilon prevents division by zero when all values are near zero.

115.text: Scale each element so the vector has roughly unit RMS. Stabilizes training by keeping activations bounded.

117.text: The GPT forward pass. Takes one token and its position, plus KV caches for previous tokens. Returns logits for the next token prediction.

118.text: Look up this token's learned embedding, a dense vector capturing what the character "means" to the model.

119.text: Look up the position embedding. The model needs to know WHERE in the sequence this token sits.

120.text: Add token identity and position information. After this, the vector knows both what and where.

121.text: Normalize before entering the transformer layers. Keeps activations stable from the start.

123.text: Process through each transformer layer. With n_layer=1 this loop runs once, but the code generalizes to deeper models.

124.text: Save the input for the residual connection. It will be added back after attention, creating a "skip road" for gradients.

125.text: Pre-norm architecture: normalize before attention, not after. This is the modern convention (GPT-2 onward) and helps training stability.

126.text: Query projection: transforms the normalized input into a "question" vector. What is this token looking for?

127.text: Key projection: transforms into a "label" vector. What does this token advertise about itself?

128.text: Value projection: transforms into the actual content that will be retrieved if attention deems this token relevant.

129.text: Append this token's keys to the cache. During generation, we accumulate keys from all previous positions so we don't recompute them.

130.text: Same for values. The KV cache is what makes autoregressive generation efficient.

132.text: We'll collect each head's output here, then concatenate them all.

133.text: Process each attention head independently. Each head can specialize in different types of token relationships.

134.text: Slice start index for this head's portion of the embedding.

135.text: Extract this head's slice of the query vector. Just 4 dimensions out of 16.

136.text: Gather this head's key slices from ALL previous tokens (via the cache). This is the "memory" the current token attends to.

137.text: Same for values, the information to retrieve.

138.text: Scaled dot-product attention. Each score measures how much the query "matches" each key. Division by sqrt(head_dim) prevents softmax from becoming too peaked.

139.text: Softmax converts scores into weights that sum to 1. A probability distribution over which past tokens to attend to.

140.text: Weighted sum of value vectors. Tokens that "match" the query contribute more. This is the core of the attention mechanism.

141.text: Concatenate all heads' outputs. Multi-head attention is just independent attentions run in parallel, then merged.

143.text: Output projection: mixes the concatenated head outputs. Lets the model learn how to combine information from different heads.

144.text: Residual connection: add the attention output to the original input. Creates a direct gradient highway through the network.

146.text: Save for the MLP's residual connection. Same pattern: snapshot, transform, add back.

147.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network. Keeps activations on a consistent scale before the MLP processes them.

148.text: MLP first layer: expand to 4x dimension. This wider hidden layer gives the network more capacity for complex transformations.

149.text: ReLU activation: zero out negatives, keep positives. Without this non-linearity, stacking linear layers would collapse into one.

150.text: MLP second layer: project back down to embedding dimension.

151.text: Second residual connection. The pattern is always: normalize, transform, add back. Two residual streams per layer.

153.text: Final projection: map from the 16-dim embedding space to the 27-dim vocabulary. Each output is a score (logit) for how likely each character is next.

154.text: Return the raw logits. The training loop converts these to probabilities and computes the loss.

156.text: Adam optimizer hyperparameters. beta1=0.85 (momentum) is lower than the default 0.9, meaning less smoothing. beta2=0.99 tracks the scale of recent gradients.

157.text: First moment (mean) estimates, initialized to zero. These smooth out noisy gradients, acting like momentum.

158.text: Second moment (variance) estimates. These adapt the learning rate per-parameter, giving larger updates to rarely-changed weights.

160.text: 1000 training steps. Each step processes one name and updates all parameters.

162.text: The main training loop. Each iteration is one gradient descent step on one name.

163.text: Cycle through the dataset. After seeing all names, start over from the beginning.

164.text: Tokenize: BOS + character IDs + BOS. The model learns to start generating after BOS and stop by predicting BOS.

165.text: Clip to the maximum context length. Most names are short, so this rarely activates.

167.text: Fresh KV caches for each training example. During training, we process the whole sequence with teacher forcing.

168.text: We'll accumulate the loss at each position and average them at the end.

170.text: Process each position one at a time. The model predicts each next character given all previous ones.

171.text: Input token and the target it should predict. The model sees token_id and should output high probability for target_id.

172.text: Run the forward pass, building the entire computation graph. Every Value operation is recorded for backpropagation.

173.text: Convert logits to probabilities. The model's confidence distribution over all 27 possible next characters.

174.text: Negative log probability of the correct answer. If the model assigns probability 1.0 to the right token, loss is 0. Lower probability means higher loss.

175.text: Collect this position's loss. We'll average them all to get the final training signal.

177.text: Average loss across all positions in this name. This single number measures how wrong the model is.

178.text: One call triggers the entire backward pass. Every parameter's gradient gets computed via the chain rule we built in the Value class.

180.text: Linear warmdown: learning rate starts at 0.01 and decays to 0. Smaller steps near the end help the model settle into a good minimum.

182.text: Iterate over every single parameter in the model for the Adam update.

183.text: Update the running mean of gradients (first moment). beta1 controls how much history to keep vs. how much to trust the current gradient.

184.text: Update the running mean of squared gradients (second moment). This tracks the scale/variance of each parameter's gradients.

185.text: Bias correction for the first moment. Compensates for zero initialization, especially important in early steps.

186.text: Bias correction for the second moment.

187.text: The Adam update: step in the direction of the smoothed gradient, scaled by the inverse of the gradient's historical magnitude. Large gradients get smaller steps.

188.text: Reset gradient to zero for the next step. Without this, gradients would accumulate across training steps.

190.text: Print progress with carriage return so the line updates in place. Watch the loss decrease over training.

192.text: Temperature controls randomness: 0.5 makes the distribution sharper (more conservative). Closer to 1.0 gives more variety and creativity.

193.text: Time to see what the model learned. Everything below is pure inference, no gradients, no updates.

195.text: Generate 20 new names the model has never seen. These come purely from learned patterns.

196.text: Fresh KV caches for generation. No context from training leaks in.

197.text: Start with the BOS token, the universal "begin a name" signal.

198.text: Collect generated characters here. We'll join them into a string at the end.

200.text: Generate one character at a time, up to the max sequence length.

201.text: Feed the current token through the model to get predictions for the next one.

202.text: Apply temperature scaling before softmax. Dividing logits by temperature < 1 makes the distribution peakier and more confident.

203.text: Sample from the probability distribution. Unlike greedy decoding (always picking the most likely), sampling introduces variety.

205.text: If the model generates BOS, it's signaling "this name is complete." Time to stop.

206.text: Exit the generation loop. The name is done.

208.text: Convert the token ID back to its character. The mapping works both ways: chars to IDs for input, IDs to chars for output.

210.text: Print the generated name. These are entirely new names invented by the model, reflecting statistical patterns learned from 32K real names.

labs.title: NumPy with Manual Backpropagation
labs.tagline: Same architecture, but NumPy handles the math. Every gradient is derived by hand.
labs.description:
This lab replaces Python scalars with NumPy arrays, making the forward pass fast — but keeps every gradient derived by hand in the backward pass.

You'll see the chain rule applied explicitly to matrix multiplications, softmax, RMSNorm, and cross-entropy loss. Each backward function computes dL/dX given dL/dY from the layer above. This is exactly what PyTorch's autograd does automatically, but here you can read every line.

The key insight: the forward pass is just linear algebra, and the backward pass is just more linear algebra applied in reverse order. Once you see it done manually, automatic differentiation feels like a natural shortcut rather than magic.
learning_objectives: Derive gradients for matrix operations using the chain rule
Implement backward passes for softmax, RMSNorm, and cross-entropy
Compare scalar autograd overhead vs. vectorized NumPy operations

exercises.prompt: Comment out the RMSNorm backward pass and replace it with a numerical gradient check using finite differences. Do the gradients match?
exercises.hint: For each parameter p, compute (loss(p+eps) - loss(p-eps)) / (2*eps) with eps=1e-5 and compare to the analytical gradient.

exercises.prompt: Swap the order of the backward passes for two consecutive layers. What happens to the gradients and why?
exercises.hint: The chain rule requires processing layers in reverse order. Swapping two layers means one layer receives the wrong upstream gradient.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — NumPy edition with manual backpropagation.

Same architecture as the pure-Python version, but using NumPy arrays for
vectorized math. Every gradient is derived and coded by hand — no autograd.
This is what PyTorch computes for you behind the scenes.

The transformer architecture follows "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et
al., 2017, https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762), implemented here as a
decoder-only variant with hand-derived gradients for every operation. The
manual backpropagation applies the chain rule as described in "Learning
representations by back-propagating errors" (Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams,
1986). Normalization uses RMSNorm following "Root Mean Square Layer
Normalization" (Zhang & Sennrich, 2019, https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.07467).
"""

import math
import os
import random

import numpy as np

random.seed(42)
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Parameters — plain NumPy arrays + matching gradient arrays
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head


def param(shape, std=0.08):
    return rng.standard_normal(shape) * std


# All parameters stored as a dict of arrays
P = {
    "wte": param((vocab_size, n_embd)),
    "wpe": param((block_size, n_embd)),
    "lm_head": param((vocab_size, n_embd)),
}
for i in range(n_layer):
    P[f"l{i}.wq"] = param((n_embd, n_embd))
    P[f"l{i}.wk"] = param((n_embd, n_embd))
    P[f"l{i}.wv"] = param((n_embd, n_embd))
    P[f"l{i}.wo"] = param((n_embd, n_embd))
    P[f"l{i}.fc1"] = param((n_embd, 4 * n_embd))
    P[f"l{i}.fc2"] = param((4 * n_embd, n_embd))

# Gradient buffers (same shapes, zeroed)
G = {k: np.zeros_like(v) for k, v in P.items()}

num_params = sum(v.size for v in P.values())
print(f"num params: {num_params}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Forward + Backward (manual)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def rmsnorm_fwd(x):
    """x: (T, D) -> out: (T, D), cache"""
    ms = np.mean(x**2, axis=-1, keepdims=True)  # (T, 1)
    scale = 1.0 / np.sqrt(ms + 1e-5)  # (T, 1)
    out = x * scale
    return out, (x, scale, ms)


def rmsnorm_bwd(dout, cache):
    x, scale, _ms = cache
    D = x.shape[-1]
    dx = dout * scale - x * scale**3 * np.sum(dout * x, axis=-1, keepdims=True) / D
    return dx


def softmax_fwd(logits):
    """logits: (T, V) or (nh, T, T2) -> probs same shape"""
    shifted = logits - logits.max(axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    exps = np.exp(shifted)
    return exps / exps.sum(axis=-1, keepdims=True)


def softmax_bwd(probs, dprobs):
    """Element-wise: dp - p * sum(dp * p)"""
    s = np.sum(dprobs * probs, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    return probs * (dprobs - s)


def relu_fwd(x):
    mask = (x > 0).astype(x.dtype)
    relu_x = x * mask
    return relu_x, mask


def relu_bwd(dout, cache):
    mask = cache
    return dout * mask


def forward(token_ids):
    """
    token_ids: (n,) int array of token ids
    Returns: logits (n, vocab_size), cache dict (for backward)
    """
    n = len(token_ids)
    cache = {}

    # Embeddings
    tok_emb = P["wte"][token_ids]  # (n, D)
    pos_emb = P["wpe"][:n]  # (n, D)
    x = tok_emb + pos_emb

    # Initial RMSNorm
    x, cache["norm_in"] = rmsnorm_fwd(x)

    for li in range(n_layer):
        x_res = x.copy()

        # Pre-attention RMSNorm
        x_normed, cache[f"l{li}.norm1"] = rmsnorm_fwd(x)

        # QKV projections: (n, D) @ (D, D) -> (n, D)
        Q = x_normed @ P[f"l{li}.wq"]  # (n, D)
        K = x_normed @ P[f"l{li}.wk"]
        V = x_normed @ P[f"l{li}.wv"]

        # Reshape for multi-head: (n, nh, hd) -> (nh, n, hd)
        Q_h = Q.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)  # (nh, n, hd)
        K_h = K.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        V_h = V.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)

        # Attention scores
        att = Q_h @ K_h.transpose(0, 2, 1) / math.sqrt(head_dim)  # (nh, n, n)
        # Causal mask
        causal_mask = np.triu(np.ones((n, n)), k=1).astype(bool)
        att = np.where(causal_mask, -1e9, att)
        att_probs = softmax_fwd(att)  # (nh, n, n)

        # Weighted sum of values
        attn_out = att_probs @ V_h  # (nh, n, hd)
        # Reshape back: (n, D)
        attn_out_cat = attn_out.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd)

        # Output projection
        attn_proj = attn_out_cat @ P[f"l{li}.wo"]  # (n, D)

        # Residual
        x = attn_proj + x_res

        cache[f"l{li}.attn"] = (x_res, x_normed, Q, K, V, Q_h, K_h, V_h, att_probs, attn_out, attn_out_cat)

        # Pre-MLP RMSNorm
        x_res2 = x.copy()
        x_normed2, cache[f"l{li}.norm2"] = rmsnorm_fwd(x)

        # MLP
        hidden = x_normed2 @ P[f"l{li}.fc1"]  # (n, 4D)
        act, cache[f"l{li}.act"] = relu_fwd(hidden)
        mlp_out = act @ P[f"l{li}.fc2"]  # (n, D)

        x = mlp_out + x_res2
        cache[f"l{li}.mlp"] = (x_res2, x_normed2, hidden, act)

    # LM head
    logits = x @ P["lm_head"].T  # (n, vocab_size)
    cache["final_x"] = x
    return logits, cache


def loss_and_cache(tokens_np):
    """Forward + cross-entropy loss for training. Returns: loss, cache for backward."""
    T = len(tokens_np)
    n = min(block_size, T - 1)
    input_ids = tokens_np[:n]
    targets = tokens_np[1 : n + 1]

    logits, cache = forward(input_ids)

    # Loss: cross-entropy
    probs = softmax_fwd(logits)  # (n, vocab_size)
    log_probs = -np.log(probs[np.arange(n), targets] + 1e-10)
    loss = log_probs.mean()

    cache["final"] = (cache["final_x"], logits, probs, targets, input_ids, n)
    return loss, cache


def backward(cache):
    """Compute gradients for all parameters, accumulate into G."""
    x, logits, probs, targets, input_ids, n = cache["final"]

    # d(loss)/d(logits) via softmax + cross-entropy shortcut
    dlogits = probs.copy()  # (n, V)
    dlogits[np.arange(n), targets] -= 1.0
    dlogits /= n

    # LM head: logits = x @ lm_head.T
    G["lm_head"] += dlogits.T @ x  # (V, D)
    dx = dlogits @ P["lm_head"]  # (n, D)

    for li in reversed(range(n_layer)):
        # --- MLP backward ---
        x_res2, x_normed2, hidden, act = cache[f"l{li}.mlp"]

        dx_res2 = dx.copy()
        # mlp_out = act @ fc2
        dact = dx @ P[f"l{li}.fc2"].T  # (n, 4D)
        G[f"l{li}.fc2"] += act.T @ dx  # (4D, D)

        dhidden = relu_bwd(dact, cache[f"l{li}.act"])

        # hidden = x_normed2 @ fc1
        dx_normed2 = dhidden @ P[f"l{li}.fc1"].T  # (n, D)
        G[f"l{li}.fc1"] += x_normed2.T @ dhidden  # (D, 4D)

        # RMSNorm backward
        dx_pre_mlp = rmsnorm_bwd(dx_normed2, cache[f"l{li}.norm2"])
        dx = dx_pre_mlp + dx_res2  # residual

        # --- Attention backward ---
        x_res, x_normed, Q, K, V, Q_h, K_h, V_h, att_probs, attn_out, attn_out_cat = cache[f"l{li}.attn"]

        dx_res = dx.copy()

        # attn_proj = attn_out_cat @ wo
        dattn_out_cat = dx @ P[f"l{li}.wo"].T  # (n, D)
        G[f"l{li}.wo"] += attn_out_cat.T @ dx  # (D, D)

        # Reshape back to multi-head: (n, D) -> (nh, n, hd)
        dattn_out = dattn_out_cat.reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2)

        # attn_out = att_probs @ V_h
        datt_probs = dattn_out @ V_h.transpose(0, 2, 1)  # (nh, n, n)
        dV_h = att_probs.transpose(0, 2, 1) @ dattn_out  # (nh, n, hd)

        # Softmax backward on attention
        datt = softmax_bwd(att_probs, datt_probs)  # (nh, n, n)
        # Causal mask: zero out upper triangle grads (they were -inf)
        causal_mask = np.triu(np.ones((n, n)), k=1).astype(bool)
        datt = np.where(causal_mask, 0.0, datt)
        datt /= math.sqrt(head_dim)

        # att = Q_h @ K_h^T
        dQ_h = datt @ K_h  # (nh, n, hd)
        dK_h = datt.transpose(0, 2, 1) @ Q_h  # (nh, n, hd)

        # Reshape heads back to (n, D)
        dQ = dQ_h.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd)
        dK = dK_h.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd)
        dV = dV_h.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd)

        # QKV projections: Q = x_normed @ wq, etc.
        dx_normed = dQ @ P[f"l{li}.wq"].T + dK @ P[f"l{li}.wk"].T + dV @ P[f"l{li}.wv"].T
        G[f"l{li}.wq"] += x_normed.T @ dQ
        G[f"l{li}.wk"] += x_normed.T @ dK
        G[f"l{li}.wv"] += x_normed.T @ dV

        # RMSNorm backward
        dx_pre_attn = rmsnorm_bwd(dx_normed, cache[f"l{li}.norm1"])
        dx = dx_pre_attn + dx_res  # residual

    # Initial RMSNorm backward
    dx = rmsnorm_bwd(dx, cache["norm_in"])

    # Embedding gradients
    for i, tid in enumerate(input_ids):
        G["wte"][tid] += dx[i]
        G["wpe"][i] += dx[i]


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Adam optimizer (manual, operating on the P/G dicts)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 1e-2, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8
M = {k: np.zeros_like(v) for k, v in P.items()}  # first moment
Vb = {k: np.zeros_like(v) for k, v in P.items()}  # second moment

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training loop
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
num_steps = 1000
for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    tokens_np = np.array(tokens)

    # Zero gradients
    for k in G:
        G[k].fill(0.0)

    loss, cache = loss_and_cache(tokens_np)
    backward(cache)

    # Adam update with linear LR decay
    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for k in P:
        M[k] = beta1 * M[k] + (1 - beta1) * G[k]
        Vb[k] = beta2 * Vb[k] + (1 - beta2) * G[k] ** 2
        m_hat = M[k] / (1 - beta1 ** (step + 1))
        v_hat = Vb[k] / (1 - beta2 ** (step + 1))
        P[k] -= lr_t * m_hat / (np.sqrt(v_hat) + eps_adam)

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss:.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")


for sample_idx in range(20):
    generated = [BOS]
    sample = []
    for _ in range(block_size):
        logits, _ = forward(np.array(generated))
        logits = logits[-1] / temperature
        probs = softmax_fwd(logits.reshape(1, -1))[0]
        token_id = rng.choice(vocab_size, p=probs)
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        generated.append(token_id)
        sample.append(uchars[token_id])
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {''.join(sample)}")

17.text: This lab keeps the same algorithm as pure Python, but swaps scalar arithmetic for arrays. The goal is speed without surrendering understanding of the gradients.

18.text: Same dataset, same task. That makes the comparison with lab 01 about representation and backprop mechanics, not about a different problem.

19.text: We keep the data pipeline close to lab 01 so the reader can focus on the real shift: vectorized forward pass, hand-derived backward pass.

21.text: Here is the big transition from lab 01: NumPy compresses the forward pass into matrix operations, but we still refuse to hide the gradient story behind autograd.

23.text: Use the same reproducible setup as lab 01 so the new complexity comes from arrays and manual gradients, not from a different run.

24.text: NumPy has its own RNG because arrays bring their own initialization path. That separation is the first hint that frameworks often manage randomness differently too.

29.text: Same shared corpus as lab 01. That continuity is what lets the framework progression feel like one unfolding story instead of separate demos.

30.text: Keep the tutorial self-contained here too so the only new burden is understanding the math, not setting up the environment.

33.text: Download URL for the names dataset.

36.text: Same tokenization as lab 01, each line becomes one training document.

41.text: BOS token ID, same convention across all labs.

48.text: Same tiny architecture as lab 01, this lab is about the computation method, not the model size.

50.text: A single transformer layer keeps the model minimal. Real GPTs stack dozens, but one is enough to learn interesting name patterns and demonstrate the full algorithm.

55.text: Helper to create a random NumPy array, replaces lab 01's nested list of Value objects. One line, same effect.

56.text: Standard normal scaled by 0.08, same initialization distribution as lab 01.

60.text: Parameters stored as a flat dict of NumPy arrays. Compare to lab 01's nested lists of Value objects, same data, vastly more efficient storage.

61.text: Token embeddings: each row is one token's learned representation.

62.text: Position embeddings: each row encodes a position in the sequence.

63.text: Output projection: maps embeddings back to vocabulary scores.

65.text: Allocate per-layer attention and MLP weights.

66.text: The query weight matrix. Multiplying the input by this produces the 'what am I looking for?' vectors for attention.

67.text: The key weight matrix. It produces 'what do I contain?' vectors that queries will be matched against to determine relevance.

68.text: The value weight matrix. It produces vectors carrying the actual content that attention will retrieve and blend together.

69.text: Output projection recombines multi-head attention outputs. After each head focuses on different relationships, this matrix merges their perspectives into a unified representation.

74.text: Every parameter gets a matching zero-filled gradient array. In lab 01, gradients lived inside each Value. Here, we manage them ourselves.

76.text: Count total parameters to verify we match lab 01.

83.text: RMSNorm forward, returns both the output AND a cache of intermediate values. The backward pass needs these cached values to compute gradients.

85.text: Mean of squares across the embedding dimension. NumPy handles this across entire matrices, not one scalar at a time.

86.text: Reciprocal square root, the scaling factor. Same formula as lab 01's (ms + 1e-5) ** -0.5.

87.text: Scale the input. Broadcasting handles the (T, 1) * (T, D) multiplication automatically.

88.text: Return the output AND the cache. This is the key pattern: forward stores everything backward will need.

91.text: RMSNorm backward, given the gradient of the output, compute the gradient of the input. This is the manual derivative you'd get from the chain rule on paper.

92.text: Unpack the cached values from the forward pass.

94.text: The hand-derived RMSNorm gradient. Two terms: direct path through the scale, and indirect path through the normalization. This is what autograd computes automatically.

98.text: Softmax forward, same subtract-max-then-exponentiate trick as lab 01, but vectorized over entire matrices.

100.text: Subtract max for numerical stability, prevents overflow in exp().

102.text: Normalize to get probabilities. Each row sums to 1.

105.text: Softmax backward. The Jacobian of softmax is dense (every output depends on every input), but this compact formula handles it.

107.text: Sum of element-wise product: this captures the coupling between all softmax outputs.

108.text: Elegant formula: p * (dp - sum(dp * p)). Derived from differentiating the softmax function, doing this by hand is a rite of passage.

111.text: ReLU forward: save a binary mask of which neurons fired. The backward pass just uses this mask as a gate.

112.text: The mask is 1 where input was positive, 0 elsewhere. This IS the ReLU derivative.

113.text: Element-wise multiply: zero out negative inputs.

117.text: ReLU backward: gradients pass through where the neuron was active, get blocked where it was dead. Multiply by the mask, that's it.

119.text: Dead simple: dout * mask. If the neuron fired in the forward pass, its gradient flows; if not, it's zero.

122.text: The forward pass processes an entire sequence at once as a matrix, no more position-by-position loop like lab 01.

127.text: How many tokens we're processing. The model will see all of these at once, unlike lab 01 which processed one position at a time.

128.text: The cache dictionary stores every intermediate result the backward pass will need.

131.text: Gather all token embeddings at once with fancy indexing. In lab 01, this was a lookup in a loop.

133.text: Broadcast addition fuses token and position information, same as lab 01 but for all positions simultaneously.

136.text: Normalize and cache. Every intermediate result stored here will be consumed during backpropagation.

139.text: Snapshot the representation before attention transforms it. The residual connection will add this back, ensuring the layer can only refine, not destroy, what came before.

142.text: Normalize and cache before attention. The cache stores the normalization statistics so the backward pass can compute exact gradients without re-doing the forward math.

145.text: Query projection: (n, D) @ (D, D). One matrix multiply replaces lab 01's nested list-comprehension linear layer.

147.text: Project into value vectors, which carry the actual information that attention will retrieve and blend. Same operation as Q and K, different learned weights.

150.text: Reshape and transpose to split into attention heads. The (n, D) matrix becomes (n_head, n, head_dim), each head gets its own slice.

151.text: Keys get the same multi-head split. Each head's keys will be matched against only that head's queries, so different heads learn different notions of relevance.

152.text: Values get the same multi-head split. Each head independently retrieves content from its own subspace, then the results are concatenated.

155.text: Batch matrix multiply: all heads compute attention scores simultaneously. One operation replaces lab 01's per-head loop.

157.text: Causal mask: upper triangle of ones. Prevents tokens from attending to future positions, the core constraint of autoregressive generation.

158.text: Fill future positions with -1e9 so softmax gives them ~zero probability.

159.text: Softmax over the last dimension, each row becomes a probability distribution over which past tokens to attend to.

162.text: Weighted sum of values using attention probabilities, the actual information retrieval step.

164.text: Reverse the reshape: merge heads back into a single (n, D) matrix.

167.text: Output projection: mix the multi-head outputs.

170.text: Residual connection: add attention output to original input.

172.text: Cache EVERYTHING the attention backward pass needs. Eleven values, this is the price of manual backprop.

175.text: Snapshot the representation before the MLP transforms it. The residual connection will add this back, letting gradients flow through an unobstructed shortcut path.

176.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network. Like the pre-attention norm, this keeps activations on a consistent scale so the MLP can learn stable transformations.

179.text: MLP expansion: project to 4x wider hidden layer.

180.text: ReLU activation with its binary mask cached for the backward pass. The mask records which neurons fired, which is all the backward pass needs to route gradients.

181.text: MLP contraction: project back to embedding dimension.

183.text: Add the MLP output back to its input via the residual connection. This is the second skip connection in the block, completing the norm-transform-add pattern.

184.text: Cache all MLP intermediates for backward.

187.text: Final linear projection to vocabulary logits. Note the transpose on lm_head, weight tying convention.

188.text: Cache the final hidden states for the backward pass.

192.text: Combines forward pass with loss computation, the complete training forward step.

194.text: Total sequence length before clipping. We need this to determine how many input-target pairs we can form from this document.

195.text: Clip to the maximum context window. Most names are short enough to fit, but this guard prevents index-out-of-bounds on unusually long entries.

197.text: Target tokens (all but first), shifted by one position.

199.text: Feed the tokens through the model to get logits and the cache of intermediates. The cache is what makes manual backprop possible without recomputing the forward pass.

202.text: Convert logits to probabilities for cross-entropy loss.

203.text: Gather the log-probabilities of the correct targets. The 1e-10 prevents log(0).

204.text: Mean negative log-probability, cross-entropy loss. Lower means the model assigns higher probability to the right answers.

206.text: Pack everything into the cache for the backward pass.

210.text: The backward pass: 80+ lines of hand-derived gradients. This is what loss.backward() does automatically in PyTorch.

215.text: Softmax + cross-entropy gradient shortcut: start with the probabilities, subtract 1 from the correct class. This elegant shorthand replaces two separate backward passes.

216.text: Subtract 1 at target positions, the combined softmax-CE gradient.

217.text: Average over the sequence, matches the mean in the loss computation.

220.text: Gradient for lm_head: dlogits.T @ x. Accumulate with += because gradients add up.

221.text: Gradient flowing back into the hidden states.

223.text: Walk backward through layers, reverse order of the forward pass.

225.text: Unpack the four intermediate values the MLP forward pass cached. Each one is needed to compute the exact gradient for the corresponding weight matrix.

227.text: Save residual gradient, it bypasses the MLP and adds directly later.

229.text: Gradient through fc2: the backward of act @ fc2 w.r.t. act.

230.text: Weight gradient for fc2: act.T @ dx. This is the outer product pattern: input.T @ output_grad.

232.text: ReLU backward: zero out gradients for dead neurons.

235.text: Gradient through fc1: same pattern as fc2.

236.text: Weight gradient for fc1: the outer product of the normalized input and the hidden gradient. This tells us how to adjust fc1 to reduce the loss.

239.text: RMSNorm backward, uses the cached normalization statistics.

240.text: Add the residual gradient. The residual connection means gradients take two paths, through the MLP and around it, and they add together.

243.text: Unpack the attention cache, all eleven intermediate values.

245.text: Save the residual gradient for the attention skip connection.

249.text: Weight gradient for the output projection.

252.text: Reshape gradient back into multi-head format.

255.text: Gradient through att_probs @ V_h w.r.t. attention probabilities.

256.text: Gradient flowing back into value vectors. The attention probabilities determine how much each value contributed to the output, so transposing them gives the correct routing.

259.text: Softmax backward on attention weights, uses the formula from softmax_bwd.

261.text: Zero out gradients for masked (future) positions, they were -inf in the forward pass.

263.text: Undo the sqrt(head_dim) scaling from the forward pass.

270.text: Reshape head gradients back to (n, D).

271.text: Key gradients get the same reshape back to full embedding dimension. The per-head gradients are simply concatenated since the heads operate on non-overlapping slices.

272.text: Value gradients reshaped identically. All three QKV gradients flow back through the same reshape-transpose pipeline that split them into heads during the forward pass.

275.text: Sum gradients from all three QKV projections. The same x_normed feeds into Q, K, and V, so its gradient is the sum of all three paths.

281.text: RMSNorm backward for the pre-attention norm.

282.text: Add residual gradient, completing the attention skip connection.

285.text: Initial RMSNorm backward, the very first normalization in the forward pass.

288.text: Embedding gradients: scatter the position-level gradients back to the embedding tables. Unlike weight matrices, embeddings use row indexing, so we accumulate manually.

289.text: Each token ID's embedding row gets the gradient from that position.

290.text: Each position's embedding row gets the same gradient.

297.text: First moment buffers, matching dict structure to parameters.

298.text: Second moment buffers. Named Vb to avoid shadowing Python's v loop variable.

304.text: The main training loop. Each iteration: pick a name, forward pass, manual backward pass, Adam update. One thousand steps through this cycle.

305.text: Cycle through the dataset with modular indexing. After seeing all names, we loop back to the beginning for another pass.

307.text: Convert to NumPy array for the forward pass.

310.text: Zero all gradients before each step. In lab 01, this was p.grad = 0 per parameter; here it's per array.

311.text: Fill with zeros, faster than creating new arrays.

314.text: Backward pass, our 80+ lines of hand-coded gradients. In lab 03, this will be one line.

317.text: Linear learning rate decay, same schedule as lab 01.

318.text: Iterate over every parameter array in the model to apply the Adam update. NumPy broadcasting handles entire weight matrices at once.

319.text: First moment update, exponential moving average of gradients.

320.text: Second moment update, exponential moving average of squared gradients.

323.text: Adam parameter update. Same formula as lab 01, but operating on entire arrays at once thanks to NumPy broadcasting.

325.text: Log every 10 steps (lab 01 logged every step, the NumPy version is fast enough that would be too verbose).

335.text: Generate 20 entirely new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution. These names never appeared in the training data.

336.text: Start with BOS token in the generated sequence.

339.text: Run the forward pass on the entire generated sequence so far. Unlike lab 01's KV-cache approach, we recompute from scratch each step.

340.text: Take only the last position's logits and apply temperature scaling.

341.text: Reshape for softmax (expects 2D input) and get probabilities.

342.text: Sample from the distribution using NumPy's RNG.

343.text: When the model outputs the BOS token during generation, it is signaling that this name is complete and we should stop adding characters.

345.text: Extend the sequence with the new token.

347.text: Print the finished name. These are entirely new names invented by the model, reflecting the statistical patterns it absorbed from 32K real names.

labs.title: PyTorch
labs.tagline: PyTorch handles tensors and gradients. See how much boilerplate disappears.
labs.description:
The same transformer architecture, now in PyTorch. Autograd handles all gradients automatically — no more manual backward passes.

The model is defined as nn.Module subclasses with learnable nn.Parameter tensors. Call loss.backward() and PyTorch traces the computation graph, computes all gradients, and stores them in .grad attributes. Compare this to Lab 02 to see exactly how much boilerplate disappears.

This is the imperative, object-oriented style that most ML practitioners use daily. The next lab adds batching to bridge the gap to real training.
learning_objectives: Implement a transformer using PyTorch nn.Module and autograd
Explain how loss.backward() eliminates manual gradient code
Compare PyTorch's imperative style with manual backpropagation

exercises.prompt: Register a backward hook on one of the attention layers to print gradient norms at each training step. Do they grow, shrink, or stay stable?
exercises.hint: Use module.register_full_backward_hook(fn) where fn receives the module, grad_input, and grad_output. Print torch.norm(grad_output[0]).

exercises.prompt: Change the optimizer from the current one to plain SGD (no momentum). How many more training steps does it need to reach the same loss?
exercises.hint: Replace the optimizer with torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=...). You may need to adjust the learning rate significantly.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — PyTorch edition.

Same algorithm as microgpt.py (pure-Python scalar version), but using PyTorch
tensors for efficient computation. Every architectural choice is preserved:
character-level tokenizer, RMSNorm (not LayerNorm), ReLU (not GeLU), no
biases, linear LR decay, and Adam with beta1=0.85, beta2=0.99.

The transformer architecture follows "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et
al., 2017, https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762), though this is a decoder-only
GPT variant rather than the original encoder-decoder design. Normalization
uses RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm, following "Root Mean Square Layer
Normalization" (Zhang & Sennrich, 2019, https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.07467).
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, nh, T, hd)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        # Scaled dot-product attention with causal mask
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)  # (1, n)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)  # (1, n)

    logits = model(input_ids)  # (1, n, vocab_size)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    for sample_idx in range(20):
        tokens = [BOS]
        for _ in range(block_size):
            idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
            logits = model(idx)
            logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
            probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
        print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")

16.text: This is the first framework jump where a huge amount of manual work disappears. The architecture stays recognizable; PyTorch absorbs the gradient bookkeeping.

17.text: Same dataset, same training problem. That keeps the lesson focused on what PyTorch changes rather than on what the model is asked to learn.

18.text: The outer training loop still looks familiar. The dramatic difference is not in data loading, but in how much machinery the framework now hides for us.

20.text: PyTorch is the first place where the code starts looking like modern deep learning practice: tensors, autograd, modules, and the option of GPU acceleration all arrive together.

21.text: Instead of manually juggling parameter arrays, we now assemble the model from objects that already know how to register, move, and update their own weights.

22.text: The important contrast with the previous lab is that the math stays the same while the amount of code we have to write around it drops sharply.

24.text: Reproducibility still matters because we want the framework change to feel like a cleaner expression of the same model, not a different experiment.

25.text: PyTorch gets its own seed because the framework now owns initialization and tensor randomness, not just Python's outer loop.

30.text: Same dataset path, all labs share the data directory.

31.text: Auto-download the names dataset if needed.

34.text: Fetch the dataset from Karpathy's repo.

40.text: Extract all unique characters from the dataset and sort them to form a stable vocabulary. Each character becomes a token with a fixed integer ID.

41.text: BOS token gets the highest ID.

48.text: Same hyperparameters as labs 01-02, this lab changes the framework, not the architecture.

49.text: 4 heads, each attending to different relationships.

50.text: A single transformer layer. Real GPTs stack 12 to 96+ layers deep, but one already captures meaningful character-level patterns.

51.text: Names longer than 16 characters get truncated. Most names fit easily within this window.

52.text: Each of the 4 heads gets 4 dimensions of the 16-dimensional embedding, letting it focus on its own subspace of relationships.

55.text: RMSNorm as an nn.Module, PyTorch's standard way to define reusable layers. Compare to lab 01's bare function.

56.text: Constructor takes dimension (unused here since we have no learnable scale) and epsilon.

58.text: A tiny constant added before division to prevent numerical explosion when the input is near zero. Standard practice in every normalization layer.

60.text: The forward method is what gets called when you use the module. Same math as lab 01, but on tensors.

61.text: One line: compute RMS and scale. rsqrt is reciprocal square root, fused for speed.

64.text: Self-attention as a module. Four weight matrices, all managed by PyTorch.

67.text: nn.Linear wraps a weight matrix with proper initialization, gradient tracking, and GPU support. No bias, matching our architecture.

68.text: Key projection, same as lab 01's attn_wk, but PyTorch tracks gradients automatically.

69.text: Project the input into value vectors that carry the actual content to be aggregated by attention.

70.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back into the embedding space.

72.text: Forward pass for attention. Notice: no cache management, no manual gradient logic. Just the math.

73.text: Unpack batch, sequence length, and channel dimensions. The B dimension is new, even though we train on one example, PyTorch expects batch format.

74.text: Project to Q, reshape into heads, and transpose so heads are a batch dimension. Same reshape as lab 02 but with PyTorch tensor ops.

75.text: Project into key vectors and reshape into separate attention heads. Keys advertise what each token contains so queries can find relevant matches.

76.text: Project into value vectors and reshape into heads. Values carry the actual content that will be retrieved and blended by the attention weights.

79.text: Scaled dot-product attention, one matrix multiply computes all attention scores. Same formula as lab 01's inner loop, fully vectorized.

80.text: Causal mask: upper triangle of True values. Positions can only attend to earlier positions.

81.text: Fill future positions with -inf so softmax gives them zero weight.

82.text: Softmax over the key dimension, each query gets a probability distribution over which keys to attend to.

84.text: Weighted value sum, transpose heads back, reshape to (B, T, C). The attention mechanism in three operations.

85.text: Output projection combines information from all heads.

88.text: MLP block, the "thinking" part of each transformer layer.

91.text: Expand to 4x dimension: the wider hidden layer provides capacity for more complex transformations.

94.text: Forward: expand, activate, contract. One line replaces lab 01's three-step MLP.

95.text: ReLU + two linear layers. The entire MLP in a single expression.

98.text: A transformer block combines attention and MLP with residual connections and normalization.

101.text: Normalize the input before the attention sub-layer. Pre-norm architecture stabilizes training by keeping activations on a consistent scale.

102.text: The self-attention sub-layer that lets each position gather information from all previous positions in the sequence.

103.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network. Same stabilizing role as the pre-attention norm, applied before a different transformation.

104.text: The position-wise feed-forward network that independently transforms each token's representation after attention has mixed information across positions.

106.text: The famous two-line transformer block: normalize-attend-add, normalize-MLP-add.

107.text: Attention with residual connection. Same as lab 01's manual x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_residual)], but expressed as clean addition.

108.text: MLP with residual connection. The entire block is two residual sub-layers.

112.text: The complete GPT model. Four components: embeddings, normalization, transformer layers, and the output head.

115.text: nn.Embedding is a lookup table, same as indexing into lab 01's wte list, but with built-in gradient tracking.

116.text: Position embeddings, learned, not sinusoidal. Each position gets its own vector.

117.text: Initial RMSNorm before the transformer layers.

118.text: ModuleList registers all layers so PyTorch can find their parameters for optimization.

119.text: Output head: project from embedding space to vocabulary logits. No bias, matching our design.

120.text: Walk through every sub-module in the model and apply our custom N(0, 0.08) initialization. This ensures consistent starting conditions across all labs.

123.text: Static method, doesn't need self because initialization doesn't depend on the model instance.

124.text: Only initialize Linear and Embedding layers, skip RMSNorm (it has no weights).

127.text: The forward pass. Compare to lab 01: this is the same algorithm, but embedding lookups, matrix multiplies, normalization, and attention are all handled by PyTorch layers.

129.text: Look up each input token's learned embedding vector from the table. This converts discrete token IDs into continuous representations the model can process.

130.text: Position embedding: arange(T) generates [0, 1, ..., T-1] for position IDs.

131.text: Fuse embeddings, normalize, and enter the transformer.

132.text: Pass the representation through each transformer layer in sequence. With one layer this loops once, but the code scales to any depth.

133.text: Each layer applies attention + MLP with residual connections.

134.text: Project to vocabulary logits, the model's prediction for each position.

140.text: CPU training. For this tiny model, CPU is fine.

141.text: Instantiate and move to device. .to(device) would matter for GPU; here it's a no-op.

144.text: PyTorch's built-in Adam optimizer. One line replaces the manual Adam implementation from labs 01-02. Same hyperparameters: lr=0.01, betas=(0.85, 0.99).

147.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step to update the model weights.

148.text: Cycle through the dataset with modular indexing. After processing all names, we loop back for another pass.

150.text: Clip to the maximum context window. Most names fit easily, but this prevents overflow on unusually long entries.

152.text: Create input tensor with batch dimension. The [...] wrapping adds the batch dimension, PyTorch always expects (B, T) shape.

153.text: Target tensor: same tokens shifted by one position.

155.text: Forward pass. All gradient tracking happens automatically, PyTorch builds the computation graph behind the scenes.

156.text: cross_entropy combines softmax + negative log likelihood in one numerically stable operation. Replaces lab 01's manual softmax + log + negation.

158.text: Zero gradients from previous step. Same as lab 01's p.grad = 0, but for all parameters at once.

159.text: THE magic line. loss.backward() computes all gradients automatically. This one call replaces lab 01's entire Value.backward() engine AND lab 02's 80+ lines of hand-derived gradients.

160.text: Linear learning rate decay, same schedule as previous labs.

161.text: PyTorch groups parameters; we update the learning rate for all groups.

162.text: Write the decayed learning rate into the optimizer. Unlike labs 01-02 where we computed lr manually each step, here we just update one config value.

163.text: Apply the Adam update. The optimizer reads the gradients computed by backward() and updates all parameters.

165.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

166.text: .item() extracts the Python float from a 0-dim tensor, needed for printing.

171.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors its highest-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

173.text: Switch to eval mode, disables dropout (if any). Good practice, though our model doesn't use dropout.

174.text: torch.no_grad() disables gradient tracking during inference, saves memory and computation. We're only generating, not training.

175.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

176.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

178.text: Feed the generated sequence (up to context length) as a batch of 1.

179.text: Forward pass, no gradient tracking needed.

180.text: Take the last position's logits, apply temperature.

181.text: Convert the temperature-scaled logits into a proper probability distribution that sums to one, ready for sampling.

182.text: multinomial samples one token from the distribution. PyTorch's equivalent of lab 01's random.choices.

183.text: When the model predicts BOS, it signals the name is complete, so we stop generating.

185.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence so it becomes part of the context for predicting the next character.

186.text: Convert token IDs to characters, skipping the initial BOS.

187.text: Print the finished generated name. These are entirely novel names the model invented from patterns it learned during training.

labs.title: PyTorch Batched
labs.tagline: Mini-batch training bridges the gap between educational toy and real training.
labs.description:
Real training doesn't process one example at a time — it uses mini-batches. This lab adds padding, attention masking, and batch-level loss computation to the PyTorch model.

Names have different lengths, so shorter ones are padded with a special token. The attention mask prevents the model from attending to padding positions. Loss is computed only on real tokens, not padding. These are the same techniques used in production training of billion-parameter models.

The batch size of 32 means 32× more work per gradient step, but the gradient is also 32× less noisy. This is the fundamental tradeoff of mini-batch SGD.
learning_objectives: Implement padding and attention masking for variable-length sequences
Explain the tradeoff between batch size and gradient noise
Design a mini-batch training loop with proper loss masking

exercises.prompt: Change the batch size from 32 to 1 and then to 128. Plot the loss curves for all three. Which converges fastest in wall-clock time?
exercises.hint: Larger batches give smoother gradients but fewer updates per second. Track both loss and elapsed time to compare fairly.

exercises.prompt: Remove the attention mask so the model can attend to padding tokens. How does the generated output change after training?
exercises.hint: Look for where the mask is applied in the attention computation. Set it to None or all-ones and observe the quality of generated names.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — PyTorch batched edition.

Same architecture as 03_pytorch, but with proper mini-batch training:
  - Batches of 32 sequences per step
  - Padding + attention masking
  - Scaled-up model (2 layers, 64-dim embeddings, context 16)
  - 1000 training steps

This is what "engineering for efficiency" looks like on top of the same algorithm.

The batched version adds padding, masking, and mini-batch SGD — standard
engineering for production training.

Reference: "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017),
https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
PAD = vocab_size  # padding token (not in vocab, ignored in loss)
vocab_size_with_pad = vocab_size + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size} (+1 pad = {vocab_size_with_pad})")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Hyperparameters (scaled up from the single-sample version)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 64  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 2  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head
batch_size = 32
num_steps = 1000


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (same architecture, just bigger)
