160.text: Convert the name into a sequence of integer token IDs with BOS markers at both ends.

161.text: Clip to the maximum context window. Most names fit easily, but this guard prevents overflow on unusually long entries.

163.text: Convert the Python token list to an MLX array so it can flow through the computation graph for forward and backward passes.

164.text: Wrap the target tokens as an MLX array. These are the correct next characters the model should learn to predict.

166.text: Compute loss and gradients in one call. Clean and efficient.

169.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

170.text: Update the optimizer's learning rate. Must wrap in mx.array, MLX is strict about types.

171.text: Push the computed gradients into the model's parameters via the optimizer. MLX's stateful optimizer applies the Adam update rule internally.

172.text: Force evaluation. MLX uses lazy evaluation, operations build a computation graph but don't execute until you call mx.eval. This lets MLX fuse and optimize operations. Without this call, nothing would actually compute.

174.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

175.text: .item() extracts a Python scalar from an MLX array.

180.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors high-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

182.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

183.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

184.text: Generate one character at a time, up to the maximum sequence length. Each step feeds all generated tokens through the full model.

186.text: Run the full model on the current token sequence to predict the next character.

187.text: Grab the logits for the last generated position and divide by temperature. Lower temperature sharpens the distribution toward the top predictions.

188.text: Sample from categorical distribution. MLX's RNG doesn't require explicit key management like JAX.

189.text: When the model outputs the BOS token during generation, it signals the name is complete and we stop adding characters.

191.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence for the next forward pass.

192.text: Map each token ID back to its character, skipping the leading BOS, to produce the generated name as a readable string.

193.text: Print the finished name. These names were invented by the model from statistical patterns it learned during training.

labs.title: MLX Batched
labs.tagline: Mini-batch training on Apple Silicon, same padding and masking as PyTorch batched.
labs.description:
The batched version of the MLX model, with the same padding, masking, and mini-batch training as PyTorch batched (Lab 04).

MLX's lazy evaluation shines here: the framework can see the entire batch computation graph before executing, enabling better memory planning and operation fusion on the Apple GPU.
learning_objectives: Implement batched training with MLX on Apple GPU
Explain how lazy evaluation enables operation fusion
Compare MLX batching with PyTorch and JAX approaches

exercises.prompt: Compare the wall-clock training time of MLX batched vs. PyTorch batched (Lab 04) on your Mac. Which framework is faster and by how much?
exercises.hint: Run both labs for the same number of steps and measure total time. On Apple Silicon, MLX may have an advantage due to unified memory eliminating CPU-GPU transfers.

exercises.prompt: Change the padding token from its current value to a different unused character. Does the model still train correctly?
exercises.hint: The padding token value itself does not matter as long as the attention mask correctly ignores padded positions. Check that the mask logic uses the same token.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — MLX batched edition.

Same architecture as 07_mlx, but with mini-batch training:
  - Batches of 32 sequences per step
  - Padding + attention masking (same approach as PyTorch batched)
  - Scaled-up model (2 layers, 64-dim embeddings, context 16)
  - 1000 training steps

Unlike JAX's vmap, MLX batching works the PyTorch way: reshape your tensors
to include a batch dimension and write the forward pass to handle (B, T, ...).
Same idea, same manual padding — unified memory is the difference.

The batched version adds padding, masking, and mini-batch SGD — standard
engineering for production training.

Reference: "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017),
https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
"""

import math
import os
import random

import mlx.core as mx
import mlx.nn as nn
import mlx.optimizers as optim
import mlx.utils

random.seed(42)
mx.random.seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
PAD = vocab_size
vocab_size_with_pad = vocab_size + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size} (+1 pad = {vocab_size_with_pad})")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Hyperparameters (scaled up)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 64  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 2  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head
batch_size = 32
num_steps = 1000


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = 1e-5

    def __call__(self, x):
        ms = mx.mean(x * x, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
        return x * mx.rsqrt(ms + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def __call__(self, x, pad_mask=None):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).reshape(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(0, 2, 1, 3)
        k = self.wk(x).reshape(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(0, 2, 1, 3)
        v = self.wv(x).reshape(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(0, 2, 1, 3)

        att = (q @ k.transpose(0, 1, 3, 2)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        # Causal mask
        causal = mx.triu(mx.ones((T, T)), k=1).astype(mx.bool_)
        att = mx.where(causal, mx.array(-1e9), att)
        # Padding mask: don't attend to PAD positions
        if pad_mask is not None:
            att = mx.where(pad_mask[:, None, None, :], mx.array(-1e9), att)
        att = mx.softmax(att, axis=-1)
        att = mx.where(mx.isnan(att), mx.array(0.0), att)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(0, 2, 1, 3).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def __call__(self, x):
        return self.fc2(mx.maximum(self.fc1(x), 0))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def __call__(self, x, pad_mask=None):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x), pad_mask)
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size_with_pad, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = [Block() for _ in range(n_layer)]
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)

    def __call__(self, idx, pad_mask=None):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(mx.arange(T))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x, pad_mask)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Batching utility
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def make_batch(docs, step, batch_size):
    """Create a padded batch of token sequences."""
    batch_docs = [docs[(step * batch_size + i) % len(docs)] for i in range(batch_size)]
    sequences = []
    for doc in batch_docs:
        toks = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        toks = toks[: block_size + 1]
        sequences.append(toks)

    max_len = max(len(s) for s in sequences)
    input_ids, target_ids, pad_masks, target_masks = [], [], [], []
    for s in sequences:
        n = len(s) - 1
        inp = s[:n] + [PAD] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        tgt = s[1 : n + 1] + [0] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        pmask = [False] * n + [True] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        tmask = [1.0] * n + [0.0] * (max_len - 1 - n)
        input_ids.append(inp)
        target_ids.append(tgt)
        pad_masks.append(pmask)
        target_masks.append(tmask)

    return (
        mx.array(input_ids),
        mx.array(target_ids),
        mx.array(pad_masks),
        mx.array(target_masks),
    )


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
model = MicroGPT()
# Match original init: N(0, 0.08) for all weights
weights = mlx.utils.tree_flatten(model.parameters())
model.load_weights([(k, mx.random.normal(v.shape) * 0.08) for k, v in weights])
# Re-zero the padding embedding after init
model.wte.weight[PAD] = mx.zeros((n_embd,))
mx.eval(model.parameters())
num_params = sum(p.size for _, p in mlx.utils.tree_flatten(model.parameters()))
print(f"num params: {num_params}")

learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 1e-2, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8


def loss_fn(model, input_ids, targets, pad_mask, target_mask):
    logits = model(input_ids, pad_mask)  # (B, T, V)
    log_probs = logits - mx.logsumexp(logits, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    B, T, V = log_probs.shape
    # Gather log-probs for target tokens
    target_log_probs = log_probs[mx.arange(B)[:, None], mx.arange(T)[None, :], targets]
    target_log_probs = target_log_probs * target_mask
    loss = -mx.sum(target_log_probs) / mx.sum(target_mask)
    return loss


loss_and_grad = nn.value_and_grad(model, loss_fn)
optimizer = optim.Adam(learning_rate=learning_rate, betas=[beta1, beta2], eps=eps_adam)

for step in range(num_steps):
    input_ids, targets, pad_mask, target_mask = make_batch(docs, step, batch_size)

    loss_val, grads = loss_and_grad(model, input_ids, targets, pad_mask, target_mask)

    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)
    optimizer.learning_rate = mx.array(lr_t)
    optimizer.update(model, grads)
    mx.eval(model.parameters(), optimizer.state)

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss_val.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")
for sample_idx in range(20):
    tokens = [BOS]
    for _ in range(block_size):
        input_ids = mx.array([tokens[-block_size:]])  # (1, T)
        logits = model(input_ids)
        logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
        token_id = mx.random.categorical(logits).item()
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        tokens.append(token_id)
    name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")

21.text: Same dataset again. The point is to see what batched training feels like once MLX's lazy, unified-memory model enters the picture.

22.text: The outer randomness stays framework-independent so the comparison with the other batched labs remains meaningful.

23.text: This lab combines two ideas at once: the realistic mini-batch training structure from the PyTorch track and the Apple-Silicon execution model from the MLX track.

25.text: MLX's core runtime is still the differentiator here: batched training now runs through a system designed around Apple hardware and deferred execution.

26.text: The layer API is familiar on purpose. That lets the reader focus on how batching interacts with MLX rather than relearning the model definition itself.

27.text: The optimizer is where MLX's runtime story becomes concrete: training steps are shaped by lazy execution and a memory model tuned for Apple Silicon.

28.text: Even in a higher-level MLX model, parameter trees still matter. These helpers make the runtime feel manageable without hiding the structure completely.

30.text: Lock the run down so the interesting differences come from batching and MLX execution, not from shuffled luck.

31.text: Seed MLX's random number generator separately for reproducible weight initialization.

36.text: Path to the shared dataset file used consistently across all labs.

37.text: Download the names dataset from the internet if it is not already present locally.

40.text: URL pointing to the raw names dataset hosted in Karpathy's repository.

43.text: Read the text file and split it into individual name strings, one per line.

44.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

47.text: Gather every unique character from all names to build our token vocabulary. Sorting ensures a deterministic, reproducible mapping from characters to integer IDs.

48.text: The beginning-of-sequence token doubles as the end-of-sequence marker for name boundaries.

49.text: The real vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS/EOS marker.

51.text: The total embedding table size includes the PAD token. The model needs an embedding for PAD even though we mask it during attention and loss.

57.text: 4x larger than lab 07, same scale-up as PyTorch batched (lab 04).

58.text: Four attention heads, each working on 16 dimensions. Multiple heads let the model attend to different relationship types simultaneously.

59.text: Two transformer layers for deeper representations, matching the batched PyTorch lab.

60.text: Each name can be at most 16 characters long, setting the attention window size.

61.text: Each attention head operates on 16 dimensions, giving finer-grained representations.

62.text: Process 32 names at once in each training step. Batching amortizes the overhead of each forward/backward pass and provides more stable gradient estimates.

63.text: Train for 1000 steps, same as all other labs but each step processes a full batch.

72.text: A tiny constant that prevents division by zero during normalization.

74.text: Run the input through RMSNorm to stabilize activations before the transformer.

75.text: Compute the mean of squared values along the last axis for RMS normalization.

76.text: Divide each element by the root-mean-square of its vector. This rescales activations to a consistent magnitude without shifting them by their mean, which is simpler than LayerNorm.

79.text: Attention module, now handles (B, T, C) shapes and padding masks, like lab 04.

82.text: Project the input into query vectors that will attend to other positions.

83.text: Project the input into key vectors that other positions will compare against.

84.text: Project the input into value vectors carrying content to aggregate by attention.

85.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back into the embedding space.

89.text: Q reshape: (B, T, D) -> (B, T, nh, hd) -> (B, nh, T, hd). Note the 4D transpose, batch dimension is preserved, unlike lab 07's 3D version.

90.text: Reshape key projections into separate attention heads for multi-head processing.

91.text: Reshape value projections into separate attention heads for parallel computation.

93.text: Compute scaled dot-product attention scores between all query-key pairs.

95.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask that blocks attention to future positions.

96.text: Set future positions to a large negative value so softmax gives them zero weight.

98.text: Padding mask handling, identical logic to lab 04's PyTorch version.

99.text: Expand pad_mask to (B, 1, 1, T) and fill PAD positions with -inf.

100.text: Turn raw attention scores into a probability distribution so weights sum to one.

101.text: Replace NaN with 0 for fully-masked rows. MLX doesn't have nan_to_num, so we use where(isnan, 0, att).

103.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors, then transpose heads back to the time dimension and flatten into the original embedding shape for the output projection.

104.text: Project the combined multi-head output through the final linear layer.

107.text: A two-layer feed-forward network identical to lab 07. Attention mixes information between positions; the MLP then processes each position independently to add nonlinear capacity.

110.text: Widen the representation to 4x the embedding dimension for richer nonlinear transformations.

111.text: Compress the expanded hidden state back to the original embedding dimension.

113.text: Run the input through the MLP combining expansion, ReLU activation, and contraction.

114.text: MLX's equivalent of ReLU: mx.maximum(x, 0) zeroes out negative values, giving the network a simple nonlinearity between the two linear layers.

117.text: Transformer block with padding mask.

120.text: Normalize the input before passing it to the attention sub-layer for stable training.

121.text: The self-attention sub-layer that mixes information across positions in the sequence.

122.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network, following the pre-norm transformer pattern.

123.text: The feed-forward network that processes each position independently after attention mixing.

125.text: Forward passes mask through to attention.

126.text: Attention with residual and padding mask.

127.text: Apply the MLP with a residual connection that preserves the original signal.

131.text: The full transformer model, now handling batched input with padding masks. Same architecture as lab 07 but with an extra batch dimension throughout.

134.text: Embedding table with PAD token. Like lab 04, the embedding table has vocab_size_with_pad rows.

135.text: A learned vector for each position in the sequence. Added to token embeddings so the model knows where each token sits, since attention alone is position-agnostic.

136.text: Apply RMSNorm to stabilize embeddings before they enter the transformer layers.

137.text: Register all transformer layers so MLX can discover and manage their parameters.

138.text: Output head maps to real vocab (no PAD).

140.text: Forward pass with optional padding mask.

141.text: Unpack batch size and sequence length from the input shape. These dimensions drive how we construct position embeddings and propagate through the layers.

142.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token in the batch.

143.text: Position embeddings broadcast across the batch.

144.text: Combine token and position embeddings and normalize before entering the transformer.

145.text: Feed the normalized embeddings through each transformer block in sequence. Each layer refines the representations through attention and MLP sublayers.

146.text: The padding mask tells each layer's attention which positions are filler so those positions get zero weight and do not corrupt the real token representations.

147.text: Project the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for next-token prediction.

153.text: Batch creation, same strategy as labs 04 and 06.

155.text: Grab the next batch of names from the corpus, wrapping around if we reach the end. This cycling ensures we keep training even if the dataset is smaller than the total steps.

157.text: Convert each name into a sequence of integer token IDs. Each character maps to its position in the sorted vocabulary, with BOS tokens bookending the name.

158.text: Wrap each name with beginning-of-sequence tokens to mark the start and end boundaries.

159.text: Clip any name longer than the model's maximum context window. Names beyond this length would exceed the positional embedding table and cause errors.

162.text: Pad to longest sequence in batch.

163.text: Allocate four parallel arrays (input, target, pad mask, target mask) all padded to the same length so they can be stacked into rectangular tensors.

165.text: The number of real tokens in this name, before any padding was added. This tells us where the actual content ends and the padding begins.

168.text: Boolean mask marking which positions are padding so attention can ignore them.

176.text: Convert the target token IDs into an MLX array for loss computation.

177.text: Boolean mask marking which positions are padding so attention can skip them.

178.text: Float mask that weights the loss so padding positions contribute zero to the gradient.

186.text: Create the model with all its layers and projections. MLX initializes parameters immediately in unified memory shared between CPU and GPU.

192.text: N(0, 0.08) initialization, match all other labs.

193.text: Sum up all parameter sizes to report the total number of trainable weights.

196.text: Adam optimizer settings: a learning rate of 1e-2 with conservative momentum (beta1=0.85, beta2=0.99). These match the other labs for a fair comparison.

199.text: Loss function. Takes the model as first arg for MLX's autograd.

200.text: Batched forward pass with padding mask.

201.text: Subtract the log-sum-exp to get log-probabilities. This is numerically more stable than taking softmax and then log, which can lose precision on small probabilities.

202.text: Unpack the batch, sequence length, and vocabulary dimensions from the log-prob tensor.

204.text: Gather target log-probs via fancy indexing. Same pattern as lab 06.

205.text: Multiply by the target mask so padding positions contribute zero to the loss. Without this, the model would waste capacity trying to predict padding tokens.

206.text: Normalize by real token count, same as lab 06.

210.text: Value-and-grad: compute loss and gradients simultaneously.

211.text: MLX's built-in Adam optimizer manages momentum and adaptive learning rates for every parameter. It works with MLX's lazy evaluation, updating only when evaluated.

213.text: Iterate over training steps, processing a batch of 32 names per step.

214.text: Assemble a padded batch of 32 names for this training step. Each sequence is tokenized, truncated, and padded so all sequences share the same length.

216.text: Run the forward pass and backpropagation together. MLX's value_and_grad returns both the scalar loss and the gradient tree in one efficient pass.

218.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

219.text: Write the decayed learning rate into the optimizer so the next weight update uses a slightly smaller step, gradually stabilizing training as we near convergence.

220.text: Push the computed gradients into the optimizer to update all model weights.

221.text: Force lazy evaluation. Without this, MLX builds up a huge unevaluated computation graph that eventually exhausts memory.

223.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

229.text: Temperature controls how peaked or flat the sampling distribution is. At 0.5, the model becomes more confident, favoring high-probability characters over rare ones.

231.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

232.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

233.text: Autoregressive generation loop: predict one character at a time, append it to the sequence, and feed the extended sequence back into the model for the next prediction.

234.text: Feed as (1, T) batch, the batched model always expects a batch dimension.

235.text: Run the full batched model on the current token sequence to predict the next character.

236.text: Grab the logits for the very last position (the model's prediction for the next token) and divide by temperature to sharpen or soften the probability distribution.

237.text: Randomly pick one token from the probability distribution. This stochastic sampling produces diverse outputs instead of always choosing the most likely character.

238.text: When the model predicts BOS, it signals the name is complete, so we stop generating.

240.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence for the next forward pass.

241.text: Map each integer token ID back to its corresponding character and join them into a readable name string, skipping the initial BOS token.

242.text: Display the generated name so we can visually assess the model's output quality and check whether it learned plausible name patterns.

labs.title: BPE Tokenizer
labs.tagline: Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch in pure Python, with zero dependencies.
labs.description:
Byte-Pair Encoding is the tokenization algorithm behind GPT, LLaMA, and most modern LLMs. This lab implements it from scratch in pure Python.

BPE starts with individual characters and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a new token. After enough merges, common words become single tokens while rare words are split into subword pieces. This balances vocabulary size against sequence length.

The lab trains a BPE tokenizer on the names dataset and uses it with a simple bigram model. Understanding tokenization is essential — it's the first thing that happens to every input and the last thing that happens to every output.
learning_objectives: Implement Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch without dependencies
Explain how BPE balances vocabulary size against sequence length
Analyze tokenization choices and their impact on model performance

exercises.prompt: Train the BPE tokenizer with 50 merges vs. 200 merges. How does the average number of tokens per name change?
exercises.hint: After training, tokenize all names and compute the mean token count. More merges means longer tokens and shorter sequences, but a larger vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Modify the merge selection to pick the second-most-frequent pair instead of the most frequent. How does the resulting vocabulary differ?
exercises.hint: In the merge loop, sort pairs by frequency and select index 1 instead of 0. Compare the final vocabulary and token lengths.

labs.code:
"""
Understanding LLMs by Building One — BPE Tokenizer

Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch, then a side-by-side comparison of
character-level vs BPE tokenization on the names dataset. The BPE algorithm
was originally introduced for data compression in "A New Algorithm for Data
Compression" (Gage, 1994) and adapted for NLP in "Neural Machine Translation
of Rare Words with Subword Units" (Sennrich, Haddow & Birch, 2016),
https://arxiv.org/abs/1508.07909. GPT-2 uses byte-level BPE as described in
"Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners" (Radford et al., 2019).

This lab implements character-level BPE for simplicity — production tokenizers
like GPT's operate on bytes, not characters, and use a much larger vocabulary
(50K-100K merges). Zero dependencies. Pure Python. Just counting and merging.
"""

import math
import os
import random

random.seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Character-level tokenizer (baseline — same as lab 01)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Every unique character gets an integer id. BOS marks start/end of sequence.
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
char_to_id = {ch: i for i, ch in enumerate(uchars)}
id_to_char = {i: ch for ch, i in char_to_id.items()}
CHAR_BOS = len(uchars)
char_vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1  # +1 for BOS

print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("CHARACTER-LEVEL TOKENIZER (baseline)")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
print(f"vocab size: {char_vocab_size} ({len(uchars)} chars + BOS)")


def char_encode(name):
    """Encode a name as a list of character token ids."""
    return [CHAR_BOS] + [char_to_id[ch] for ch in name] + [CHAR_BOS]


def char_decode(ids):
    """Decode token ids back to a string (strip BOS)."""
    return "".join(id_to_char[i] for i in ids if i != CHAR_BOS)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# BPE tokenizer — trained from scratch
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# BPE starts with the character vocabulary and iteratively merges the most
# frequent adjacent pair into a new token. This is EXACTLY how GPT-2/3/4,
# LLaMA, etc. build their tokenizers (at much larger scale with ~50K merges).
#
# The tradeoff: larger vocab = shorter sequences = faster training/inference,
# but more parameters in the embedding table. At our tiny scale (names),
# character-level works great. At GPT scale (internet text), BPE with
# 50K-100K tokens is essential — without it, sequences would be absurdly long.
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("BPE TOKENIZER (training from scratch)")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")

NUM_MERGES = 200  # number of merge operations (new tokens to learn)

# Step 1: Start with character-level tokens for each name.
# We work with lists of integers. Initially each integer = one character.
# We reserve ids 0..25 for a-z and 26 for BOS, same as the char tokenizer.
BPE_BOS = len(uchars)
base_vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1  # characters + BOS

# Build the initial corpus: each name becomes a list of char token ids.
# We wrap with BOS just like the char tokenizer.
corpus = []
for name in docs:
    tokens = [BPE_BOS] + [char_to_id[ch] for ch in name] + [BPE_BOS]
    corpus.append(tokens)


def count_pairs(corpus):
    """Count frequency of each adjacent token pair across all sequences."""
    counts = {}
    for seq in corpus:
        for i in range(len(seq) - 1):
            pair = (seq[i], seq[i + 1])
            counts[pair] = counts.get(pair, 0) + 1
    return counts


def merge_pair(corpus, pair, new_id):
    """Replace every occurrence of `pair` with `new_id` in all sequences."""
    new_corpus = []
    for seq in corpus:
        new_seq = []
        i = 0
        while i < len(seq):
            if i < len(seq) - 1 and seq[i] == pair[0] and seq[i + 1] == pair[1]:
                new_seq.append(new_id)
                i += 2
            else:
                new_seq.append(seq[i])
                i += 1
        new_corpus.append(new_seq)
    return new_corpus


# Step 2: Iteratively merge the most frequent pair.
# Each merge creates a new token and shrinks all sequences by 1 wherever
# that pair appeared. The merge table records what was merged.
merges = {}  # (pair) -> new_token_id
vocab_size = base_vocab_size


def bpe_decode_token(tid):
    """Decode a single BPE token id to its string representation."""
    if tid == BPE_BOS:
        return ""
    if tid < len(uchars):
        return id_to_char[tid]
    # This is a merged token — find its components
    for (a, b), merged_id in merges.items():
        if merged_id == tid:
            return bpe_decode_token(a) + bpe_decode_token(b)
    return "?"


def bpe_display_token(tid):
    """Decode a BPE token for display, keeping BOS visible as '|'."""
    if tid == BPE_BOS:
        return "|"
    if tid < len(uchars):
        return id_to_char[tid]
    for (a, b), merged_id in merges.items():
        if merged_id == tid:
            return bpe_display_token(a) + bpe_display_token(b)
    return "?"


def token_str(tid):
    """Human-readable token string for display."""
    if tid == BPE_BOS:
        return "<BOS>"
    if tid < len(uchars):
        return id_to_char[tid]
    return bpe_decode_token(tid)


print(f"\nStarting vocab: {vocab_size} tokens ({len(uchars)} chars + BOS)")
print(f"Training {NUM_MERGES} merges...\n")
print(f"{'Merge':>5}  {'Pair':>20}  {'Visual':>12}  {'Freq':>6}  {'Vocab':>5}")
print("-" * 60)

for i in range(NUM_MERGES):
    counts = count_pairs(corpus)
    if not counts:
        print(f"No more pairs to merge after {i} merges.")
        break
    # Find the most frequent pair
    best_pair = max(counts, key=counts.get)
    best_count = counts[best_pair]
    new_id = vocab_size
    merges[best_pair] = new_id

    if i < 20 or i % 10 == 0 or i == NUM_MERGES - 1:
        visual = f"'{token_str(best_pair[0])}'+'{token_str(best_pair[1])}'"
        print(f"{i + 1:>5}  {best_pair!s:>20}  {visual:>12}  {best_count:>6}  {vocab_size + 1:>5}")

    corpus = merge_pair(corpus, best_pair, new_id)
    vocab_size += 1

print(f"\nFinal BPE vocab size: {vocab_size} ({base_vocab_size} base + {NUM_MERGES} merges)")


def bpe_decode(ids):
    """Decode a list of BPE token ids back to a string."""
    return "".join(bpe_decode_token(tid) for tid in ids)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# BPE encode: string -> token ids
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def bpe_encode(name):
    """Encode a name using the learned BPE merges."""
    # Start with character-level tokens
    tokens = [BPE_BOS] + [char_to_id[ch] for ch in name] + [BPE_BOS]
    # Apply merges in the order they were learned (greedy, left-to-right)
    for pair, new_id in merges.items():
        tokens = merge_pair([tokens], pair, new_id)[0]
    return tokens


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Show encoding examples: character-level vs BPE
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("ENCODING EXAMPLES: Character vs BPE")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
example_names = ["emma", "olivia", "charlotte", "alexander", "ann", "lee"]
# Also pick some random names from the dataset
example_names += random.sample(docs, 4)
# Deduplicate while preserving order
example_names = list(dict.fromkeys(example_names))[:10]

print("\nToken counts include BOS start/end markers (shown as '|' in BPE encoding).\n")
print(f"{'Name':<14} {'Char tokens':>11} {'BPE tokens':>10}  {'Compression':>11}  BPE encoding")
print("-" * 75)
for name in example_names:
    char_toks = char_encode(name)
    bpe_toks = bpe_encode(name)
    ratio = len(char_toks) / len(bpe_toks) if bpe_toks else 0
    bpe_visual = [f"'{bpe_display_token(t)}'" for t in bpe_toks]
    print(f"{name:<14} {len(char_toks):>11} {len(bpe_toks):>10}  {ratio:>10.2f}x  {' '.join(bpe_visual)}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Corpus-wide compression statistics
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("COMPRESSION STATISTICS (full corpus)")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")

total_char_tokens = sum(len(char_encode(name)) for name in docs)
total_bpe_tokens = sum(len(bpe_encode(name)) for name in docs)
avg_char = total_char_tokens / len(docs)
avg_bpe = total_bpe_tokens / len(docs)

print(f"Total names:          {len(docs):>10}")
print(f"Char vocab size:      {char_vocab_size:>10}")
print(f"BPE vocab size:       {vocab_size:>10}")
print(f"Total char tokens:    {total_char_tokens:>10}")
print(f"Total BPE tokens:     {total_bpe_tokens:>10}")
print(f"Avg tokens/name char: {avg_char:>10.2f}")
print(f"Avg tokens/name BPE:  {avg_bpe:>10.2f}")
print(f"Overall compression:  {total_char_tokens / total_bpe_tokens:>10.2f}x")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# BPE vocabulary: show what each merged token represents
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("LEARNED BPE VOCABULARY (merged tokens)")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
print(f"\nBase tokens (0-{len(uchars) - 1}): {' '.join(uchars)}")
print(f"BOS token: {BPE_BOS}")
print("\nMerged tokens:")
print(f"{'ID':>5}  {'Represents':>12}  {'Components'}")
print("-" * 40)
for (a, b), new_id in merges.items():
    merged_str = bpe_display_token(new_id)
    comp_a = bpe_display_token(a).replace("|", "<BOS>")
    comp_b = bpe_display_token(b).replace("|", "<BOS>")
    print(f"{new_id:>5}  {merged_str!r:>12}  '{comp_a}' + '{comp_b}'")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Bigram language model — trained with BOTH tokenizations
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# A bigram model is the simplest language model: P(next_token | current_token).
# It's just a lookup table of transition counts, normalized to probabilities.
# No neural network, no autograd — pure counting.
#
# We train one bigram model on character tokens and one on BPE tokens to show
# how tokenization affects sequence modeling. With BPE, each "step" covers
# more text, so the bigram captures longer-range patterns.
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("BIGRAM LANGUAGE MODEL COMPARISON")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")


def train_bigram(encoded_corpus, v_size):
    """Train a bigram model: count transitions, add-1 smooth, normalize."""
    # counts[a][b] = number of times token b follows token a
    counts = [[0] * v_size for _ in range(v_size)]
    for seq in encoded_corpus:
        for i in range(len(seq) - 1):
            counts[seq[i]][seq[i + 1]] += 1
    # Normalize to probabilities with add-1 (Laplace) smoothing
    probs = []
    for row in counts:
        total = sum(row) + v_size  # add-1 smoothing
        probs.append([(c + 1) / total for c in row])
    return probs


def sample_bigram(probs, bos_token, max_len=20):
    """Sample a sequence from the bigram model."""
    tokens = [bos_token]
    for _ in range(max_len):
        # Sample next token from probability distribution
        p = probs[tokens[-1]]
        r = random.random()
        cumsum = 0
        for tid, prob in enumerate(p):
            cumsum += prob
            if r < cumsum:
                tokens.append(tid)
                break
        if tokens[-1] == bos_token and len(tokens) > 1:
            break
    return tokens


def bigram_log_likelihood(probs, encoded_corpus):
    """Compute average log-likelihood per token (higher = better)."""
    total_ll = 0.0
    total_tokens = 0
    for seq in encoded_corpus:
        for i in range(len(seq) - 1):
            p = probs[seq[i]][seq[i + 1]]
            if p > 0:
                total_ll += math.log(p)
            total_tokens += 1
    return total_ll / total_tokens if total_tokens > 0 else 0.0


# Train character-level bigram
print("\nTraining character-level bigram...")
char_corpus = [char_encode(name) for name in docs]
char_bigram = train_bigram(char_corpus, char_vocab_size)
char_ll = bigram_log_likelihood(char_bigram, char_corpus)
print(f"  Vocab size: {char_vocab_size}")
print(f"  Avg log-likelihood per token: {char_ll:.4f}")
print(f"  Perplexity per token: {math.exp(-char_ll):.2f}")

# Train BPE bigram
print("\nTraining BPE bigram...")
bpe_corpus = [bpe_encode(name) for name in docs]
bpe_bigram = train_bigram(bpe_corpus, vocab_size)
bpe_ll = bigram_log_likelihood(bpe_bigram, bpe_corpus)
print(f"  Vocab size: {vocab_size}")
print(f"  Avg log-likelihood per token: {bpe_ll:.4f}")
print(f"  Perplexity per token: {math.exp(-bpe_ll):.2f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Generate names from both models
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("GENERATED NAMES")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")

print("\nCharacter-level bigram (27 tokens, ~6 tokens/name):")
for i in range(10):
    toks = sample_bigram(char_bigram, CHAR_BOS)
    name = char_decode(toks)
    print(f"  sample {i + 1:>2}: {name}")

print(f"\nBPE bigram ({vocab_size} tokens, ~{avg_bpe:.1f} tokens/name):")
for i in range(10):
    toks = sample_bigram(bpe_bigram, BPE_BOS)
    name = bpe_decode(toks)
    print(f"  sample {i + 1:>2}: {name}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Key takeaway
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("KEY TAKEAWAY")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
print(f"""
BPE compresses the corpus from {total_char_tokens} to {total_bpe_tokens} tokens ({total_char_tokens / total_bpe_tokens:.1f}x shorter)
by learning {NUM_MERGES} merge rules that capture common character sequences.
Vocab grows from {char_vocab_size} to {vocab_size} tokens (more embedding params, but shorter sequences).

This is the algorithm behind GPT-2, LLaMA, and most modern LLM tokenizers.
At GPT scale, BPE with 50K-100K merges is essential to keep sequences tractable.
""")

labs.title: Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE)
labs.tagline: Position encoded by rotating Q and K vectors in complex space.
labs.description:
RoPE encodes position information by rotating query and key vectors in complex space. Instead of adding a position embedding to the input, RoPE multiplies Q and K by rotation matrices whose angles depend on position.

The key property: the dot product between rotated Q and K depends only on their relative position, not absolute. This means the model naturally generalizes to longer sequences than it was trained on. RoPE is used by LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, and most modern open-weight models.

The implementation uses the real-valued form (pairs of dimensions rotated together) rather than complex numbers, matching production implementations.
learning_objectives: Implement Rotary Position Embeddings using real-valued rotation
Explain why relative position encoding enables length generalization
Compare RoPE with absolute position embeddings

exercises.prompt: Change the RoPE base frequency from 10000 to 1000 and to 100000. How does this affect the model's ability to handle different sequence lengths?
exercises.hint: The base frequency controls how quickly the rotation angles change across positions. Lower values create faster rotations, which may lose long-range position information.

exercises.prompt: Disable RoPE entirely by replacing the rotation with an identity operation (multiply by 1). Compare the generated names to see if the model can still learn position without explicit encoding.
exercises.hint: Set the rotation angles to zero so cos=1 and sin=0. The model may still learn some positional patterns from the causal mask alone.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE).

Same architecture as version 03 (PyTorch), but learned positional embeddings are
replaced with Rotary Position Embeddings. RoPE encodes position by rotating query
and key vectors in complex space, so the dot product q·k naturally depends on the
*relative* distance (m-n) rather than absolute positions m and n separately.
Every modern LLM (LLaMA, Mistral, GPT-NeoX) uses RoPE. This lab shows why.

The RoPE technique is from "RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position
Embedding" (Su et al., 2021), https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.09864. The implementation
uses the real-valued rotation form (pairs of adjacent dimensions), matching the
approach used in production by LLaMA ("LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation
Language Models", Touvron et al., 2023, https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.13971). Note
that the base frequency of 10000 follows the original paper; later work like
"YaRN: Efficient Context Window Extension of Large Language Models" (Peng et al.,
2023, https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.00071) explores different base frequencies for
length extension.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model config
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# RoPE helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def precompute_freqs(dim, max_len):
    """Precompute rotation frequencies for RoPE.

    theta_i = 1 / (10000 ^ (2i / dim))  for i in 0..dim//2
    Returns cos and sin of shape (max_len, dim//2).
    """
    i = torch.arange(0, dim, 2, dtype=torch.float32)  # (dim//2,)
    theta = 1.0 / (10000.0 ** (i / dim))  # (dim//2,)
    positions = torch.arange(max_len, dtype=torch.float32)  # (max_len,)
    angles = torch.outer(positions, theta)  # (max_len, dim//2)
    return torch.cos(angles), torch.sin(angles)


def apply_rope(x, cos_freqs, sin_freqs):
    """Apply rotary embeddings to x of shape (B, n_head, T, head_dim).

    Split head_dim into pairs, rotate each pair:
        [x1, x2] -> [x1*cos - x2*sin, x1*sin + x2*cos]
    """
    T = x.shape[2]
    cos_t = cos_freqs[:T].unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)  # (1, 1, T, dim//2)
    sin_t = sin_freqs[:T].unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)  # (1, 1, T, dim//2)
    x1 = x[..., 0::2]  # even indices
    x2 = x[..., 1::2]  # odd indices
    out1 = x1 * cos_t - x2 * sin_t
    out2 = x1 * sin_t + x2 * cos_t
    return torch.stack((out1, out2), dim=-1).flatten(-2)


# Precompute once for the whole model
rope_cos, rope_sin = precompute_freqs(head_dim, block_size)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model components
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, use_rope=False):
        super().__init__()
        self.use_rope = use_rope
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        if self.use_rope:
            q = apply_rope(q, rope_cos, rope_sin)
            k = apply_rope(k, rope_cos, rope_sin)
            # V is NOT rotated — RoPE only affects Q and K

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, use_rope=False):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention(use_rope=use_rope)
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, use_rope=False):
        super().__init__()
        self.use_rope = use_rope
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        if not use_rope:
            self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block(use_rope=use_rope) for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        x = self.wte(idx)
        if not self.use_rope:
            x = x + self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(x)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training + comparison
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def train(model, label, num_steps=1000):
    """Train a model and return the loss history."""
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
    losses = []
    for step in range(num_steps):
        doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
        tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]])
        targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]])

        logits = model(input_ids)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
        for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
            pg["lr"] = lr_t
        optimizer.step()
        losses.append(loss.item())

        if (step + 1) % 200 == 0:
            print(f"  [{label}] step {step + 1:4d}/{num_steps} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

    return losses


def generate(model, label, num_samples=10, temperature=0.5):
    """Generate names from a trained model."""
    model.eval()
    print(f"\n--- {label}: generated names ---")
    with torch.no_grad():
        for i in range(num_samples):
            tokens = [BOS]
            for _ in range(block_size):
                idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]])
                logits = model(idx)
                logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
                probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
                token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
                if token_id == BOS:
                    break
                tokens.append(token_id)
            name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
            print(f"  sample {i + 1:2d}: {name}")
    model.train()


# Train both variants from the same random initialization
print("\n=== Training: Learned Positional Embeddings (baseline) ===")
torch.manual_seed(42)
model_learned = MicroGPT(use_rope=False)
n_params_learned = sum(p.numel() for p in model_learned.parameters())
print(f"  params: {n_params_learned}")
losses_learned = train(model_learned, "learned-pos")

print("\n=== Training: Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) ===")
torch.manual_seed(42)
model_rope = MicroGPT(use_rope=True)
n_params_rope = sum(p.numel() for p in model_rope.parameters())
print(f"  params: {n_params_rope} (no wpe — {n_params_learned - n_params_rope} fewer)")
losses_rope = train(model_rope, "rope")

# Compare final losses
print("\n=== Results ===")
avg_window = 50
avg_learned = sum(losses_learned[-avg_window:]) / avg_window
avg_rope = sum(losses_rope[-avg_window:]) / avg_window
print(f"  learned-pos  final avg loss (last {avg_window}): {avg_learned:.4f}")
print(f"  rope         final avg loss (last {avg_window}): {avg_rope:.4f}")

generate(model_learned, "Learned Positional Embeddings")
generate(model_rope, "RoPE")

21.text: RoPE changes how position is represented inside attention, not the surrounding training loop, so the setup remains intentionally familiar.

22.text: Use the same shared corpus so the only meaningful change is how position enters the model.

23.text: Fix the training order so any behavioral change comes from rotary position encoding, not from a different run.

25.text: PyTorch handles the mechanics; the new idea in this lab is geometric, not infrastructural.

26.text: Same transformer building blocks as before. That makes RoPE easier to isolate as an attention-level upgrade rather than a full architectural rewrite.

27.text: Softmax and loss stay ordinary. The interesting difference is where positional information enters the computation.

29.text: Lock both RNGs so the before-and-after story stays about position handling instead of run-to-run noise.

30.text: PyTorch gets its own seed because initialization is part of the experiment too.

35.text: Portable path to the shared dataset.

36.text: If the dataset file is missing, we will download it automatically. This makes the script self-contained so you can run it without manual setup.

37.text: Lazy import, only when the file needs downloading.

39.text: The raw URL for Karpathy's names dataset. About 32K human names that serve as our training corpus across all labs.

40.text: Fetch the file from the internet once and save it locally. After the first download, every future run loads instantly from disk.

42.text: Read all names from disk, strip whitespace, and drop blank lines. Each name becomes one training document for next-token prediction.

43.text: Shuffle so the model doesn't learn alphabetical order.

45.text: Unique sorted characters become the base vocabulary.

46.text: BOS token gets the next available id.

53.text: Embedding dimension, small enough to train quickly, large enough to see RoPE's effect.

54.text: 4 attention heads, each operating on its own subspace.

55.text: Single layer keeps focus on the positional encoding change.

56.text: Maximum sequence length, names are short.

63.text: Precompute the rotation frequencies once, they never change during training or inference.

69.text: Pair up dimensions: indices 0,2,4,... Each pair will be rotated together as a 2D vector.

70.text: The frequency for each pair: theta_i = 1/10000^(2i/dim). Lower pairs rotate fast (local patterns), higher pairs rotate slowly (global patterns). 10000 is the standard base from the RoPE paper.

71.text: Position indices: 0, 1, 2, ... Each position gets a different rotation angle.

72.text: Outer product gives a 2D table: angles[pos][pair] = how much to rotate dimension-pair pair at position pos.

73.text: Return cos and sin, the two components of the rotation matrix. Precomputed once, reused every forward pass.

76.text: Apply the rotation to query or key tensors. This is where position information enters the attention computation.

82.text: Grab the sequence length to slice the precomputed frequencies.

83.text: Broadcast cos frequencies to match the (batch, heads, time, pairs) shape.

85.text: Split each head's dimensions into even-indexed (x1) and odd-indexed (x2), these form 2D rotation pairs.

86.text: The paired dimension, together with x1, this forms a 2D vector that gets rotated.

87.text: 2D rotation formula, real part: x1*cos - x2*sin. Same as rotating a point in 2D space.

88.text: 2D rotation formula, imaginary part: x1*sin + x2*cos. The angle encodes the absolute position.

89.text: Interleave rotated pairs back into the original dimension ordering. The key insight: when Q at position m and K at position n are dot-producted, the rotation angles subtract, making attention depend on m-n (relative distance) automatically.

93.text: Compute once at startup. These frequency tables are shared by all layers and all forward passes.

99.text: RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm, simpler (no mean subtraction), works just as well. Used by LLaMA and most modern LLMs.

100.text: Dimension parameter stored for documentation; RMSNorm doesn't actually need it.

102.text: A tiny epsilon (1e-5) added inside the square root to prevent division by zero when activations happen to be near-zero.

105.text: Normalize by root-mean-square of activations. No learned scale parameter in this minimal version.

108.text: Attention module with a flag to switch between learned positional embeddings and RoPE.

109.text: The use_rope flag lets us run a fair A/B comparison with identical architecture otherwise.

111.text: Store the flag so forward knows which position strategy to use.

112.text: Query projection, same for both variants.

113.text: Key projection, same weights, but RoPE will rotate these before the dot product.

114.text: Value projection, notably, RoPE does NOT touch values.

115.text: Output projection merges heads back together.

117.text: The attention forward pass takes a full input tensor and produces contextualized output. RoPE rotation happens internally before the dot product.

118.text: Unpack batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension.

119.text: Project to queries, reshape into heads: (B, T, C) -> (B, n_head, T, head_dim).

120.text: Keys get the same per-head reshape as queries. Each head's keys will only be compared against that same head's queries.

121.text: Same for values, but values won't be rotated even with RoPE.

123.text: Branch: if RoPE is enabled, rotate Q and K by position-dependent angles.

124.text: Rotate queries, each position gets a unique rotation angle.

125.text: Rotate keys, when Q and K are dot-producted, the rotation angles subtract to give relative position.

129.text: Upper-triangular mask: each position can only attend to itself and earlier positions (causal/autoregressive).

130.text: Set future positions to -infinity so softmax gives them zero weight.

133.text: Weighted sum of values, reshape back from heads to embedding dimension.

134.text: Output projection, mix information across heads.

137.text: Feed-forward network: expand to 4x, apply nonlinearity, project back.

140.text: Expand to 4x the embedding dimension, the "hidden" layer of the MLP.

141.text: Project back down to embedding dimension.

143.text: ReLU activation between the two linear layers.

144.text: Two-layer MLP with ReLU: the pattern-matching complement to attention's pattern-finding.

147.text: Transformer block: attention + MLP, each with pre-norm and residual connection.

148.text: Thread the RoPE flag down to the attention layer so it knows whether to apply rotary position encoding or rely on the learned position embeddings.

150.text: Pre-norm before attention (LLaMA-style, more stable than post-norm).

151.text: Attention layer, configured for either learned positions or RoPE.

152.text: Normalize activations before the feed-forward network. Like the pre-attention norm, this stabilizes training by keeping activations on a consistent scale.

153.text: The position-independent feed-forward network. After attention has mixed information across positions, the MLP transforms each position's representation individually.

155.text: Forward: residual connections let gradients flow straight through.

161.text: The full model, identical architecture for both variants, differing only in positional encoding.

162.text: Constructor takes use_rope to choose the position strategy.

164.text: Remember whether this model uses RoPE so the forward pass knows whether to add learned position embeddings or skip them entirely.

165.text: Token embedding table: maps each of 27 token ids to a 16-dimensional vector.

166.text: Only create learned positional embeddings when NOT using RoPE, this is the parameter saving.

167.text: Learned position embeddings: one vector per position. RoPE eliminates this entire table.

169.text: Stack transformer blocks, each configured with the same position strategy.

170.text: Output projection: from embedding space back to vocabulary logits.

171.text: Walk through every submodule and apply the custom weight initializer. This ensures consistent starting conditions whether the module is a linear layer or an embedding.

174.text: Small random initialization (std=0.08) for all linear and embedding weights.

176.text: Normal distribution init, not too large (exploding), not too small (vanishing).

178.text: Forward pass: embed, add positions (if learned), normalize, transform, project to logits.

180.text: Convert each input token ID into a dense 16-dimensional vector. These embeddings are the model's learned representation of each character.

181.text: Only add learned positional embeddings for the baseline variant.

182.text: Add position vectors to token embeddings, the learned-position approach from labs 01-08.

184.text: Send the representation through each transformer block in sequence. Each block first applies attention (with or without RoPE) then the MLP.

186.text: Project to vocabulary-sized logits for next-token prediction.

192.text: Shared training function, both models train with identical hyperparameters for a fair comparison.

194.text: Adam optimizer with slightly aggressive betas for small-model training.

196.text: Train for 1000 steps, cycling through the shuffled dataset.

197.text: Cycle through names. With 32K names and 1000 steps, we see ~3% of the data.

199.text: Clip to block_size to handle names longer than 16 characters.

201.text: Input: all tokens except the last.

202.text: Target: all tokens shifted by one, the next-token prediction task.

205.text: Cross-entropy loss: how surprised is the model by the correct next token?

207.text: Zero out gradients from the previous step.

208.text: Backpropagate the loss through the entire computation graph.

209.text: Linear learning rate warmdown: start at 1e-2, linearly decay to 0.

210.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every optimizer parameter group so the next weight update takes a smaller step as training progresses.

221.text: Generate names autoregressively, one token at a time, feeding each back as input.

223.text: Switch to eval mode (disables dropout, etc.).

225.text: No gradient computation needed during generation.

227.text: Start with just the BOS token.

229.text: Feed the current sequence (up to block_size) through the model.

230.text: Get logits for the last position, the model's prediction for the next token.

231.text: Divide by temperature: lower = more confident/repetitive, higher = more random/creative.

232.text: Turn raw logits into a proper probability distribution via softmax. Sampling from these probabilities gives us the next token.

233.text: Sample from the distribution, stochastic generation.

234.text: If we sampled BOS, the name is complete.

236.text: Add the sampled token and continue generating.

237.text: Decode token ids back to characters.

243.text: Train the baseline first: standard learned positional embeddings, same as labs 01-08.

244.text: Reset seed so both models start from the same random initialization.

245.text: Baseline model with learned position embeddings.

246.text: Count parameters, the baseline has extra parameters for the position embedding table.

248.text: Train the baseline and capture loss history.

250.text: Now train the RoPE variant with the same seed and data order.

251.text: Same random init for a fair comparison.

252.text: RoPE model, no learned position embeddings, positions encoded through rotation.

253.text: Count parameters, fewer than baseline because there's no wpe embedding table.

254.text: Show the parameter saving: RoPE encodes position for free (no learned parameters).

255.text: Train RoPE variant with identical hyperparameters.

259.text: Moving average of recent losses smooths out step-to-step noise.

260.text: Average the last 50 losses for each model.

261.text: Same window for the RoPE model.

265.text: Generate names from the learned-position model.

266.text: Generate names from the RoPE model, quality should be comparable despite fewer parameters.

labs.title: Grouped-Query Attention (GQA/MQA)
labs.tagline: From MHA to MQA to GQA: sharing KV heads reduces memory.
labs.description:
Grouped-Query Attention is a simple but powerful optimization: instead of giving every query head its own key-value head, multiple query heads share the same KV head.

Standard multi-head attention (MHA) has equal numbers of Q, K, and V heads. Multi-query attention (MQA) uses just one KV head for all queries. GQA is the middle ground — groups of query heads share a KV head. This reduces KV cache memory (critical for inference) with minimal quality loss.

LLaMA 2 70B, Mistral, and Gemma all use GQA. This lab implements all three variants so you can compare them directly.
learning_objectives: Implement MHA, MQA, and GQA attention variants
Analyze the memory-quality tradeoff of KV head sharing
Compare attention head configurations used in production models

exercises.prompt: Train the model with MHA, MQA, and GQA using the same number of total parameters. Which variant produces the best names after the same number of steps?
exercises.hint: Adjust embedding dimensions so total parameter count is similar. Compare loss curves and generated output quality across all three variants.

exercises.prompt: Print the KV cache size (in number of elements) for each variant during generation. What is the memory ratio between MHA and MQA?
exercises.hint: Count the number of key and value tensors stored. MQA uses 1 KV head vs. n_heads for MHA, so the ratio should match the number of query heads.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — Grouped-Query Attention (GQA/MQA) edition.

Shows the progression from Multi-Head Attention (MHA) → Multi-Query Attention
(MQA) → Grouped-Query Attention (GQA). The key idea: KV heads can be shared
across multiple query heads. At production scale this often preserves most of
the quality while dramatically reducing memory for the KV cache during inference.

Same base architecture as lab 03 (PyTorch), but the attention module is
parameterised by n_kv_head so a single class covers all three variants.

The standard multi-head attention mechanism was introduced in "Attention Is All
You Need" by Vaswani et al. (2017) (https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762). Shazeer
(2019) later proposed Multi-Query Attention in "Fast Transformer Decoding: One
Write-Head is All You Need" (https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.02150), which reduces
the KV cache to a single head. Ainslie et al. (2023) generalised this idea in
"GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models from Multi-Head
Checkpoints" (https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.13245), showing that an intermediate
number of KV heads offers a favourable trade-off between quality and efficiency.

This implementation demonstrates the core concept of KV head sharing as
described in Ainslie et al., using a simplified single-file transformer so the
mechanism is easy to study in isolation.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model config
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model components
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class FlexAttention(nn.Module):
    """Unified attention that covers MHA, GQA, and MQA via n_kv_head."""

    def __init__(self, n_kv_head):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_kv_head = n_kv_head
        assert n_head % n_kv_head == 0, "n_head must be divisible by n_kv_head"
        self.repeats = n_head // n_kv_head

        # Q projection: always full heads
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        # K, V projections: only n_kv_head heads
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_kv_head * head_dim, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_kv_head * head_dim, bias=False)
        # Output projection
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape

        # Project queries (full heads), keys and values (fewer heads)
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, n_head, T, hd)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, self.n_kv_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, n_kv_head, T, hd)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, self.n_kv_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, n_kv_head, T, hd)

        # Expand KV heads to match Q heads by repeating
        if self.repeats > 1:
            k = k.repeat_interleave(self.repeats, dim=1)  # (B, n_head, T, hd)
            v = v.repeat_interleave(self.repeats, dim=1)  # (B, n_head, T, hd)

        # Standard scaled dot-product attention with causal mask
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_kv_head):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = FlexAttention(n_kv_head)
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_kv_head):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block(n_kv_head) for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training + comparison
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
num_steps = 1000
temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high

variants = [
    ("MHA", n_head),  # 4 KV heads — standard multi-head attention
    ("GQA", 2),  # 2 KV heads — grouped-query attention
    ("MQA", 1),  # 1 KV head  — multi-query attention
]


def generate(model, label, num_samples=10):
    """Generate names from a trained model."""
    model.eval()
    print(f"\n  --- {label} generated names ---")
    with torch.no_grad():
        for sample_idx in range(num_samples):
            tokens = [BOS]
            for _ in range(block_size):
                idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
                logits = model(idx)
                logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
                probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
                token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
                if token_id == BOS:
                    break
                tokens.append(token_id)
            name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
            print(f"  sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")


docs_snapshot = list(docs)

for variant_name, n_kv_head in variants:
    print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
    print(f"  {variant_name}: n_head={n_head}, n_kv_head={n_kv_head}")
    print(f"{'=' * 60}")

    # Reset seed and restore original doc order for fair comparison
    random.seed(42)
    torch.manual_seed(42)
    docs = list(docs_snapshot)

    model = MicroGPT(n_kv_head).to(device)
    num_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
    print(f"num params: {num_params}")

    # KV cache memory estimate (fp16): n_kv_head * head_dim * seq_len * 2(K+V) * 2(bytes) * n_layer
    kv_cache_bytes = n_kv_head * head_dim * block_size * 2 * 2 * n_layer
    print(
        f"KV cache memory (fp16): {kv_cache_bytes} bytes "
        f"({n_kv_head} kv_heads x {head_dim} head_dim x {block_size} seq_len x 2 tensors x 2 bytes x {n_layer} layers)"
    )

    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)

    for step in range(num_steps):
        doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
        tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
        targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

        logits = model(input_ids)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
        for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
            pg["lr"] = lr_t
        optimizer.step()

        if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
            print(f"  step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

    print(f"\n  {variant_name} final loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
    generate(model, variant_name)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Summary
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("  Summary: KV head sharing vs parameter count")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
for variant_name, n_kv_head in variants:
    kv_params = 2 * n_kv_head * head_dim * n_embd * n_layer  # wk + wv params
    kv_cache = n_kv_head * head_dim * block_size * 2 * n_layer * 2
    print(
        f"  {variant_name:3s} (n_kv_head={n_kv_head}): "
        f"KV proj params={kv_params:4d}, "
        f"KV cache (fp16)={kv_cache:4d} bytes"
    )
print("\n  At production scale, GQA is often the expected sweet spot: fewer KV params than MHA,")
print("  more capacity than MQA. This tiny run mainly demonstrates the KV-cache tradeoff, not a decisive quality ranking.")

26.text: GQA changes the economics of attention, not the surrounding training loop, so the setup stays familiar while the key-value sharing happens deeper in the model.

27.text: Use the same shared corpus so the comparison is about attention head sharing, not about a different task.

28.text: The whole point is to compare MHA, GQA, and MQA fairly, so the stochastic parts of the run need to stay pinned down.

30.text: PyTorch handles the usual tensor work. The new idea in this lab is architectural: query heads no longer need one private KV head each.

31.text: Same standard layers as before. That is useful here because it makes the KV-sharing change stand out clearly instead of being buried in unrelated refactors.

32.text: Loss and softmax stay unchanged. GQA is a memory-layout and capacity tradeoff, not a new objective.

34.text: Reset both RNGs so differences in quality, parameter count, and cache size are attributable to KV sharing alone.

35.text: The weight initialization must match too; otherwise the variant comparison would be contaminated before attention even runs once.

40.text: Construct a portable file path to the shared dataset, relative to this script's location, so the code works regardless of where you clone the repo.

41.text: If the dataset file is missing, we download it automatically. This keeps the script self-contained with no manual setup required.

47.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace and skipping blanks. Each name becomes one training document.

50.text: Gather every unique character from all names and sort them to build a deterministic character-to-integer vocabulary mapping.

51.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID, sitting just past the last letter.

58.text: Same small model config as previous labs, differences will come from KV head sharing.

59.text: 4 query heads, this stays constant across all three variants.

60.text: Single layer keeps the comparison focused on attention architecture.

61.text: The longest sequence the model can process at once. Names are short, so 16 tokens is more than enough for this dataset.

68.text: RMSNorm, same lightweight normalization as lab 10.

74.text: Normalize by root-mean-square, no learned parameters.

77.text: The star of this lab: one attention class that handles MHA, GQA, and MQA by varying n_kv_head.

80.text: The only parameter that changes between variants: how many KV heads to use.
