82.text: Remember how many KV heads this attention layer uses. This value controls how much K/V sharing happens during the forward pass.

83.text: Query heads must divide evenly into KV groups, each group of query heads shares one KV head.

84.text: The repeat factor is the whole sharing ratio: 1 means classic MHA, 2 means grouped-query attention, 4 means multi-query attention.

87.text: Queries always have the full n_head heads, query capacity is never reduced.

89.text: Reducing key heads is where GQA saves KV-cache memory. Here two query-head groups can share one K stream instead of learning four separate ones.

90.text: MQA is the extreme case on the same axis: one shared value head can serve all query heads, minimizing cache size at some quality cost.

92.text: Output projection is always full-sized, it recombines all query heads.

94.text: Forward pass, project, expand KV if needed, attend.

95.text: Unpack batch, sequence length, embedding dimension.

98.text: Queries: always full heads. Shape: (B, n_head, T, head_dim).

99.text: Keys: only n_kv_head heads. With MQA this is (B, 1, T, head_dim), 4x smaller than queries.

100.text: Values: same reduced shape as keys.

103.text: If KV heads < query heads, we need to expand KV to match. This is the GQA/MQA trick.

104.text: Repeat each KV head to serve its group of query heads. Logically shared, but physically copied for the matmul. In practice, this repeat is nearly free, it's a view/broadcast, not a memcpy.

105.text: Values get the same expansion as keys so that each query head has a matching value vector to retrieve content from.

108.text: Standard scaled dot-product attention, identical for all three variants after KV expansion.

109.text: Causal mask: no peeking at future tokens.

113.text: Weighted sum of values, reshape back to (B, T, C).

114.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

117.text: Standard MLP, unchanged across variants.

120.text: Project each token's embedding into a 4x wider hidden space to give the network more room to compute.

121.text: Compress the expanded 4x-wide hidden representation back down to the original embedding dimension.

123.text: Run the input through the two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

127.text: Transformer block parameterized by KV head count.

128.text: Thread the KV head count down to the attention layer so it knows how many key-value heads to allocate and how much sharing to apply.

130.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer. Pre-norm keeps gradients well-behaved and is the standard in modern transformers like LLaMA.

132.text: Normalize activations before the feed-forward network. Like the pre-attention norm, this stabilizes training by keeping activations on a consistent scale.

135.text: Forward: residual connections around attention and MLP.

136.text: Add the attention output back to the input via a residual connection. This lets the layer refine the representation without risk of destroying useful information.

137.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

141.text: Full model, takes n_kv_head to configure the attention variant.

142.text: KV head count flows from model to block to attention.

144.text: Look up a learned 16-dimensional vector for each input token to represent its identity.

145.text: A learned vector for each position in the sequence. Added to token embeddings so the model can distinguish token order, since attention alone is permutation-invariant.

146.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer layers.

147.text: Stack of transformer blocks, all with the same KV head configuration.

148.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

149.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

152.text: Static method that initializes all Linear and Embedding weights with a small Gaussian distribution.

154.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian so activations stay reasonably scaled at the start of training.

156.text: Run the full model on the input sequence to produce next-token logits for every position.

157.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

158.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

159.text: Create a position embedding for each slot in the sequence so the model knows token ordering.

160.text: Sum the token and position embeddings, then normalize. This combined representation is what enters the transformer blocks.

161.text: Send the combined embeddings through each transformer block in sequence. Each block applies attention (with KV sharing) followed by the MLP.

163.text: Map the final hidden state at each position to a 27-dimensional vector of logits, one per vocabulary token, for next-token prediction.

169.text: Use the CPU since this tiny 16-dimensional model trains in seconds. At this scale, GPU transfer overhead would actually slow things down.

173.text: Three variants to compare, all using the same model class with different n_kv_head values.

174.text: MHA is the no-sharing baseline: four query heads, four KV heads, and therefore the largest KV cache. It gives maximum per-head freedom, but also maximum memory cost.

175.text: GQA is the compromise: the four query heads are still there, but they now flow through just two shared KV groups. That cuts cache size in half without collapsing all heads onto one shared memory.

176.text: MQA pushes sharing to the limit: all four query heads read from the same single KV head. It is the cheapest in memory, but also the most aggressive loss of KV diversity.

180.text: Shared generation function for all variants.

181.text: Disable dropout and other training-only behavior so the model produces deterministic outputs.

184.text: Generate 10 sample names from this variant so we can visually compare output quality across MHA, GQA, and MQA configurations.

185.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model knows it is starting fresh.

187.text: Feed current sequence through the model.

188.text: Take the model's prediction for the very last position, which contains the logits for what token should come next in the sequence.

189.text: Divide the logits by the temperature before softmax to control how peaked or flat the distribution is.

190.text: Convert the temperature-scaled logits into a proper probability distribution that sums to one.

191.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution to introduce controlled diversity.

192.text: Stop if BOS is generated (end of name).

194.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating the next one.

195.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into characters and join them into a readable name string.

202.text: Train each variant in sequence with identical data and seeds.

207.text: Reset seeds before each variant for a fair comparison, same random init and data order.

208.text: Reset the PyTorch random seed so each variant starts from exactly the same weight initialization.

212.text: Count total parameters, fewer KV heads = fewer KV projection parameters.

213.text: Instantiate the model with this variant's specific number of KV heads. The same class handles MHA, GQA, and MQA through this one parameter.

216.text: KV cache memory estimate, the key metric for inference. Fewer KV heads = proportionally less cache memory. At 7B scale with 2048 context, this difference is gigabytes per request.

225.text: Pick the next name from the shuffled list, wrapping around when we reach the end. This cycling ensures continuous training across all steps.

227.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits the model's positional embedding table.

228.text: The standard training loop: pick a name, tokenize it, run a forward pass, compute loss, backpropagate, and update weights for 1000 steps.

231.text: Convert the first n token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as input.

233.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy loss.

234.text: Run the full model on the current input batch to get next-token predictions.

236.text: Compute gradients for every parameter by propagating the loss backward through the computation graph.

237.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

238.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it on the next step.

239.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

240.text: Apply the optimizer step, adjusting every parameter in the direction that reduces the loss.

246.text: Generate sample names from this variant.

254.text: Summary table: show how KV sharing affects parameter count and cache size.

255.text: Calculate KV projection parameters (wk + wv weights) for each variant.

256.text: Calculate KV cache memory in bytes (fp16 inference).

labs.title: KV Cache
labs.tagline: The single most important inference optimization.
labs.description:
During autoregressive generation, each new token attends to all previous tokens. Without caching, this means recomputing K and V for the entire sequence at every step — O(n²) total work for n tokens.

The KV cache stores previously computed key and value tensors and appends new ones incrementally. Each generation step only computes K and V for the new token, then concatenates with the cache. This reduces per-step work from O(n) to O(1) for the projection, though attention itself is still O(n).

Every production LLM serving system uses KV caching. It's the difference between seconds and minutes for long sequences.
learning_objectives: Implement incremental KV caching for autoregressive generation
Analyze the complexity reduction from O(T^3) to O(T^2)
Explain why KV caching is the most important inference optimization

exercises.prompt: Disable the KV cache and time the generation of a 50-character name with and without caching. What is the speedup ratio?
exercises.hint: Without cache, each step recomputes all keys and values from scratch. Time both versions and divide. The ratio should grow roughly linearly with sequence length.

exercises.prompt: Print the shape of the cached key and value tensors at each generation step. Verify they grow by exactly one position per step.
exercises.hint: After each step, print cache_k.shape and cache_v.shape. The sequence dimension should increment by 1 each time.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — KV Cache edition.

Same model as lab 03, but with a KV-cache-aware inference path that avoids
recomputing Key and Value tensors for already-processed positions. This is
THE fundamental optimization behind every fast LLM serving system.
Without KV cache: each new token reprocesses the full sequence -> O(n^3) total.
With KV cache: each new token only computes attention for ONE position -> O(n^2) total.

KV caching is a standard inference optimization for autoregressive transformers,
implicit in the original "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017),
https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762 decoder design. The technique became essential
at scale as documented in "Efficient Transformers: A Survey" (Tay et al., 2022),
https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.06732. This lab implements the basic form: cache K and
V tensors from previous positions, compute only the new position's Q/K/V, and
concatenate with the cache. Every production serving system (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM,
etc.) uses this as its foundation.
"""

import math
import os
import random
import time

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model config
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head

# ===========================================================================
# Unified model — supports both standard and KV-cached inference
# ===========================================================================
# One model, two inference paths. When kv_cache=None, attention behaves like
# standard causal attention (same as lab 03). When kv_cache is provided,
# only the new positions compute Q/K/V and cached K/V are prepended.


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    """Attention with optional KV cache for efficient autoregressive decoding."""

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x, kv_cache=None):
        B, T_new, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T_new, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T_new, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T_new, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        if kv_cache is not None:
            cached_k, cached_v = kv_cache
            k = torch.cat([cached_k, k], dim=2)
            v = torch.cat([cached_v, v], dim=2)

        new_cache = (k, v)
        T_total = k.shape[2]

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        # Causal mask: row i (absolute pos T_total-T_new+i) attends to cols 0..abs_pos
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T_new, T_total, device=x.device, dtype=torch.bool), diagonal=T_total - T_new + 1)
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T_new, C)
        return self.wo(out), new_cache


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x, kv_cache=None):
        attn_out, new_cache = self.attn(self.norm1(x), kv_cache=kv_cache)
        x = x + attn_out
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x, new_cache


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx, past_caches=None, start_pos=0):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(start_pos, start_pos + T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        new_caches = []
        for i, layer in enumerate(self.layers):
            cache_i = past_caches[i] if past_caches is not None else None
            x, new_cache = layer(x, kv_cache=cache_i)
            new_caches.append(new_cache)
        return self.lm_head(x), new_caches


# ===========================================================================
# Training (identical to lab 03 — ignores caches during training)
# ===========================================================================
device = "cpu"
model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

    logits, _ = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()

    if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ===========================================================================
# Inference comparison — naive (no cache) vs KV cache
# ===========================================================================
# Same model, same weights — two generation strategies.
temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
num_samples = 20
model.eval()


def sample_token(logits):
    probs = F.softmax(logits[0, -1] / temperature, dim=-1)
    return torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()


def generate_naive(num_samples=20):
    """Standard generation: re-run full sequence at every step. O(n^3) total attention."""
    total_attn_ops = 0
    names = []
    for sample_idx in range(num_samples):
        torch.manual_seed(1000 + sample_idx)
        tokens = [BOS]
        for _ in range(block_size):
            idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
            T = idx.shape[1]
            total_attn_ops += T * T * n_head
            logits, _ = model(idx)  # no cache — recomputes everything
            token_id = sample_token(logits)
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        names.append("".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:]))
    return names, total_attn_ops


def generate_with_cache(num_samples=20):
    """KV-cache generation: only process new token each step. O(n^2) total attention."""
    total_attn_ops = 0
    names = []
    for sample_idx in range(num_samples):
        torch.manual_seed(1000 + sample_idx)
        tokens = [BOS]
        past_caches = None
        for _ in range(block_size):
            if past_caches is None:
                idx = torch.tensor([tokens], device=device)
                T_new, start_pos = len(tokens), 0
            else:
                idx = torch.tensor([[tokens[-1]]], device=device)
                T_new, start_pos = 1, len(tokens) - 1
            total_attn_ops += T_new * len(tokens) * n_head
            logits, past_caches = model(idx, past_caches=past_caches, start_pos=start_pos)
            token_id = sample_token(logits)
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        names.append("".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:]))
    return names, total_attn_ops


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("INFERENCE COMPARISON: Naive vs KV Cache")
print("=" * 60)

with torch.no_grad():
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    names_naive, ops_naive = generate_naive(num_samples)
    t_naive = time.perf_counter() - t0

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    names_cached, ops_cached = generate_with_cache(num_samples)
    t_cached = time.perf_counter() - t0

# Verify identical outputs
print("\n--- Generated names (both methods produce identical output) ---")
all_match = all(n1 == n2 for n1, n2 in zip(names_naive, names_cached))
for i, (n1, n2) in enumerate(zip(names_naive, names_cached)):
    match = "OK" if n1 == n2 else "MISMATCH!"
    print(f"  {i + 1:2d}: {n1:12s}  |  {n2:12s}  [{match}]")
print(f"\nAll outputs identical: {all_match}")

# Operation counts
print("\n--- Attention operation counts (Q*K multiply-adds) ---")
print(f"  Naive (full recompute):  {ops_naive:,} ops")
print(f"  KV cache (incremental):  {ops_cached:,} ops")
print(f"  Reduction:               {ops_naive / ops_cached:.1f}x fewer operations")

# Timing
print("\n--- Wall-clock time ---")
print(f"  Naive:    {t_naive * 1000:.1f} ms")
print(f"  KV cache: {t_cached * 1000:.1f} ms")
if t_cached > 0:
    print(f"  Speedup:  {t_naive / t_cached:.2f}x")

# Theoretical analysis
T = block_size
print("\n--- Theoretical analysis ---")
print(f"  For a sequence of length T = {T}:")
print(f"  Naive total attention:   sum(t^2 for t=1..T) = T(T+1)(2T+1)/6 = {T * (T + 1) * (2 * T + 1) // 6} (per head)")
print(f"  Cached total attention:  sum(t   for t=1..T) = T(T+1)/2       = {T * (T + 1) // 2} (per head)")
print(f"  Ratio:                   (2T+1)/3 = {(2 * T + 1) / 3:.1f}x")

# ===========================================================================
# Summary
# ===========================================================================
print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("WHY KV CACHE MATTERS")
print("=" * 60)
print("""
  Cache stores K,V from previous positions. Each new token computes only its
  own Q,K,V and attends to all cached K,V — no recomputation.

                  Without cache         With cache
  Step 1:         1 x 1  attention      1 x 1  attention
  Step 2:         2 x 2  attention      1 x 2  attention
  Step 3:         3 x 3  attention      1 x 3  attention
  ...
  Step T:         T x T  attention      1 x T  attention
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Total:          ~T^3/3 operations     ~T^2/2 operations

  At scale (7B model, 2048 ctx): ~680x speedup, ~1 GB cache per request.
  Prerequisite for speculative decoding, PagedAttention, disaggregated serving.
""")

20.text: This lab is about wasted inference work, so even the imports hint at the story: same model, but now we can time the naive path against the cached one.

21.text: Same dataset, same task. That keeps the focus on the inference optimization rather than on model or data changes.

22.text: Randomness is only supporting cast here; the real experiment is how much repeated computation we can eliminate during generation.

23.text: Timing matters in this lab because KV cache is not a theoretical nicety, it is the optimization that makes autoregressive decoding practical.

25.text: PyTorch gives us the same baseline model as before. The important difference is that attention can now remember yesterday's K and V instead of recomputing them.

26.text: Nothing dramatic changes in the layer stack. That is the point: the speedup comes from preserving history, not from inventing a new model.

27.text: Softmax and loss stay ordinary. What changes is how much of the prefix attention has to rebuild at each decoding step.

29.text: Benchmarks only mean something if the runs are comparable, so we lock the stochastic pieces down before measuring.

30.text: Set the PyTorch random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

35.text: Construct a portable file path to the shared dataset, relative to this script's location, so the code works regardless of where you clone the repo.

36.text: If the dataset file is missing, we download it automatically. This keeps the script self-contained with no manual setup required.

42.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

43.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

46.text: Gather every unique character from all names and sort them to build a deterministic character-to-integer vocabulary mapping.

47.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

48.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

54.text: Same tiny model config as lab 03, the model is identical, only inference changes.

55.text: Four parallel attention heads, each processing a different 4-dimensional slice.

56.text: A single transformer layer keeps the comparison focused on the caching optimization.

57.text: The longest sequence the model can process at once. Names are short, so 16 tokens is more than enough for this dataset.

58.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the 16-dimensional embedding.

74.text: Divide by the root-mean-square of the activation vector. Simpler than LayerNorm since it skips mean subtraction, but equally effective for stabilizing training.

77.text: The critical change: attention now accepts an optional KV cache. Same weights, two inference paths.

82.text: Q, K, V, and output projections, identical to the uncached version.

83.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into key vectors for attention.

84.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into value vectors for attention.

85.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension.

87.text: Forward now takes an optional kv_cache, this is the entire innovation of this lab.

88.text: T_new is 1 during cached inference (just the new token) vs. full sequence length without cache.

89.text: Project queries for the new positions only. During cached inference, this is a single token.

90.text: Project keys for the new positions only.

91.text: Project values for the new positions only.

93.text: If we have a cache from previous steps, prepend it.

94.text: Unpack the cached K and V tensors from previous positions.

95.text: This is the crucial reuse step: old keys stay alive, and only the new key is appended. The history grows without being recomputed.

96.text: Values follow the same pattern as keys, completing the cached prefix. The new token now sees the full past through reused K/V rather than rebuilt K/V.

98.text: Save the updated cache for the next step, it grows by one position each time.

99.text: T_total = total sequence length including cached positions.

101.text: Attention: Q is (B, heads, T_new, hd), K is (B, heads, T_total, hd). The result is (B, heads, T_new, T_total).

103.text: Build the causal mask with torch.triu: each new query at absolute position T_total-T_new+i can attend to all positions up to and including itself, but not beyond.

104.text: Replace scores for future positions with negative infinity. After softmax, these become exactly zero, enforcing the causal constraint that tokens cannot peek ahead.

107.text: Weighted sum of values and reshape.

108.text: Return both the output AND the updated cache, the cache is passed to the next step.

111.text: The two-layer feed-forward network, unchanged from previous labs. The MLP operates per-position and does not need any caching since it has no cross-position state.

114.text: Project each token's representation into a 4x wider hidden space for richer computation.

115.text: Compress the expanded hidden representation back down to the original embedding dimension.

117.text: Run the input through the two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation.

118.text: Two linear layers with ReLU in between. The expansion to 4x width gives the network more room to learn nonlinear transformations before compressing back down.

121.text: Transformer block, now threads the KV cache through.

124.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer. Pre-norm keeps gradients well-behaved and is the standard in modern transformers like LLaMA.

125.text: The attention sublayer, now accepting an optional KV cache. When a cache is provided, it only processes new positions and prepends cached keys and values.

129.text: Forward now accepts and returns a cache.

130.text: Attention: receives cache, returns updated cache.

131.text: Add the attention output back to the input via a residual connection. The MLP and attention each contribute refinements without overwriting the original representation.

132.text: MLP with residual (MLP doesn't need a cache).

133.text: Return both the block's output and the updated KV cache. The caller collects caches from all layers and passes them back on the next generation step.

136.text: Full model, threads caches through all layers.

139.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

140.text: Positional embeddings, start_pos will offset these during cached inference.

141.text: Normalize the combined embeddings before feeding them into the transformer layers.

142.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each with its own attention and MLP. During cached inference, each layer maintains its own independent KV cache.

143.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for next-token prediction.

144.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

147.text: Static method that initializes weights with a small Gaussian for stable early training.

149.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian so activations stay reasonably scaled.

151.text: Forward now takes past_caches and start_pos, the KV cache interface.

152.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

153.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

154.text: Position embeddings offset by start_pos. During cached inference, this is the position of the new token, not 0. Without this offset, the model would think every new token is at position 0.

155.text: Combine token and position embeddings, normalize.

156.text: Collect updated caches from each layer.

158.text: Grab the cache for this layer (or None on the first pass).

159.text: Forward through the layer, receiving the updated cache.

160.text: Save this layer's updated cache into the collection. On the next generation step, it will be passed back so the layer can skip recomputing past keys and values.

161.text: Return logits AND the full cache stack for the next generation step.

167.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

168.text: Instantiate the model with the same architecture as lab 03. The only difference is that the forward pass now supports an optional KV cache for efficient generation.

171.text: Set up the Adam optimizer with momentum and epsilon tuned for this small model.

172.text: Train for 1000 steps, which is enough for this tiny model to learn basic name patterns.

174.text: Training loop, caches are ignored during training (standard teacher-forced training).

175.text: Pick the next training document by cycling through the shuffled name list.

176.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

177.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits the model's positional embeddings.

179.text: Convert the token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as its input sequence.

180.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

182.text: Forward pass. The _ discards caches, training doesn't need them.

183.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy.

185.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

186.text: Compute gradients for every parameter by propagating the loss backward through the graph.

187.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

188.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it.

190.text: Apply the optimizer step, adjusting every parameter in the direction that reduces the loss.

199.text: Temperature controls the sampling distribution's sharpness. At 0.5, high-probability tokens become even more dominant, producing more conservative but coherent names.

201.text: Switch to eval mode for inference.

204.text: Shared sampling helper: take logits, apply temperature, sample one token.

205.text: Temperature-scaled softmax on the last position's logits.

209.text: Naive generation: re-run the FULL sequence through the model at every step. This is O(n^3) total attention.

211.text: Track attention operations to quantify the difference.

213.text: Fixed seed per sample so naive and cached produce identical outputs.

214.text: Same seed as cached generation, outputs MUST match.

215.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

217.text: Feed the ENTIRE sequence so far. This is the waste, we recompute Q/K/V for all previous tokens.

218.text: Count attention operations: T*T per head, for all heads.

219.text: O(T^2) per step, and T grows each step, giving O(T^3) total.

220.text: No cache, everything is recomputed from scratch every step.

221.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution for stochastic generation.

222.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

224.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating.

225.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into a readable name string.

229.text: KV-cached generation: only process the NEW token each step. O(n^2) total attention.

231.text: Count the attention operations (Q times K multiply-adds) at each step so we can precisely measure how much computation the KV cache saves.

234.text: Same seed, outputs will be identical.

235.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

236.text: Initialize the cache as empty since no tokens have been processed yet on the first pass.

238.text: First step: no cache yet, process the full initial sequence (just BOS).

240.text: Position zero for the very first token. The positional embedding needs this offset to assign the correct position encoding to the BOS token.

242.text: Subsequent steps: only feed the LAST token. This is the key optimization, one token instead of the full sequence.

243.text: Start position = where this token sits in the sequence.

244.text: Attention ops: T_new * T_total per head. With T_new=1, this is just T_total, linear, not quadratic.

245.text: This is the entire payoff of the lab in one line: feed one fresh token in, get one larger cache out. The old prefix survives as state instead of being rebuilt from scratch.

246.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution for stochastic generation.

247.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

249.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating.

250.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into a readable name string.

258.text: Disable gradient tracking for both generation methods so the comparison is fair and neither wastes time computing gradients during inference.

259.text: Start the wall-clock timer for naive generation. Even at this tiny scale, the cubic cost is measurable against the quadratic cached version.

263.text: Start the wall-clock timer for cached generation. Comparing against the naive timer reveals the real-world speedup from avoiding recomputation.

269.text: Verify outputs are IDENTICAL, the cache is an optimization, not an approximation. Same model, same weights, same random seed = same output.

270.text: Side-by-side comparison of every generated name.

271.text: Flag any mismatch (there shouldn't be any).

279.text: The headline metric: how many fewer attention operations the cache requires.

285.text: Real timing comparison. At this tiny scale the speedup is modest; at 7B scale it's 100x+.

289.text: Theoretical analysis of the complexity difference.

292.text: Naive: sum of t^2 for t=1..T = T(T+1)(2T+1)/6. Cubic growth.

293.text: Cached: sum of t for t=1..T = T(T+1)/2. Quadratic growth.

294.text: The ratio grows linearly with sequence length, longer sequences benefit more from caching.

labs.title: Sampling Strategies
labs.tagline: Five strategies producing completely different output from the same model.
labs.description:
The model outputs logits — raw scores for every token in the vocabulary. How you convert logits to a token choice dramatically affects output quality.

This lab implements five strategies: greedy (always pick the highest), temperature scaling (sharpen or flatten the distribution), top-k (only consider the k most likely), top-p/nucleus (only consider tokens whose cumulative probability reaches p), and min-p (only consider tokens above a fraction of the top token's probability).

The same model produces boring repetitive output with greedy sampling and creative diverse output with temperature + top-p. Understanding sampling is essential for anyone building LLM applications.
learning_objectives: Implement five sampling strategies: greedy, temperature, top-k, top-p, min-p
Analyze how each strategy affects output diversity and quality
Design sampling configurations for different use cases

exercises.prompt: Generate 20 names with temperature=0.1 and then with temperature=2.0. Count how many unique names you get in each case.
exercises.hint: Low temperature makes the distribution peaked (repetitive output), high temperature makes it flat (diverse but potentially nonsensical). Count duplicates in each batch.

exercises.prompt: Combine top-k=5 with top-p=0.9 in the same sampling call. Which filter is more restrictive in practice? Print the number of candidates remaining after each filter.
exercises.hint: Apply top-k first, then top-p to the remaining candidates. Print len(candidates) after each step to see which one eliminates more options.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — Sampling Strategies.

Same microGPT architecture as the PyTorch edition (03), but focused on
demonstrating how different sampling strategies shape model output.
Five strategies: greedy, temperature, top-k, top-p (nucleus), min-p.
Same model, completely different outputs.

Top-p (nucleus) sampling is from "The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration"
(Holtzman et al., 2020), https://arxiv.org/abs/1904.09751. Min-p sampling is
from "Min P Sampling: Balancing Creativity and Coherence at High Temperature"
(Nguyen et al., 2024), https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.01082. Temperature scaling
and top-k are standard techniques. Top-p and min-p are the most widely used in
production -- top-p dynamically adjusts the candidate set based on distribution
shape, while min-p offers a simpler adaptive threshold.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

    logits = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()

    if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Sampling strategies
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


def sample_greedy(logits):
    """Always pick the highest probability token. Deterministic but repetitive."""
    return logits.argmax().item()


def sample_temperature(logits, T=1.0):
    """Scale logits by 1/T before softmax.
    T->0: approaches greedy (peaked). T=1: model's learned distribution.
    T->inf: approaches uniform random."""
    probs = F.softmax(logits / T, dim=-1)
    return torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()


def sample_top_k(logits, k=5, T=1.0):
    """Keep only top-k logits, set rest to -inf, then sample with temperature.
    Prevents sampling from the long tail of unlikely tokens."""
    scaled = logits / T
    topk_vals, _ = torch.topk(scaled, k)
    scaled[scaled < topk_vals[-1]] = float("-inf")
    probs = F.softmax(scaled, dim=-1)
    return torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()


def sample_top_p(logits, p=0.9, T=1.0):
    """Nucleus sampling: sort by probability, keep smallest set whose
    cumulative probability >= p, zero out rest, renormalize, sample.
    Dynamically adjusts candidates based on distribution shape."""
    probs = F.softmax(logits / T, dim=-1)
    sorted_probs, sorted_idx = torch.sort(probs, descending=True)
    cumsum = torch.cumsum(sorted_probs, dim=-1)
    # Remove tokens with cumulative probability above p (keep first token always)
    mask = cumsum - sorted_probs > p
    sorted_probs[mask] = 0.0
    sorted_probs /= sorted_probs.sum()
    # Scatter back to original order
    out = torch.zeros_like(probs)
    out.scatter_(0, sorted_idx, sorted_probs)
    return torch.multinomial(out, 1).item()


def sample_min_p(logits, min_p=0.05, T=1.0):
    """Keep tokens with probability >= min_p * max_probability.
    Simpler than top-p, adapts to distribution shape naturally.
    Peaked distributions: few tokens pass. Flat: many pass."""
    probs = F.softmax(logits / T, dim=-1)
    threshold = min_p * probs.max()
    probs[probs < threshold] = 0.0
    probs /= probs.sum()
    return torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Generation
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


def generate(model, sampler_fn, n=20):
    """Generate n names using the given sampling function."""
    names = []
    for _ in range(n):
        tokens = [BOS]
        for _ in range(block_size):
            idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
            logits = model(idx)
            raw_logits = logits[0, -1]
            token_id = sampler_fn(raw_logits.clone())
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
        names.append(name)
    return names


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Compare all strategies
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("SAMPLING STRATEGY COMPARISON")
print("=" * 60)

model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    strategies = [
        ("Greedy (T→0)", lambda lg: sample_greedy(lg)),
        ("Temperature 0.3 (conservative)", lambda lg: sample_temperature(lg, T=0.3)),
        ("Temperature 1.0 (model dist)", lambda lg: sample_temperature(lg, T=1.0)),
        ("Temperature 2.0 (chaotic)", lambda lg: sample_temperature(lg, T=2.0)),
        ("Top-k=3, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_top_k(lg, k=3, T=0.8)),
        ("Top-k=10, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_top_k(lg, k=10, T=0.8)),
        ("Top-p=0.5, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_top_p(lg, p=0.5, T=0.8)),
        ("Top-p=0.95, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_top_p(lg, p=0.95, T=0.8)),
        ("Min-p=0.1, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_min_p(lg, min_p=0.1, T=0.8)),
        ("Min-p=0.01, T=0.8", lambda lg: sample_min_p(lg, min_p=0.01, T=0.8)),
    ]

    for label, sampler in strategies:
        # Reset RNG for reproducibility within each strategy
        torch.manual_seed(42)
        names = generate(model, sampler, n=20)
        print(f"\n--- {label} ---")
        for i, name in enumerate(names):
            print(f"  {i + 1:2d}. {name}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Visualization: probability distributions after applying each filter
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("PROBABILITY DISTRIBUTION VISUALIZATION")
print("=" * 60)

# Show how each strategy reshapes the probability distribution for
# the same input. We encode "mar" and look at next-token probabilities.
prefix_str = "mar"
prefix_tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in prefix_str]

with torch.no_grad():
    idx = torch.tensor([prefix_tokens], device=device)
    logits = model(idx)
    raw_logits = logits[0, -1]  # logits for next token after "mar"

    def bar_chart(label, probs, top_n=10):
        """Print a horizontal bar chart of the top-n token probabilities."""
        n_active = (probs > 0).sum().item()
        print(f"\n  {label}  ({n_active} active tokens)")
        n = min(top_n, n_active)
        vals, idxs = torch.topk(probs, n)
        bar_width = 30  # max bar length in characters
        for v, i in zip(vals, idxs):
            tok = uchars[i.item()] if i.item() < len(uchars) else "."
            p = v.item()
            bar = "#" * int(p * bar_width + 0.5)
            print(f"    {tok} {bar:<{bar_width}} {p:.0%}")

    print(f'\nNext token after "{prefix_str}":')

    # Raw distribution (T=1.0)
    raw_probs = F.softmax(raw_logits, dim=-1)
    bar_chart("Raw (T=1.0)", raw_probs)

    # Temperature 0.3
    t03_probs = F.softmax(raw_logits / 0.3, dim=-1)
    bar_chart("T=0.3 (conservative)", t03_probs)

    # Temperature 2.0
    t20_probs = F.softmax(raw_logits / 2.0, dim=-1)
    bar_chart("T=2.0 (chaotic)", t20_probs)

    # Top-k=3
    topk_logits = raw_logits.clone() / 0.8
    topk_vals, _ = torch.topk(topk_logits, 3)
    topk_logits[topk_logits < topk_vals[-1]] = float("-inf")
    topk_probs = F.softmax(topk_logits, dim=-1)
    bar_chart("Top-k=3, T=0.8", topk_probs)

    # Top-p=0.9
    topp_probs = F.softmax(raw_logits / 0.8, dim=-1)
    sorted_probs, sorted_idx = torch.sort(topp_probs, descending=True)
    cumsum = torch.cumsum(sorted_probs, dim=-1)
    mask = cumsum - sorted_probs > 0.9
    sorted_probs[mask] = 0.0
    sorted_probs /= sorted_probs.sum()
    topp_out = torch.zeros_like(topp_probs)
    topp_out.scatter_(0, sorted_idx, sorted_probs)
    bar_chart("Top-p=0.9, T=0.8", topp_out)

    # Min-p=0.1
    minp_probs = F.softmax(raw_logits / 0.8, dim=-1)
    threshold = 0.1 * minp_probs.max()
    minp_probs[minp_probs < threshold] = 0.0
    minp_probs /= minp_probs.sum()
    bar_chart("Min-p=0.1, T=0.8", minp_probs)

print()

18.text: Sampling does not change the model's knowledge at all, only how we turn its logits into decisions, so the surrounding setup stays intentionally boring.

19.text: Same corpus, same trained model family. The lab is isolating one question: how much output behavior comes from decoding policy alone?

20.text: Reproducibility matters here because sampling is deliberately stochastic and we want to compare strategies, not chase random drift.

22.text: PyTorch carries the same model as earlier labs. The interesting action comes after the model emits logits, not inside the network itself.

23.text: The transformer stays the same. That makes the lesson sharper: wildly different outputs can come from changing only the decoding rule.

24.text: Softmax is central here because every sampling strategy is really a different way of shaping or pruning the probability distribution before selection.

26.text: Lock both RNGs so the demonstrations are repeatable and the difference between strategies is interpretable.

27.text: PyTorch's RNG also controls sampling itself, so seeding it is part of making the comparison honest.

32.text: Construct a portable file path to the shared dataset, relative to this script's location, so the code works regardless of where you clone the repo.

33.text: Download the dataset from the internet if it is not already on disk.

39.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

40.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

42.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset to form the token vocabulary.

43.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

44.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

50.text: Same model config as lab 03, the model is identical, only the sampling changes.

51.text: Four parallel attention heads, each processing a different 4-dimensional slice of the embedding.

52.text: A single transformer layer is enough for this small character-level model.

53.text: Maximum context window of 16 tokens, which comfortably fits the longest names in the dataset.

54.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the 16-dimensional embedding.

57.text: Root-mean-square normalization that stabilizes activations without learned parameters.

63.text: Divide by the root-mean-square of the activation vector. Simpler than LayerNorm since it skips mean subtraction, but equally effective for stabilizing training.

66.text: Standard causal self-attention, identical to lab 03.

70.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into key vectors for attention.

71.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into value vectors for attention.

72.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension.

74.text: Run the attention computation: project to Q, K, V, apply the causal mask, and combine.

75.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input tensor shape.

76.text: Project and reshape queries into heads.

77.text: Project and reshape the input into key vectors, one per attention head.

78.text: Project and reshape the input into value vectors, one per attention head.

80.text: Compute scaled dot-product attention scores between every pair of query and key vectors.

81.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask so each token can only attend to itself and earlier positions.

82.text: Replace future-position scores with negative infinity so softmax assigns them zero weight.

83.text: Convert attention scores into a probability distribution that sums to one over the key dimension.

85.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors and reshape back to the original embedding layout.

86.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

89.text: A standard two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

92.text: Project each token's embedding into a 4x wider hidden space for richer computation.

93.text: Compress the expanded hidden representation back down to the original embedding dimension.

95.text: Run the input through the two-layer MLP with ReLU activation in between.

96.text: Two linear layers with ReLU in between. The expansion to 4x width gives the network more room to learn nonlinear transformations before compressing back down.

99.text: One transformer block containing self-attention and a feed-forward network with residual connections.

102.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

103.text: The causal self-attention sublayer that lets each token attend to previous positions.

104.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

105.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

107.text: Apply attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections that let gradients flow easily.

108.text: Add the attention output to the residual stream, preserving the original information.

109.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

113.text: The full transformer model, identical to lab 03, serving as the shared backbone for all sampling strategies.

116.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

117.text: Learned positional embeddings that tell the model where each token sits in the sequence.

118.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer.

119.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each containing attention and MLP sublayers.

120.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

121.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

125.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian so activations stay reasonably scaled at the start.

128.text: Run the full model on the input sequence to produce next-token logits for every position.

129.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

130.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

131.text: A learned vector for each position in the sequence. Added to token embeddings so the model knows where each token sits, since attention alone is position-agnostic.

132.text: Add token and position embeddings together, then normalize before entering the transformer.

133.text: Pass the normalized representation through each transformer block in sequence.

135.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

141.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

142.text: Instantiate the model with the same architecture as lab 03. The same trained weights will be used by every sampling strategy, isolating the effect of the sampler.

143.text: Display the total parameter count to verify the model size before training.

145.text: Set up the Adam optimizer with momentum and epsilon tuned for this small model.

146.text: Train for 1000 steps, enough for this tiny model to learn basic character-level name patterns.

148.text: Standard training loop, identical to lab 03.

149.text: Pick the next training document by cycling through the shuffled name list.

150.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

151.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits inside the model's positional embedding table.

153.text: Convert the first n token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as input.

154.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

156.text: Run the full model on the current input batch to get next-token predictions.

157.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy loss.

159.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

160.text: Compute gradients for every parameter by propagating the loss backward through the graph.

161.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

162.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it on the next step.

164.text: Apply the optimizer step, adjusting every parameter in the direction that reduces the loss.

174.text: The simplest possible strategy: always pick the most likely token. Deterministic, produces the same output every time. Safe but boring.

176.text: argmax = highest probability token. No randomness at all.

179.text: Temperature reshapes the probability distribution. Low temperature sharpens it (confident), high temperature flattens it (random). The model weights don't change, you're just adjusting the "confidence knob."

183.text: Divide logits by T before softmax. T=0.5 doubles the logits, making the peak sharper. T=2.0 halves them, spreading probability more evenly.

187.text: Top-k: hard cutoff at exactly k candidates. Simple and effective, but the cutoff is arbitrary, sometimes 5 tokens are plausible, sometimes 50 are.

190.text: Scale the logits by temperature first to reshape the distribution, then mask everything outside the top k. This order matters because temperature changes which tokens are "top."

191.text: Find the k-th highest logit value.

192.text: Everything below the threshold becomes -infinity. Exactly k tokens survive.

193.text: Softmax over only the surviving tokens, they now get all the probability mass.

197.text: Top-p (nucleus sampling): keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. Adapts to the distribution, peaked distributions get few candidates, flat ones get many.

201.text: Divide logits by temperature and convert to probabilities before sorting. The temperature reshapes the distribution first, which affects which tokens fall inside the nucleus.

202.text: Sort tokens by probability, highest first.

203.text: Cumulative sum: after each token, how much total probability have we accumulated?

205.text: Remove tokens once cumulative probability exceeds p, but always keep the top token. The subtraction ensures the first token that pushes past p is kept.

206.text: Zero out tokens beyond the nucleus.

209.text: Scatter back to original token ordering for sampling.

210.text: Put filtered probabilities back at their original indices.

211.text: Draw one token randomly from the nucleus. Only the tokens inside the cumulative probability threshold compete, so unlikely tokens are excluded naturally.

214.text: Min-p: keep tokens with probability >= min_p * max_prob. Simpler than top-p, naturally adapts: when the model is confident (high max_prob), threshold is high and few tokens pass; when uncertain, threshold drops and more tokens are considered.

218.text: Divide logits by temperature and convert to probabilities. The temperature is applied before min-p filtering to reshape which tokens clear the relative threshold.

219.text: Threshold = min_p fraction of the highest probability. A relative cutoff, not absolute.

220.text: Zero out anything below the threshold.

221.text: Divide by the new total so the surviving probabilities sum back to one after filtering.

222.text: Draw one token from the tokens that survived the min-p filter. Fewer survivors when the model is confident, more when it is uncertain, all handled automatically.

230.text: Shared generation function: same model, different sampler, that's the whole point of this lab.

232.text: Allocate an empty list to collect the generated names. Each strategy fills this list, and we display them side-by-side afterward for easy comparison.

234.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

236.text: Feed current sequence through the model.

237.text: Extract the raw logit vector for the last position, which predicts what token comes next.

238.text: Extract the raw logits for the last position, the model's prediction for what comes next.

239.text: Clone so each sampler gets a fresh copy (some modify logits in-place).

240.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

242.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and move on to the next position.

243.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into characters and join them into a readable name string.

255.text: Switch to evaluation mode so the model produces consistent outputs without training noise.

256.text: Disable gradient tracking since we only need forward passes for generation, not training.

257.text: Ten sampling strategies, all using the exact same trained model.

258.text: Greedy: deterministic, always the most likely name. Same output every time.

259.text: T=0.3: nearly greedy but with a tiny bit of randomness. Very conservative names.

260.text: T=1.0: the model's own learned distribution, unmodified. The "honest" output.

261.text: T=2.0: chaotic, flattens probabilities so rare tokens get sampled often. Produces gibberish.

262.text: Top-k=3 with T=0.8: only the 3 most likely tokens compete. Simple but rigid.

263.text: Top-k=10 with T=0.8: 10 candidates. More variety than k=3.

264.text: Top-p=0.5 with T=0.8: tight nucleus, keeps just enough tokens to cover 50% of probability mass.

265.text: Top-p=0.95 with T=0.8: loose nucleus, almost everything gets a chance.

266.text: Min-p=0.1 with T=0.8: keep tokens with >= 10% of the top token's probability. Adapts automatically.

267.text: Min-p=0.01 with T=0.8: very permissive, most tokens pass the threshold.

270.text: Loop through all ten sampling configurations, generate 20 names with each, and print the results. Same model throughout, so any differences come purely from the sampler.

272.text: Reset RNG for each strategy so they all start from the same random state.

286.text: Visualize how each strategy reshapes the probability distribution for a specific context.

287.text: Encode "mar" to see what the model predicts next, likely 'i', 'y', 'a', etc.

291.text: Run the model on "mar" to get raw logits for the next token.

292.text: Logits for the next token after "mar".

295.text: Helper to format the top probabilities as a readable string.

296.text: Show the top tokens and their probabilities, plus how many tokens have non-zero probability.

308.text: Raw model distribution at T=1.0, the model's unfiltered opinion.

311.text: T=0.3: the distribution becomes very peaked, the top token dominates.

315.text: T=2.0: the distribution flattens out, even unlikely tokens get noticeable probability.

319.text: Top-k=3: exactly 3 tokens survive, rest are zero. Hard cutoff.

326.text: Top-p=0.9: keep tokens until cumulative probability hits 90%. Variable number of candidates.

337.text: Min-p=0.1: keep tokens with >= 10% of the max probability. Naturally adaptive.

labs.title: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)
labs.tagline: Freeze the model, inject tiny low-rank matrices, and train only those.
labs.description:
LoRA is the most popular parameter-efficient fine-tuning method. Instead of updating all model weights, you freeze them and inject small low-rank matrices (A and B) alongside each target layer.

The original weight matrix W stays frozen. A new path x → A → B (where A and B have much smaller dimensions) learns the task-specific adaptation. At inference, you can merge W + BA into a single matrix with zero overhead.

This lab shows LoRA applied to the attention projections. With rank 4, you train ~1% of the original parameters while matching full fine-tuning quality on this task.
learning_objectives: Implement low-rank adaptation matrices for parameter-efficient fine-tuning
Explain how LoRA achieves full fine-tuning quality with ~1% parameters
Evaluate when to use LoRA vs. full fine-tuning

exercises.prompt: Change the LoRA rank from 4 to 1 and to 16. Compare the final loss and generated names for each rank. At what rank does quality stop improving?
exercises.hint: Lower rank means fewer trainable parameters and faster training, but potentially worse adaptation. Track loss convergence and output quality for each rank.

exercises.prompt: Apply LoRA to only the value projection instead of all attention projections. Does the model still adapt successfully?
exercises.hint: Modify which layers receive LoRA adapters. The value projection often captures the most task-specific information, so it may be sufficient alone.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT with LoRA — parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

Pre-trains a standard microGPT on all names, then fine-tunes with LoRA
(Low-Rank Adaptation) on a filtered subset — names whose consonants are
all drawn from {m, n, r} (e.g., emma, aria, naomi, aurora). This creates a
distinct phonetic style that is easy to inspect in the generated samples. Only the small
LoRA matrices are trained — the base model stays frozen. Demonstrates:
  - Freezing base weights and injecting low-rank adapters
  - Training a small fraction of parameters to shift the output distribution
  - Merging LoRA weights back into the base model (zero runtime overhead)

Reference:
  "LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models" (Hu et al., 2021)
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685

This implementation follows the core LoRA algorithm from Hu et al. (2021),
including the alpha/rank scaling factor. For simplicity, adapters are
injected only on the query and value projections (wq, wv) — the original
paper found this subset sufficient for most tasks.
"""

import copy
import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# LoRA Linear layer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


class LoRALinear(nn.Module):
    """Wraps an existing nn.Linear with a low-rank adaptation.

    The base weight is frozen. Only lora_A and lora_B are trainable.
    B is zero-initialized so the adapter starts as a no-op (W + 0).
    The scaling factor alpha/rank controls the magnitude of the adaptation
    (see Hu et al., 2021, Section 4.1).
    """

    def __init__(self, base_linear, rank=4, alpha=None):
        super().__init__()
        self.base = base_linear
        self.base.weight.requires_grad_(False)  # freeze base
        d_out, d_in = base_linear.weight.shape
        self.lora_A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(rank, d_in) * 0.01)
        self.lora_B = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(d_out, rank))  # init B to zero
        self.scaling = (alpha if alpha is not None else rank) / rank

    def forward(self, x):
        # base output + scaled low-rank adaptation
        # W*x + (alpha/rank) * B*A*x  (B is zero-init, so LoRA starts as no-op)
        return self.base(x) + (x @ self.lora_A.T) @ self.lora_B.T * self.scaling


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (identical to lab 03)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high


def generate(model, n_samples=20, label="sample", check_fn=None, quiet=False):
    """Generate names from the model, optionally marking matches."""
    model.eval()
    names = []
    with torch.no_grad():
        for i in range(n_samples):
            tokens = [BOS]
            for _ in range(block_size):
                idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
                logits = model(idx)
                logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
                probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
                token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
                if token_id == BOS:
                    break
                tokens.append(token_id)
            name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
            names.append(name)
            if not quiet:
                mark = f"  {'<-- soft' if check_fn(name) else ''}" if check_fn else ""
                print(f"  {label} {i + 1:2d}: {name}{mark}")
    model.train()
    return names


def train_step(model, optimizer, doc, step, num_steps, base_lr=1e-2):
    """Single training step (shared between pre-training and fine-tuning)."""
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)
    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

    logits = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = base_lr * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()
    return loss.item()


# ===========================================================================
# Phase 1: Pre-train base model on ALL names
# ===========================================================================
print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("PHASE 1: Pre-training base model on all names")
print("=" * 60)

model = MicroGPT().to(device)
total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
print(f"total params: {total_params}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    loss = train_step(model, optimizer, doc, step, num_steps)
    if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"  step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps} | loss {loss:.4f}")

print("\nBaseline generation (pre-trained on all names):")
base_names = generate(model, n_samples=10, label="base")

# Save base model state for later comparison
base_state = copy.deepcopy(model.state_dict())

# ===========================================================================
# Phase 2: Create fine-tuning target — "soft" names (consonants ⊆ {m, n, r})
# ===========================================================================
vowels = set("aeiou")
soft_consonants = set("mnr")
ft_docs = [d for d in docs if (set(d.lower()) - vowels).issubset(soft_consonants) and len(set(d.lower()) - vowels) >= 1]
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("PHASE 2: Fine-tuning target — 'soft' names (consonants ⊆ {m, n, r})")
print(f"{'=' * 60}")
print(f"filtered dataset: {len(ft_docs)} names (out of {len(docs)} total)")
print(f"examples: {', '.join(ft_docs[:8])}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# LoRA helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


def is_soft(name):
    """Check if a name uses only 'soft' consonants (m, n, r)."""
    return (set(name.lower()) - vowels).issubset(soft_consonants) and len(name) > 0


def inject_lora(module, rank, alpha=None, targets=("wq", "wv")):
    """Replace targeted nn.Linear layers with LoRALinear wrappers."""
    for name, child in module.named_children():
        if isinstance(child, nn.Linear) and name in targets:
            setattr(module, name, LoRALinear(child, rank=rank, alpha=alpha))
        else:
            inject_lora(child, rank=rank, alpha=alpha, targets=targets)


def merge_lora(module):
    """Merge LoRA weights back: W_new = W_base + scaling * B @ A."""
    for name, child in module.named_children():
        if isinstance(child, LoRALinear):
            merged = nn.Linear(
                child.base.weight.shape[1],
                child.base.weight.shape[0],
                bias=False,
            )
            merged.weight = nn.Parameter(child.base.weight + child.scaling * child.lora_B @ child.lora_A)
            setattr(module, name, merged)
        else:
            merge_lora(child)


# Generate baseline scores (same seed for fair comparison)
base_model = MicroGPT().to(device)
base_model.load_state_dict(base_state)
print("\nBase model (no fine-tuning):")
torch.manual_seed(42)
base_only_names = generate(base_model, n_samples=20, label="base", check_fn=is_soft)
base_soft = sum(1 for n in base_only_names if is_soft(n))
print(f"  -> {base_soft}/20 soft names")

# ===========================================================================
# Phase 3: Rank ablation — LoRA with rank 1, 2, 4
# ===========================================================================

base_total = sum(p.numel() for p in base_model.parameters())
n_samples = 20
ft_steps = 500
results = []  # (rank, adapter_params, pct, soft_count, soft_pct, merge_ok)

for lora_rank in [1, 2, 4]:
    print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
    print(f"RANK {lora_rank}: Inject → Fine-tune → Evaluate → Merge")
