    print("=" * 60)

    # Restore base model and freeze all weights
    model = MicroGPT().to(device)
    model.load_state_dict(base_state)
    for p in model.parameters():
        p.requires_grad_(False)

    # Inject LoRA on query and value projections (standard recipe)
    for layer in model.layers:
        inject_lora(layer, rank=lora_rank)

    total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
    trainable = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
    frozen = total_params - trainable
    assert frozen == base_total, f"freeze error: {frozen} != {base_total}"
    pct = 100.0 * trainable / total_params

    # Show adapter dimensions: A(rank×d_in) @ B(d_out×rank) adapts W(d_out×d_in)
    for name, child in model.named_modules():
        if isinstance(child, LoRALinear):
            d_out, d_in = child.base.weight.shape
            r = child.lora_A.shape[0]
            a_size = r * d_in
            b_size = d_out * r
            short = name.split(".")[-1]
            print(
                f"  {short}: W({d_out}x{d_in})={d_out * d_in} params"
                f" → A({r}x{d_in})={a_size} + B({d_out}x{r})={b_size}"
                f" = {a_size + b_size} adapter params"
            )
    print(f"  total: {trainable} adapter params ({pct:.1f}% of {total_params}, base frozen: {frozen})")

    # Fine-tune on soft names
    lora_params = [p for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad]
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(lora_params, lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
    for step in range(ft_steps):
        doc = ft_docs[step % len(ft_docs)]
        loss = train_step(model, optimizer, doc, step, ft_steps)
        if (step + 1) % 100 == 0 or step == 0:
            print(f"  step {step + 1:4d} / {ft_steps} | loss {loss:.4f}")

    # Generate and evaluate
    torch.manual_seed(42)
    lora_names = generate(model, n_samples=n_samples, label=f"r{lora_rank}", check_fn=is_soft)
    soft_count = sum(1 for n in lora_names if is_soft(n))
    soft_pct = 100.0 * soft_count / n_samples
    print(f"  -> {soft_count}/{n_samples} soft names ({soft_pct:.0f}%)")

    # Merge LoRA weights into base and verify identical output
    merge_lora(model)
    torch.manual_seed(42)
    merged_names = generate(model, n_samples=n_samples, quiet=True)
    merge_ok = all(a == b for a, b in zip(lora_names, merged_names))
    merged_total = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
    print(f"  merge: {'ok' if merge_ok else 'MISMATCH'}, params back to {merged_total}")

    results.append((lora_rank, trainable, pct, soft_count, soft_pct, merge_ok))

# ===========================================================================
# Summary table
# ===========================================================================
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
print("RANK ABLATION SUMMARY")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"  Base model: {base_soft}/{n_samples} soft ({100.0 * base_soft / n_samples:.0f}%)")
print(f"  Base params: {base_total}")
print()
print(f"  {'rank':>4}  {'adapter':>7}  {'% total':>7}  {'soft':>4}  {'soft%':>5}  {'merge':>5}")
print(f"  {'----':>4}  {'-------':>7}  {'-------':>7}  {'----':>4}  {'-----':>5}  {'-----':>5}")
for rank, ap, pct, sc, sp, mo in results:
    print(f"  {rank:>4}  {ap:>7}  {pct:>6.1f}%  {sc:>2}/{n_samples}  {sp:>4.0f}%  {'ok' if mo else 'FAIL':>5}")

23.text: LoRA works by leaving the base model intact and learning a tiny correction on top, so making clean copies of the trained weights matters.

24.text: The transformer math stays standard. LoRA is not changing attention itself, only how we adapt certain layers with a small trainable detour.

25.text: Use the same corpus and same task so the comparison is about adaptation strategy, not about different data.

26.text: We want to isolate the effect of LoRA, so all the ordinary sources of randomness stay controlled.

28.text: PyTorch gives us the full trainable baseline model; LoRA's trick will be to train only a tiny low-rank side path instead of touching all weights.

29.text: Standard layers, same architecture. The educational value here is seeing how little of that architecture actually needs to move during fine-tuning.

30.text: The objective and nonlinearities stay unchanged; parameter efficiency comes entirely from how we choose to adapt the existing weights.

32.text: Fix both RNGs so the story stays about full fine-tuning versus low-rank adaptation, not about luck in initialization or sampling.

33.text: Set the PyTorch random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

38.text: Build a portable file path to the dataset, relative to this script's location.

39.text: Download the dataset from the internet if it is not already on disk.

45.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

46.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

47.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset to form the token vocabulary.

48.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

49.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

58.text: The LoRA wrapper: the key innovation of this lab. Wraps an existing nn.Linear and adds two tiny matrices that learn to steer the original layer's output.

67.text: Takes an existing trained linear layer and wraps it with low-rank adapters.

69.text: Store the original linear layer. Its weight will be frozen during fine-tuning.

70.text: Freeze the base weight so no gradients flow into it during LoRA fine-tuning.

71.text: Read the dimensions from the existing weight matrix.

72.text: lora_A: the down-projection (rank x d_in). Random init with small values to break symmetry. This matrix learns to read important features from the input.

74.text: lora_B: the up-projection (d_out x rank). Zero init is crucial — it makes the adapter a no-op at the start. The model behaves exactly like the base model until training shifts B away from zero.

76.text: Forward pass: base output + adapter output. Two paths, summed.

79.text: The math: W*x + B*A*x. Since B starts at zero, the adapter contributes nothing initially. As B learns non-zero values, the adapter gently steers the output. With rank=2, A is (2, d_in) and B is (d_out, 2) — tiny compared to W's (d_out, d_in).

86.text: Four parallel attention heads, each processing a different 4-dimensional slice of the embedding.

87.text: A single transformer layer is enough for this small character-level model.

88.text: Maximum context window of 16 tokens, which comfortably fits the longest names.

89.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the 16-dimensional embedding.

92.text: Root-mean-square normalization that stabilizes activations without learned parameters.

98.text: Normalize by root-mean-square to keep activations stable without learned scale parameters.

101.text: Standard causal attention, identical to lab 03.

104.text: Q, K, V, output projections. LoRA will later wrap wq and wv only (the standard LoRA recipe from the original paper).

105.text: Key projection — not wrapped by LoRA in this lab, following the finding that K projections contribute less to adaptation.

106.text: Value projection — will be wrapped by LoRA alongside wq.

107.text: Output projection — not wrapped by LoRA in this lab.

110.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input tensor shape.

111.text: Project and reshape the input into query vectors, one per attention head.

112.text: Project and reshape the input into key vectors, one per attention head.

113.text: Project and reshape the input into value vectors, one per attention head.

115.text: Compute scaled dot-product attention scores between every pair of query and key vectors.

116.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask so each token can only attend to itself and earlier positions.

117.text: Replace future-position scores with negative infinity so softmax assigns them zero weight.

118.text: Convert attention scores into a probability distribution that sums to one over the key dimension.

120.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors and reshape back to the original embedding layout.

121.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

124.text: A standard two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

127.text: Project each token's embedding into a 4x wider hidden space for richer computation.

128.text: Compress the expanded hidden representation back down to the original embedding dimension.

130.text: Run the input through the two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation.

131.text: Run the two-layer MLP with ReLU, producing the feed-forward output for this position.

134.text: One transformer block containing self-attention and a feed-forward network with residual connections.

137.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

138.text: The causal self-attention sublayer that lets each token attend to previous positions.

139.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

140.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

142.text: Apply attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections that let gradients flow easily.

143.text: Add the attention output to the residual stream, preserving the original information.

144.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

148.text: Full model, identical to lab 03 for pre-training.

151.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

152.text: Learned positional embeddings that tell the model where each token sits in the sequence.

153.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer.

154.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each containing attention and MLP sublayers.

155.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

156.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

160.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian (std=0.08) so activations start at a reasonable scale, neither exploding nor vanishing through the layers.

163.text: Run the full model on the input sequence to produce next-token logits for every position.

164.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

165.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

166.text: Create a position embedding for each slot in the sequence so the model knows token ordering.

167.text: Add token and position embeddings together, then normalize before entering the transformer.

168.text: Pass the normalized representation through each transformer block in sequence.

170.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

176.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

177.text: Set the sampling temperature to 0.5 for moderately conservative name generation.

180.text: Generate names from the model. The optional check_fn marks names that match the target style, and quiet suppresses output (used for merge verification).

182.text: Switch to evaluation mode so the model produces consistent outputs during generation.

184.text: Disable gradient tracking since we only need forward passes for generation, not training.

186.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

188.text: Feed the current token sequence through the model to get next-token logits. During generation we re-run the full sequence at each step since there is no KV cache.

190.text: Divide the logits by the temperature before softmax to control how peaked the distribution is.

191.text: Convert the temperature-scaled logits into a proper probability distribution that sums to one.

192.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution to introduce controlled diversity.

193.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

195.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating the next one.

196.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into characters and join them into a readable name string.

198.text: Only print when not in quiet mode (merge verification runs silently).

199.text: If a check function is provided, mark matching names with <-- soft so the output is easy to scan visually.

205.text: Shared training step, used for both pre-training and LoRA fine-tuning.

207.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

208.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits the model's positional embedding table.

209.text: Convert the token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as its input sequence.

210.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

212.text: Run the full model on the current input batch to get next-token predictions.

213.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy loss.

215.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

216.text: Backprop. During LoRA fine-tuning, gradients only flow into the unfrozen LoRA parameters.

217.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

219.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it on the next step.

220.text: Update weights. Only LoRA parameters move because base weights are frozen.

231.text: Instantiate the base model that will first be pre-trained on all names. Later, we will freeze these weights and inject LoRA adapters for fine-tuning.

236.text: Train for 1000 steps of standard next-token prediction on the full names corpus. This gives the model a solid foundation before we specialize it with LoRA.

238.text: Pre-training loop: standard next-token prediction on the full names corpus.

239.text: Pick the next name from the shuffled list, wrapping around when we reach the end.

240.text: Run one training step on the current document, returning the loss value.

245.text: Generate some baseline names to see the pre-trained model's behavior before LoRA.

248.text: Save the pre-trained state so we can restore it for each rank in the ablation. Deep copy because LoRA modifies the model in-place.

253.text: Define vowels for the consonant filter.

254.text: The target phonetic style: names using only the soft consonants m, n, r. This makes the fine-tuning subset easy to score and inspect in the generated samples.

255.text: Filter the dataset to names whose consonants are all in {m, n, r}. Requires at least one consonant so we exclude vowel-only strings.

269.text: Check if a generated name qualifies as 'soft' — all its consonants are drawn from {m, n, r}. Used to score the output and annotate printed names.

274.text: Replace only the targeted nn.Linear layers (by name) with LoRALinear wrappers. Defaults to wq and wv, following the original LoRA paper's finding that query and value projections carry the most adaptation signal.

276.text: Walk the module tree looking for layers whose name matches the target list.

277.text: Found a targeted linear layer — wrap it with a LoRA adapter.

278.text: Swap the original linear layer for the LoRA-wrapped version in-place.

281.text: Recurse into child modules to find nested linear layers.

283.text: Merge LoRA: fold the learned adapter weights back into the base weight matrix. After merging, the model is a standard nn.Linear again with zero runtime overhead.

285.text: Walk the module tree looking for LoRA-wrapped layers.

286.text: Found a LoRA layer — merge it back into a plain linear layer.

287.text: Allocate a standard nn.Linear with the same dimensions, ready to receive the merged weight matrix.

292.text: The merge formula: W_new = W_base + B @ A. Mathematically identical to the LoRA forward pass but baked into a single weight matrix — no adapter overhead at inference time.

293.text: Replace the LoRA wrapper with the merged standard linear layer.

296.text: Descend into child modules to merge any deeper LoRA wrappers.

299.text: Reload a fresh copy of the base model to generate comparison samples.

302.text: Pin the random seed so the base and LoRA models sample from the same random sequence for a fair comparison.

303.text: Generate 20 names from the base model with soft-name markers to establish the baseline hit rate.

311.text: Count base model parameters once for the freeze assertion inside the loop.

316.text: Run the full LoRA experiment at rank 1, 2, and 4 to see how adapter size affects the phonetic shift. This is a rank ablation — a standard technique in LoRA research.

322.text: Restore a fresh copy of the pre-trained base model for each rank so experiments are independent.

325.text: Freeze every parameter in the base model. Only the LoRA matrices (injected next) will be trainable.

329.text: Inject LoRA adapters into query and value projections of each transformer layer.

331.text: Recount parameters — total grows slightly because of the LoRA matrices added on top of the frozen base.

332.text: Count only the trainable parameters (the LoRA A and B matrices).

335.text: Safety check: assert that every base parameter is frozen. If this fails, something went wrong with the freeze.

338.text: Walk the model tree to find all LoRA-wrapped layers and display their dimensions.

345.text: Print each adapter's factorization: the full weight W(d_out x d_in) is adapted by tiny matrices A(rank x d_in) and B(d_out x rank). Their product B@A has the same shape as W but uses far fewer parameters.

348.text: Print the total adapter parameter count, its percentage of total model params, and confirm the base model is fully frozen.

352.text: Collect only the LoRA parameters for the optimizer — base weights are excluded.

353.text: Fresh Adam optimizer for fine-tuning, operating only on the tiny LoRA parameter set.

354.text: Fine-tuning loop: 500 steps on the soft-name subset. Same train_step as pre-training, but gradients only flow into the LoRA matrices.

355.text: Cycle through only the soft-consonant names for fine-tuning.

361.text: Generate 20 names from the LoRA-adapted model with the same seed used for the baseline, so results are directly comparable.

364.text: Count how many generated names satisfy the soft-consonant constraint.

369.text: Merge LoRA adapters back into the base weights so the model is a standard nn.Linear again.

371.text: Generate from the merged model silently and verify every name matches the LoRA output exactly — merging is exact, not an approximation.

374.text: After merging, the parameter count should equal the original base model — all LoRA overhead is gone.

385.text: Print the baseline for context: what fraction of names are 'soft' without any LoRA adaptation.

387.text: Print one row per rank. The table shows diminishing returns: even rank 1 (64 params, ~1.5%) achieves a dramatic shift from baseline, while rank 2+ saturates at ~90%.

388.text: Summary table header showing the key metrics: adapter size, percentage of total params, soft-name hit rate, and merge verification.

labs.title: PyTorch Quantized
labs.tagline: Compress a trained model from FP32 to INT8.
labs.description:
INT8 quantization compresses model weights from 32-bit floats to 8-bit integers — a 4× reduction in model size and memory bandwidth.

The process: train normally in FP32, then for each weight matrix, compute a scale factor that maps the float range to [-128, 127]. At inference, multiply the int8 weights by the scale to approximate the original floats. The approximation error is small because neural network weights tend to cluster near zero.

This is how production models run on edge devices, phones, and cost-efficient servers. The lab trains, quantizes, and compares output quality before and after compression.
learning_objectives: Implement post-training INT8 quantization with scale factors
Analyze quantization error and its impact on model output
Evaluate the size-accuracy tradeoff of weight compression

exercises.prompt: Quantize to INT4 instead of INT8 by mapping weights to the range [-8, 7]. How much worse is the generated output compared to INT8?
exercises.hint: Change the scale factor to map to 4-bit range. Compare generated names from FP32, INT8, and INT4 models to see the quality degradation.

exercises.prompt: Print the maximum absolute quantization error for each weight matrix. Which layer has the largest error and why?
exercises.hint: Compute abs(original_weight - dequantized_weight).max() for each layer. Layers with wider weight distributions lose more precision.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — PyTorch quantized edition.

Same architecture as 03_pytorch, but with INT8 quantization for inference.
Trains in FP32, quantizes Linear layers to INT8, compares size and speed.
Shows ~4x memory reduction. Production systems also get speed improvements.

Post-training quantization follows the principles in "Quantization and Training
of Neural Networks for Efficient Integer-Arithmetic-Only Inference" (Jacob et al.,
2018), https://arxiv.org/abs/1712.05877. For LLM-specific quantization, see
"LLM.int8(): 8-bit Matrix Multiplication for Transformers at Scale" (Dettmers
et al., 2022), https://arxiv.org/abs/2208.07339. Note that this lab implements
simulated per-tensor symmetric quantization for educational purposes -- the
forward pass dequantizes INT8 weights back to float, so it demonstrates model
SIZE savings, not the speed improvements that real INT8 GEMM kernels provide.
Production systems use per-channel quantization and also quantize activations.
"""

import math
import os
import random
import time

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (larger than 03 to show quantization benefits)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 64  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 2  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

    logits = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Quantization
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- quantization ---")


# NOTE: This is a *simulated* quantization for educational purposes.
# The forward pass dequantizes INT8 weights back to the activation dtype,
# so it does NOT use actual INT8 GEMM kernels. The benchmark below
# demonstrates model SIZE savings, not speed improvements.
class QuantizedLinear(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, fp32_linear):
        super().__init__()
        w = fp32_linear.weight.data
        scale = w.abs().max() / 127.0
        self.register_buffer("weight_int8", torch.round(w / scale).to(torch.int8))
        self.register_buffer("scale", scale.detach().clone())
        self.bias = fp32_linear.bias

    def forward(self, x):
        return F.linear(x, self.weight_int8.to(x.dtype) * self.scale, self.bias)


def quantize_model(model):
    model_q = type(model)()
    model_q.load_state_dict(model.state_dict())
    for module in model_q.modules():
        for name, child in module.named_children():
            if isinstance(child, nn.Linear):
                setattr(module, name, QuantizedLinear(child))
    return model_q


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference & Benchmark
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high


@torch.no_grad()
def generate(model, n=1):
    model.eval()
    samples = []
    for _ in range(n):
        tokens = [BOS]
        for _ in range(block_size):
            idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
            probs = F.softmax(model(idx)[0, -1] / temperature, dim=-1)
            token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        samples.append(tokens[1:])
    return samples


# NOTE: Speed comparison is included for completeness, but this simulated
# quantization path is expected to be similar or slower than FP32 because
# we dequantize on every forward pass. The key takeaway is model size reduction.
def benchmark(model):
    import tempfile

    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as d:
        path = os.path.join(d, "model.pt")
        torch.save(model.state_dict(), path)
        size_mb = os.path.getsize(path) / (1024 * 1024)
    start = time.time()
    generate(model, 100)
    return size_mb, (time.time() - start) * 10


fp32_size, fp32_time = benchmark(model)
print(f"FP32: {fp32_size:.3f} MB, {fp32_time:.2f} ms/sample")

model_int8 = quantize_model(model)
int8_size, int8_time = benchmark(model_int8)
print(
    f"INT8: {int8_size:.3f} MB ({int8_size / fp32_size:.1%}), {int8_time:.2f} ms/sample ({int8_time / fp32_time:.1%})"
)
print("\nNote: This implementation prioritizes memory savings.")
print("Production systems use INT8 kernels for both size and speed benefits.")

for label, m in [("FP32", model), ("INT8", model_int8)]:
    torch.manual_seed(42); print(f"\n--- {label} samples ---")
    for i, s in enumerate(generate(m, 10)):
        print(f"  {i + 1:2d}: {''.join(uchars[t] for t in s)}")

19.text: Quantization changes representation, not the model's architecture, so the ordinary transformer math remains the same around the compression step.

20.text: Same shared corpus again. That keeps the lesson focused on compression and inference cost rather than on task differences.

21.text: We still control the run setup because we want to compare FP32 and INT8 behavior on equal footing.

22.text: Timing is part of the point here: quantization is valuable because smaller weights are cheaper to move through memory, not just because they save disk space.

24.text: PyTorch handles training in normal precision first; only after that do we compress the learned weights into a cheaper format.

25.text: The same familiar layers will later be wrapped or replaced with quantized versions so you can see exactly where the memory savings come from.

144.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

145.text: Instantiate the model and move it to the target device for training.

178.text: The quantized linear layer: stores weights as INT8 integers (1 byte each) instead of FP32 floats (4 bytes each). A single scale factor maps between the two representations.

182.text: Compute the scale factor: map the weight range [-max, +max] to [-127, +127]. This single scalar is the key, it determines the precision of the quantization.

183.text: Round to nearest integer and cast to INT8. Each weight now fits in 1 byte instead of 4. The register_buffer saves it as a non-parameter tensor (no gradients).

184.text: Keep the scale factor as a buffer so we can undo the quantization during inference by multiplying INT8 values back to float.

185.text: Carry over bias if the original layer had one (ours don't).

187.text: Forward: dequantize on-the-fly by casting back to float and multiplying by scale. In production, INT8 matmul kernels avoid this cast entirely, they compute directly in integer arithmetic.

188.text: Cast INT8 to float, multiply by scale, then do the matrix multiply. Mathematically: x @ (W_int8 * scale)^T. The approximation error is small because neural network weights are typically well-distributed.

191.text: Quantize the entire model: create a fresh copy and replace all Linear layers with QuantizedLinear.

labs.title: Text Diffusion
labs.tagline: Names emerge from pure noise through iterative unmasking.
labs.description:
Instead of generating left-to-right, masked diffusion starts from a sequence of all [MASK] tokens and iteratively reveals them. At each step, the model predicts what's behind every mask, and the most confident predictions are unmasked.

This is the text equivalent of image diffusion models like Stable Diffusion. The key difference from autoregressive models: the generation order is determined by model confidence, not left-to-right position. The model might unmask the middle of a name before the beginning.

The lab uses PyTorch for tensors and autograd, but with a fundamentally different training objective (predict masked tokens) and generation process (iterative unmasking with confidence-based remasking).
learning_objectives: Implement masked diffusion for non-autoregressive text generation
Compare confidence-based unmasking with left-to-right generation
Analyze the tradeoffs between diffusion and autoregressive approaches

exercises.prompt: Change the number of diffusion steps from the default to just 2 steps. How does the quality of generated names compare to using 10 or 20 steps?
exercises.hint: Fewer steps means the model must unmask more tokens per step with less confidence. Watch for garbled names when steps are too few.

exercises.prompt: Modify the unmasking schedule to always unmask from left to right instead of by confidence. Does this make the output better or worse?
exercises.hint: Replace the confidence-based selection with positional ordering. This makes it behave more like autoregressive generation but within the diffusion framework.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — Masked Diffusion Language Model (PyTorch).

Instead of generating names left-to-right like a GPT, names emerge from pure
noise -- all [MASK] tokens -- through iterative unmasking. Same transformer
architecture, fundamentally different generative paradigm.

Built on the image-domain foundation of "Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic
Models" (Ho et al., 2020), https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.11239. The discrete-text
diffusion framework follows "Simple and Effective Masked Diffusion Language
Models" (Sahoo et al., 2024), https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.07524, and "Large
Language Diffusion Models" (Nie et al., 2025), https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.09992.
The denoising schedule and confidence-based unmasking in this lab are
illustrative simplifications -- the original MDLM uses a continuous-time ELBO
with an absorbing-state forward process.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

# MASK replaces BOS, PAD handles fixed-length sequences
uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
MASK = len(uchars)  # [MASK] — the "noise" state that the model learns to denoise
PAD = len(uchars) + 1  # [PAD] — fills unused positions in fixed-length sequences
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 2
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model — bidirectional transformer (no causal mask)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 2  # number of layers (diffusion needs depth to gather scattered clues)
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length (names are padded/truncated to this)
head_dim = n_embd // n_head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class BidirectionalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    """Every position attends to every other — no causal mask."""

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        # Bidirectional: no causal mask, every position sees every other
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)

        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = BidirectionalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroDiffusion(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        # Weight tying — same matrix for input embeddings and output projection
        self.lm_head.weight = self.wte.weight
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
model = MicroDiffusion().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 3000
batch_size = 32  # diffusion needs batching — single-sample gradients are too noisy
print(f"batch size: {batch_size} (diffusion needs batching — single-sample gradients are too noisy)")

for step in range(num_steps):
    # Build a batch of (clean, noisy) pairs, each with its own random masking
    clean_batch = []
    noisy_batch = []
    mask_batch = []  # True where masked

    for b in range(batch_size):
        doc = docs[(step * batch_size + b) % len(docs)]
        clean = [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [PAD] * (block_size - len(doc))
        clean = clean[:block_size]

        # Forward process: corrupt by masking each token with probability t
        # Log-uniform t sampling: t ∝ 1/t, cancels the 1/t ELBO weight
        t = math.exp(random.uniform(math.log(0.2), 0))
        noisy = [MASK if random.random() < t else c for c in clean]
        is_masked = [n == MASK for n in noisy]

        if not any(is_masked):
            # Ensure at least one masked token for a training signal
            pos = random.randrange(block_size)
            noisy[pos] = MASK
            is_masked[pos] = True

        clean_batch.append(clean)
        noisy_batch.append(noisy)
        mask_batch.append(is_masked)

    # Forward pass — predict clean tokens from noisy input
    input_ids = torch.tensor(noisy_batch, device=device)  # (B, block_size)
    targets = torch.tensor(clean_batch, device=device)  # (B, block_size)
    mask = torch.tensor(mask_batch, device=device)  # (B, block_size)

    logits = model(input_ids)  # (B, block_size, vocab_size)
    logits[:, :, MASK] = logits[:, :, MASK] - 1e6  # never predict MASK

    # Loss only on masked positions, averaged across the batch
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits[mask], targets[mask])

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()

    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference — iterative denoising from all-MASK to clean names
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
num_denoise_steps = 64
print("\n--- inference (new, hallucinated names) ---")

model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    for sample_idx in range(20):
        seq = [MASK] * block_size  # start from pure noise

        for step_i in range(num_denoise_steps, 0, -1):
            t = math.cos(math.pi / 2 * (1 - step_i / num_denoise_steps))
            s = math.cos(math.pi / 2 * (1 - (step_i - 1) / num_denoise_steps))
            temperature = 0.3 + 0.5 * t  # explore early, commit late

            input_ids = torch.tensor([seq], device=device)
            logits = model(input_ids)[0]  # (block_size, vocab_size)

            predicted = list(seq)
            confidences = []
            for i in range(block_size):
                if seq[i] == MASK:
                    logits_i = logits[i].clone()
                    logits_i[MASK] = logits_i[MASK] - 1e6  # never predict MASK
                    probs = F.softmax(logits_i / temperature, dim=-1)
                    predicted[i] = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
                    confidences.append((probs.max().item(), i))

            # Remask least confident predictions (unless final step)
            if s > 0 and confidences:
                n_to_remask = int(len(confidences) * s / t)
                confidences.sort()
                for _, i in confidences[:n_to_remask]:
                    predicted[i] = MASK
            seq = predicted

        name = "".join(uchars[c] for c in seq if c < len(uchars))
        print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")

1.text: This lab implements a masked diffusion language model — the text equivalent of image diffusion. Instead of adding Gaussian noise to pixels, we replace characters with MASK tokens.

2.text: The model name says it all: instead of predicting the next token, this model starts from an all-MASK sequence and iteratively reveals tokens.

8.text: Two key papers: MDLM (Sahoo et al., 2024) introduced the masked diffusion framework; LLaDA (Nie et al., 2025) scaled it to large language models and showed competitive results.

18.text: Diffusion replaces left-to-right generation with a denoising process, so the math here supports both ordinary attention scaling and the noise schedule that drives unmasking.

19.text: Same names corpus as the autoregressive labs. That makes the paradigm shift easier to feel: the data is familiar, the generation process is not.

20.text: Randomness is part of the model here, not just setup noise. Diffusion repeatedly corrupts and reveals tokens, so stochasticity is part of the teaching story.

22.text: PyTorch gives us the same transformer machinery, but this lab asks it to solve a different problem: predict masked positions everywhere at once instead of next-token only.

23.text: The building blocks look familiar, which is useful because it shows how much of the transformer survives even when the generation paradigm changes completely.

24.text: The loss and softmax are ordinary; the unusual part is that the model keeps revisiting uncertain positions instead of committing left to right.

26.text: Even a stochastic denoising process should be inspectable, so we seed the run to make the unmasking trajectory reproducible.

27.text: PyTorch's RNG also needs to be pinned so the diffusion behavior you see comes from the algorithm, not a different initialization.

29.text: The setup is still standard PyTorch. The diffusion-specific behavior shows up in the masking objective and the iterative unmasking loop, not in the imports.

32.text: Builds the path to the dataset file relative to this script, one directory up in data/.

34.text: Lazy import: only load urllib if we actually need to download.

39.text: Each line becomes one document. Stripping whitespace prevents ghost tokens.

40.text: Shuffling prevents the model from memorizing alphabetical order.

41.text: Quick sanity check: we should see ~32K names.

43.text: In autoregressive models we needed BOS to start generation. In diffusion, MASK serves that role — the model starts from all-MASK and denoises. PAD is still needed for fixed-length batching.

44.text: Sorted unique characters become our vocabulary. Each character gets a deterministic token ID from 0 to n-1.

45.text: MASK is the "noise" token. In diffusion, we corrupt data by replacing real tokens with MASK, then train the model to reverse that corruption. This replaces the BOS token used in autoregressive models.

46.text: PAD fills unused positions in fixed-length sequences. Diffusion processes every position simultaneously, so all sequences must be the same length.

47.text: Two extra tokens beyond the character set: MASK and PAD.

48.text: 28 tokens: 26 lowercase letters + MASK + PAD. The small vocabulary makes it easy to see diffusion dynamics without complexity.

51.text: The model section. The transformer architecture is nearly identical to the autoregressive labs — the key difference is bidirectional attention (no causal mask).

53.text: 16-dimensional embeddings, small but enough for character-level patterns.

54.text: 4 attention heads, each seeing 4 dimensions of the 16-dim embedding.

55.text: 2 layers instead of 1 — diffusion needs more depth because it must gather clues from scattered unmasked positions, unlike autoregressive models that always have full left context.

56.text: Fixed sequence length of 16. All names are padded or truncated to fit. Diffusion processes every position in parallel.

57.text: Each head operates on a 4-dimensional slice: 16 / 4 = 4.

60.text: RMSNorm: simpler than LayerNorm, just scales by inverse root-mean-square. No learnable parameters.

65.text: The forward method normalizes by root-mean-square: simpler than LayerNorm because there is no mean subtraction or learned bias.

66.text: Compute root-mean-square and scale so vectors have roughly unit magnitude. The rsqrt fuses the division and square root for efficiency.

69.text: The key difference from GPT: bidirectional attention. Every position can see every other position — no causal mask. This lets the model gather clues from all unmasked tokens in any direction to fill in the blanks.

72.text: Constructor sets up the same Q/K/V/O projections as the autoregressive attention. The difference is purely in the forward pass: no causal mask.

74.text: Query projection: "what am I looking for?"

76.text: Value projection: the actual content that attention retrieves.

79.text: The forward pass computes multi-head attention. B=batch, T=sequence length, C=embedding dimension.

81.text: Reshape Q into (batch, heads, seq_len, head_dim) so each head attends independently.

82.text: Keys and values get the same (B, heads, T, head_dim) reshape so all three tensors align for batched attention.

86.text: Scaled dot-product attention. No causal mask here — this is the key difference from GPT. Every position sees every other.

87.text: Softmax over all positions gives attention weights. In GPT, future positions would be masked to -inf here.

89.text: Weighted sum of values, then reshape back to (batch, seq_len, n_embd).

93.text: Standard two-layer feed-forward network, identical to the autoregressive labs. The MLP processes each position independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

96.text: Expand to 4x width — the "thinking space" of the MLP.

100.text: ReLU between the two linear layers creates the nonlinearity that lets the network learn complex patterns.

103.text: Transformer block: attention then MLP, each with pre-norm and residual connections. Same architecture as autoregressive labs — only the attention mask changes.

106.text: Pre-norm: normalize before each sublayer for stable training.

107.text: Bidirectional attention instead of the causal attention used in GPT.

108.text: Second pre-norm before the MLP sublayer.

112.text: Attention sublayer with residual connection. Gradients flow through both the attention path and the skip connection.

113.text: MLP sublayer with residual connection. Same pattern: normalize, transform, add back.

117.text: The full model: a bidirectional transformer that predicts clean tokens from masked input.

120.text: Token embeddings: each of the 28 tokens (26 chars + MASK + PAD) gets a 16-dim vector.

121.text: Position embeddings: each of the 16 positions gets its own vector so the model knows where tokens sit.

122.text: Normalize the combined embeddings before the transformer layers, same stabilization as the autoregressive model.

123.text: Stack of transformer blocks. More layers let the model reason across longer distances in the masked sequence.

124.text: Output projection: maps hidden states to vocabulary logits.

126.text: Weight tying: the same matrix that embeds input tokens also projects outputs to logits. Halves embedding parameters and creates a consistent token space.

127.text: Walk all submodules and apply the weight initialization so training starts from a well-scaled random state.

132.text: Small std (0.08) prevents exploding activations at initialization.

134.text: Forward pass: embed tokens and positions, normalize, pass through transformer layers, project to vocabulary logits. Identical to autoregressive forward except attention is bidirectional.

136.text: Look up token embeddings for the input sequence.

137.text: Add position embeddings so the model knows where each token sits.

138.text: Normalize the combined embedding before feeding into the transformer layers.

139.text: Pass through each transformer layer sequentially. Each layer refines the representation using bidirectional attention across all positions.

141.text: Project every position to vocabulary logits using the tied embedding matrix.

145.text: Training section. The loop structure is different from autoregressive: each step randomly masks a batch, predicts the masked tokens, and only backpropagates through masked positions.

147.text: CPU is sufficient for this tiny model. The focus is on understanding the diffusion algorithm, not raw speed.

148.text: Instantiate the model on CPU. For this tiny model, CPU is plenty fast.

149.text: Print the parameter count so you can verify this is a tiny model — same order of magnitude as the autoregressive labs.

151.text: Adam optimizer with the same hyperparameters as other labs.

152.text: 3000 training steps. Diffusion needs more iterations than autoregressive models because each step only trains on randomly masked positions.

153.text: Batch size of 32 is critical for diffusion. With batch=1, each gradient is based on a single random masking pattern — far too noisy for the model to learn. Batching averages over 32 different mask patterns per step, giving stable gradients.

154.text: Print batch size so it's visible in the output — a reminder that batching is essential for diffusion.

156.text: Each training step: build a batch of masked sequences, predict the masked tokens, compute loss only on masked positions. This is the core diffusion training loop.

158.text: Three parallel lists accumulate the batch: clean sequences (targets), noisy sequences (inputs), and mask flags (where to compute loss).

162.text: Build each example independently: tokenize a name, corrupt it with random masking, record which positions were masked.

163.text: Cycle through documents, advancing by batch_size each step to see different names.

164.text: Tokenize the name and pad with PAD tokens to fill the fixed-length sequence.

165.text: Truncate if the name is longer than block_size.

167.text: The forward diffusion process: corrupt clean tokens by randomly masking them. The noise level t controls how much corruption to apply.

169.text: Sample noise level t from a log-uniform distribution between 0.2 and 1.0. Log-uniform means t ∝ 1/t, which cancels the 1/t factor in the ELBO loss — a clever importance sampling trick from MDLM.

170.text: Corrupt the clean sequence: each token is independently replaced with MASK with probability t. High t → heavy corruption (mostly MASK), low t → light (mostly clean).

171.text: Record which positions were masked — we'll only compute loss on these.

173.text: Edge case: if nothing got masked, force at least one mask so there's a training signal.

179.text: Append the clean, noisy, and mask data for this example to the batch lists.

183.text: Forward pass: feed the entire batch of noisy sequences through the model in one shot.

184.text: Stack all noisy sequences into a batch tensor.

186.text: Boolean mask tensor: True where the token was masked.

188.text: Forward the entire batch through the bidirectional model in one pass.

189.text: Suppress MASK in predictions by subtracting a huge number from its logit. The model should predict real tokens, never MASK itself. This is the "subs" parameterization from MDLM.

192.text: Cross-entropy loss only on masked positions. The boolean mask indexes into both logits and targets, selecting just the positions the model should reconstruct. PyTorch averages automatically.

194.text: Clear gradients from the previous step before backpropagation.

195.text: Backpropagate: compute gradients for every parameter.

196.text: Linear learning rate decay: start fast, end slow.

199.text: Apply the computed gradients to update model weights one step toward lower loss.

201.text: Log every 10 steps so we can track convergence. Loss should decrease from ~3.3 (random) toward ~1.5 (learning patterns).

204.text: Inference section. The trained model generates names by starting from all-MASK and iteratively unmasking positions based on confidence.

207.text: 64 denoising steps: each step unmasks a few more positions, gradually revealing the name from pure noise.

208.text: Print a header to separate training output from generated samples.

210.text: Switch to eval mode and disable gradient tracking for inference.

211.text: Disable gradient tracking during inference — we're only sampling, not training.

212.text: Generate 20 sample names, each starting from pure noise and iteratively crystallizing through confidence-based unmasking.

213.text: Start from pure noise: every position is MASK. The name doesn't exist yet — it will crystallize through iterative unmasking.

215.text: Count down from full noise to clean. Each step reduces the noise level.

216.text: Cosine noise schedule: more time is spent at intermediate noise levels where the interesting decisions happen.

217.text: Next noise level (one step less noisy). The gap between t and s determines how many tokens to unmask this step.

218.text: Temperature annealing: explore broadly early on (0.8) when the model is uncertain, commit to specific choices later (0.3) as the name takes shape.

220.text: Wrap the current sequence as a batch-of-one tensor for the model forward pass.

221.text: Get the model's predictions for all positions in one bidirectional forward pass.

223.text: Copy the current sequence. Masked positions will be overwritten with model predictions; unmasked positions stay as-is.

224.text: Track how confident the model is at each masked position. After predicting, the least-confident positions get re-masked for another try.

226.text: Only predict for currently masked positions — unmasked tokens are already decided.

228.text: Suppress MASK in predictions, same trick as training.

229.text: Apply temperature scaling before sampling. Low temperature sharpens the distribution, high temperature flattens it.

230.text: Sample a token from the probability distribution. Unlike argmax, this preserves diversity.

231.text: Record the model's confidence (max probability) alongside the position — high confidence means the model is sure.

234.text: If we haven't reached the final step, re-mask the least confident predictions to give them another chance.

235.text: How many to re-mask: proportional to remaining noise ratio s/t. As noise decreases, fewer tokens get re-masked.

236.text: Sort by confidence (ascending) so the least confident predictions are first — these get re-masked.

238.text: Put MASK back — this position will get another try in the next denoising step.

239.text: Update the sequence with the (partially re-masked) predictions for the next iteration.

241.text: Decode: convert token IDs back to characters, skipping any PAD tokens.

242.text: Print each generated name — these emerged from pure noise through iterative unmasking, with no left-to-right constraint.

labs.title: Speculative Decoding
labs.tagline: A small draft model guesses ahead, a larger target model verifies.
labs.description:
Speculative decoding uses two models: a small, fast draft model proposes several tokens at once, then the large target model verifies them all in a single forward pass.

The key insight: verifying N tokens in parallel costs about the same as generating 1 token (because the target model processes them as a batch). If the draft model guesses correctly most of the time, you get N tokens for the cost of ~1. When it guesses wrong, you reject and fall back — the output distribution is mathematically identical to the target model alone.

This is a lossless speedup: same output quality, fewer forward passes. Used in production by vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and others.
learning_objectives: Implement draft-and-verify speculative decoding
Prove that speculative decoding produces identical output distributions
Analyze the speedup as a function of draft model acceptance rate

exercises.prompt: Increase the number of draft tokens from the default to double and triple. At what point does the acceptance rate drop enough to negate the speedup?
exercises.hint: More draft tokens means more speculative work, but the probability of all being accepted decreases exponentially. Track accepted/proposed ratio.

exercises.prompt: Make the draft model intentionally worse by reducing its size. At what point is speculative decoding slower than just using the target model alone?
exercises.hint: A very small draft model has low acceptance rates. Measure total forward passes for both approaches to find the crossover point.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — Speculative decoding edition.

Same architecture as the PyTorch version (03/16), but demonstrating
speculative decoding: a small "draft" model guesses multiple tokens ahead,
then the larger "target" model verifies them all in a single forward pass.
The output distribution is mathematically identical to the target model alone.

This is the #1 technique used in production inference systems (vLLM,
TensorRT-LLM, SGLang) because autoregressive decoding is memory-bound —
each token requires reading all model weights but does very little compute.
"""

import math
import os
import random
import time

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model — two sizes: a small draft model and a larger target model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_embd, n_head):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_head = n_head
        self.head_dim = n_embd // n_head
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, self.n_head, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, self.n_head, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, self.n_head, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)
        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_embd):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_embd, n_head):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention(n_embd, n_head)
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP(n_embd)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_embd, n_head, n_layer):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block(n_embd, n_head) for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"

# Draft model: small, fast, less accurate
draft_cfg = {"n_embd": 32, "n_head": 4, "n_layer": 1}
# Target model: larger, slower, better quality
target_cfg = {"n_embd": 64, "n_head": 4, "n_layer": 2}


def train_model(model, name, num_steps=1000):
    print(f"\n--- training {name} ({sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())} params) ---")
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
    for step in range(num_steps):
        doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
        tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)
        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
        targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)
        logits = model(input_ids)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
        for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
            pg["lr"] = lr_t
        optimizer.step()
        if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
            print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")


draft_model = MicroGPT(**draft_cfg).to(device)
target_model = MicroGPT(**target_cfg).to(device)

train_model(draft_model, "draft")
train_model(target_model, "target")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference methods
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high


@torch.no_grad()
def get_probs(model, tokens):
    """Get probability distribution for the next token(s) given a token sequence."""
    idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
    logits = model(idx)[0] / temperature  # (T, V)
    return F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)  # (T, V)


# --- 1) Naive autoregressive decoding (baseline) ---


def generate_autoregressive(model, max_len=block_size):
    """Standard decoding: one token at a time, one forward pass per token."""
    tokens = [BOS]
    with torch.no_grad():
        for _ in range(max_len):
            probs = get_probs(model, tokens)[-1]  # last position
            token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
    return tokens[1:]  # strip BOS


# --- 2) Speculative decoding ---


def generate_speculative(draft, target, max_len=block_size, K=4):
    """
    Speculative decoding: the draft model proposes K tokens, the target
    model verifies them all in one forward pass.

    The acceptance/rejection algorithm guarantees the output distribution
    is IDENTICAL to target-only generation:

    For each drafted token at position i with draft probability q(x):
      - Target model gives probability p(x) for the same token
      - Accept with probability min(1, p(x) / q(x))
      - On rejection: sample from adjusted distribution max(0, p(x) - q(x))
        (normalized), which corrects for the draft model's bias

    This is mathematically proven to sample from p(x) exactly.

    This technique was introduced in "Fast Inference from Transformers via
    Speculative Decoding" by Leviathan, Kalman, and Matias (2023),
    available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.17192. A concurrent and closely
    related approach was presented in "Accelerating Large Language Model
    Decoding with Speculative Sampling" by Chen, Borgeaud, Irving et al.
    (2023), available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.01318. The implementation
    here faithfully follows the accept/reject algorithm from Leviathan et al.
    (2023), which provably preserves the target model's output distribution.
    """
    tokens = [BOS]
    stats = {"drafted": 0, "accepted": 0, "target_fwd": 0, "draft_fwd": 0}

    with torch.no_grad():
        while len(tokens) - 1 < max_len:
            # Step 1: Draft model generates K candidate tokens
            draft_tokens = list(tokens)
            draft_probs_list = []  # (token_id, q(x), full distribution) for each drafted token
            for _ in range(K):
                if len(draft_tokens) - 1 >= max_len:
                    break
                probs_d = get_probs(draft, draft_tokens)[-1]
                stats["draft_fwd"] += 1
                token_id = torch.multinomial(probs_d, 1).item()
                draft_probs_list.append((token_id, probs_d[token_id].item(), probs_d))
                draft_tokens.append(token_id)

            if not draft_probs_list:
                break

            stats["drafted"] += len(draft_probs_list)

            # Step 2: Target model scores ALL positions in one forward pass
            # This is the key efficiency win: one forward pass instead of K
            target_probs = get_probs(target, draft_tokens)  # (T, V)
            stats["target_fwd"] += 1

            # Step 3: Accept/reject each drafted token
            n_accepted = 0
            for i, (drafted_token, q_x, draft_dist) in enumerate(draft_probs_list):
                # Target model's probability for the drafted token
                # Position in target_probs: tokens before draft + i
                pos = len(tokens) - 1 + i
                if pos >= target_probs.shape[0]:
                    break
                p_x = target_probs[pos, drafted_token].item()

                # Accept with probability min(1, p(x) / q(x))
                if q_x == 0:
                    break
                accept_prob = min(1.0, p_x / q_x)

                if random.random() < accept_prob:
                    # Accept this token
                    tokens.append(drafted_token)
                    n_accepted += 1
                    if drafted_token == BOS:
                        tokens.pop()  # remove BOS, end generation
                        stats["accepted"] += n_accepted - 1
                        return tokens[1:], stats
                else:
                    # Reject: sample from adjusted distribution max(0, p - q)
                    pos_probs = target_probs[pos]
                    adjusted = torch.clamp(pos_probs - draft_dist, min=0)
                    adj_sum = adjusted.sum()
                    if adj_sum > 0:
                        adjusted = adjusted / adj_sum
                    else:
                        adjusted = pos_probs
                    token_id = torch.multinomial(adjusted, 1).item()
                    if token_id == BOS:
                        stats["accepted"] += n_accepted
                        return tokens[1:], stats
                    tokens.append(token_id)
                    break  # restart drafting from the new position

            else:
                # All K tokens accepted — sample one bonus token from target
                bonus_pos = len(tokens) - 1
                if bonus_pos < target_probs.shape[0]:
                    bonus_probs = target_probs[bonus_pos]
                    token_id = torch.multinomial(bonus_probs, 1).item()
                    if token_id != BOS:
                        tokens.append(token_id)

            stats["accepted"] += n_accepted

    return tokens[1:], stats


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Compare the three approaches
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- speculative decoding comparison ---\n")

draft_model.eval()
target_model.eval()
n_samples = 50


def timed_generate(name, gen_fn):
    print(f"generating {n_samples} samples with {name}...")
    start = time.time()
    samples = [gen_fn() for _ in range(n_samples)]
    elapsed = time.time() - start
    print(f"  time: {elapsed:.3f}s ({elapsed / n_samples * 1000:.1f} ms/sample)")
    return samples, elapsed


auto_samples, auto_time = timed_generate(
    "autoregressive decoding (target)", lambda: generate_autoregressive(target_model)
)

# Speculative: also collect accept/reject stats
total_stats = {"drafted": 0, "accepted": 0, "target_fwd": 0, "draft_fwd": 0}
spec_results = []

spec_samples, spec_time = timed_generate(
    "speculative decoding (K=4)",
    lambda: spec_results.append(generate_speculative(draft_model, target_model, K=4)) or spec_results[-1][0],
)
for _, stats in spec_results:
    for k in total_stats:
        total_stats[k] += stats[k]

draft_samples, draft_time = timed_generate(
    "autoregressive decoding (draft)", lambda: generate_autoregressive(draft_model)
)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Results
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
acceptance_rate = total_stats["accepted"] / max(total_stats["drafted"], 1)
avg_target_fwd = total_stats["target_fwd"] / n_samples
avg_draft_fwd = total_stats["draft_fwd"] / n_samples
avg_auto_tokens = sum(len(s) for s in auto_samples) / n_samples
avg_spec_tokens = sum(len(s) for s in spec_samples) / n_samples

print("\n--- results ---\n")
print(f"acceptance rate: {acceptance_rate:.1%} (of draft tokens accepted by target)")
print(f"avg target forward passes: {avg_target_fwd:.1f} (speculative) vs {avg_auto_tokens:.1f} (autoregressive)")
print(f"avg draft forward passes:  {avg_draft_fwd:.1f}")
print(f"speedup: {auto_time / spec_time:.2f}x (speculative vs autoregressive)")
print(f"avg tokens generated: {avg_auto_tokens:.1f} (auto) vs {avg_spec_tokens:.1f} (spec)")

print("""
--- why this matters ---

Autoregressive decoding is memory-bound: each token requires reading ALL model
weights from memory, but only computes a single vector-matrix product per layer.
The GPU's compute units sit mostly idle, waiting for data from slow memory.

Speculative decoding exploits this: the target model can verify K tokens in
nearly the same time as generating 1, because the bottleneck is reading weights
(which happens once regardless of sequence length), not computing attention.

In production (7B-70B models on GPUs), speculative decoding achieves:
  - 2-3x speedup with a well-matched draft model
  - Higher acceptance rates with better draft models (EAGLE, Medusa)
  - Zero quality loss — the output distribution is mathematically identical

At our tiny scale, the overhead of Python loops can mask the benefit.
On real hardware with large models, this is transformative.
""")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Show sample outputs
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("--- sample outputs ---\n")
for label, samples in [
    ("autoregressive (target)", auto_samples),
    ("speculative decoding", spec_samples),
    ("draft model only", draft_samples),
]:
    print(f"{label}:")
    for i, s in enumerate(samples[:10]):
        print(f"  {i + 1:2d}: {''.join(uchars[t] for t in s)}")
    print()

14.text: Speculative decoding keeps ordinary attention math but changes the control flow of inference: guess several tokens, then verify them in bulk.

15.text: Same names task, so the speedup story is about decoding strategy rather than about a different workload.

17.text: Timing is central here: speculative decoding only matters if one target-model pass can replace many expensive autoregressive passes.
