labs.title: Tiled Attention (FlashAttention)
labs.tagline: Three attention implementations producing identical outputs. The difference is memory trips.
labs.description:
FlashAttention restructures the attention computation to minimize memory transfers between fast SRAM and slow HBM (main GPU memory).

Standard attention materializes the full N×N attention matrix in HBM — that's O(N²) memory. FlashAttention processes attention in tiles that fit in SRAM, accumulating results with online softmax (the log-sum-exp trick). The output is mathematically identical, but memory usage drops from O(N²) to O(N).

This lab implements three versions — naive, standard, and tiled — so you can verify they produce the same output while understanding why the tiled version is faster on real hardware.
learning_objectives: Implement naive, standard, and tiled (FlashAttention) attention
Explain the online softmax trick using log-sum-exp
Analyze why tiled attention reduces memory from O(N^2) to O(N)

exercises.prompt: Change the tile size in the tiled attention implementation. What is the smallest tile that still produces numerically identical output to standard attention?
exercises.hint: Try tile sizes of 1, 2, 4, 8, etc. Compare outputs using np.allclose with a tight tolerance. Tile size affects performance but not correctness.

exercises.prompt: Add a counter to track the number of memory reads and writes in each attention implementation. How do the counts compare between naive and tiled?
exercises.hint: Count each array access as a memory operation. Tiled attention does more total arithmetic but fewer memory round-trips, which is faster on real hardware.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — Tiled attention edition.

Same architecture as the PyTorch version (03), but demonstrating three
attention implementations for inference: standard, online-softmax, and
tiled (FlashAttention algorithm). All three produce identical outputs;
the difference is how many "slow memory" (HBM) operations they need.

Training uses PyTorch; the attention implementations are in explicit NumPy
to make every memory access visible and countable.

The tiled algorithm implements the core idea from "FlashAttention: Fast and
Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness" (Dao et al., 2022),
https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.14135. The online softmax technique used within
tiles is from "Online normalizer calculation for softmax" (Milakov &
Gimelshein, 2018), https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.02867. Also see
"FlashAttention-2: Faster Attention with Better Parallelism and Work
Partitioning" (Dao, 2023), https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.08691. Note that this
is a pedagogical NumPy implementation of the algorithmic idea -- the real
FlashAttention is a fused CUDA kernel that exploits GPU SRAM directly. The
math is identical; the performance difference comes from hardware-level
memory management.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)
torch.set_default_dtype(torch.float64)  # Intentional for this standalone lab (float64 needed for numerical accuracy demos)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    urllib.request.urlretrieve(
        "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt", input_path
    )

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS, vocab_size = len(uchars), len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (PyTorch — identical architecture to Lab 03)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd, n_head, n_layer, block_size = 16, 4, 1, 16
head_dim = n_embd // n_head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        out = (F.softmax(att, dim=-1) @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(self.norm2(x))))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        x = self.norm_in(self.wte(idx) + self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device)))
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
model = MicroGPT()
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)
    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]])
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]])
    logits = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = lr_t
    optimizer.step()
    if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Extract trained weights to NumPy for attention experiments
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training used PyTorch for convenience. The attention implementations below
# are in explicit NumPy so every memory access is visible and countable.
sd = {k: v.detach().numpy() for k, v in model.state_dict().items()}
P = {"wte": sd["wte.weight"], "wpe": sd["wpe.weight"], "lm_head": sd["lm_head.weight"]}
for i in range(n_layer):
    for w in ("wq", "wk", "wv", "wo"):
        P[f"l{i}.{w}"] = sd[f"layers.{i}.attn.{w}.weight"].T
    for w in ("fc1", "fc2"):
        P[f"l{i}.{w}"] = sd[f"layers.{i}.{w}.weight"].T


def softmax_np(logits):
    exps = np.exp(logits - logits.max(axis=-1, keepdims=True))
    return exps / exps.sum(axis=-1, keepdims=True)


def rmsnorm_np(x):
    return x / np.sqrt(np.mean(x**2, axis=-1, keepdims=True) + 1e-5)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Three attention implementations — the core of this lab
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- comparing attention implementations ---\n")


def inference_forward(token_ids, attn_fn):
    """Forward pass using a pluggable attention function (all NumPy)."""
    n = len(token_ids)
    last_stats = {}
    x = rmsnorm_np(P["wte"][token_ids] + P["wpe"][np.arange(n)])
    for li in range(n_layer):
        x_res = x.copy()
        x_n = rmsnorm_np(x)
        Q_h, K_h, V_h = (
            (x_n @ P[f"l{li}.{w}"]).reshape(n, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 0, 2) for w in ("wq", "wk", "wv")
        )
        attn_out, last_stats = attn_fn(Q_h, K_h, V_h, n)
        x = attn_out.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(n, n_embd) @ P[f"l{li}.wo"] + x_res
        x_res = x.copy()
        x_n = rmsnorm_np(x)
        h = x_n @ P[f"l{li}.fc1"]
        x = np.maximum(h, 0) @ P[f"l{li}.fc2"] + x_res
    return x @ P["lm_head"].T, last_stats


# --- 1) Standard attention: materialize the full NxN matrix ---


def standard_attention(Q, K, V, seq_len):
    """
    Classic attention: compute the full attention matrix, write it to memory.
    On a real GPU this writes the NxN matrix to HBM (slow main memory).
    """
    stats = {"hbm_reads": 0, "hbm_writes": 0}
    stats["hbm_reads"] += 2 * n_head * seq_len * head_dim
    att = Q @ K.transpose(0, 2, 1) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
    stats["hbm_writes"] += n_head * seq_len * seq_len
    causal = np.triu(np.ones((seq_len, seq_len)), k=1).astype(bool)
    att = np.where(causal, -1e9, att)
    stats["hbm_reads"] += n_head * seq_len * seq_len
    att_probs = softmax_np(att)
    stats["hbm_writes"] += n_head * seq_len * seq_len
    stats["hbm_reads"] += n_head * seq_len * seq_len + n_head * seq_len * head_dim
    out = att_probs @ V
    stats["hbm_writes"] += n_head * seq_len * head_dim
    return out, stats


# --- 2) Online softmax attention: never store the full NxN matrix ---


def online_softmax_attention(Q, K, V, seq_len):
    """
    Online softmax (Milakov & Gimelshein, 2018): compute attention row by row
    without ever materializing the full NxN attention matrix.

    This is the algorithmic foundation of FlashAttention.
    """
    stats = {"hbm_reads": 0, "hbm_writes": 0}
    out = np.zeros_like(Q)

    for h in range(n_head):
        for i in range(seq_len):
            q_i = Q[h, i]
            stats["hbm_reads"] += head_dim

            running_max = -np.inf
            running_sum = 0.0
            running_out = np.zeros(head_dim)

            for j in range(i + 1):  # causal: only attend to positions <= i
                k_j = K[h, j]
                v_j = V[h, j]
                stats["hbm_reads"] += 2 * head_dim

                score = np.dot(q_i, k_j) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
                new_max = max(running_max, score)
                correction = math.exp(running_max - new_max) if running_max != -np.inf else 0.0
                running_sum = running_sum * correction + math.exp(score - new_max)
                running_out = running_out * correction + math.exp(score - new_max) * v_j
                running_max = new_max

            out[h, i] = running_out / running_sum
            stats["hbm_writes"] += head_dim

    return out, stats


# --- 3) Tiled attention (FlashAttention algorithm) ---


def tiled_attention(Q, K, V, seq_len, block_size_tile=4):
    """
    FlashAttention: tile the computation into blocks.

    Instead of processing one key at a time (online softmax) or the full
    matrix (standard), process BLOCKS of keys at once. Each block fits in
    fast on-chip memory (SRAM).
    """
    stats = {"hbm_reads": 0, "hbm_writes": 0}
    Bc = block_size_tile
    Br = block_size_tile
    out = np.zeros_like(Q)

    for h in range(n_head):
        for i_start in range(0, seq_len, Br):
            i_end = min(i_start + Br, seq_len)
            Qi = Q[h, i_start:i_end]
            block_rows = i_end - i_start
            stats["hbm_reads"] += block_rows * head_dim

            row_max = np.full(block_rows, -np.inf)
            row_sum = np.zeros(block_rows)
            row_out = np.zeros((block_rows, head_dim))

            max_j = i_end  # causal: only attend up to current position
            for j_start in range(0, max_j, Bc):
                j_end = min(j_start + Bc, max_j)
                Kj = K[h, j_start:j_end]
                Vj = V[h, j_start:j_end]
                block_cols = j_end - j_start
                stats["hbm_reads"] += 2 * block_cols * head_dim

                scores = Qi @ Kj.T / math.sqrt(head_dim)

                # Apply causal mask within this block
                for bi in range(block_rows):
                    for bj in range(block_cols):
                        if (j_start + bj) > (i_start + bi):
                            scores[bi, bj] = -1e9

                # Online softmax update across blocks
                block_max = scores.max(axis=-1)
                new_max = np.maximum(row_max, block_max)
                old_correction = np.exp(row_max - new_max)
                exp_scores = np.exp(scores - new_max[:, None])

                row_sum = row_sum * old_correction + exp_scores.sum(axis=-1)
                row_out = row_out * old_correction[:, None] + exp_scores @ Vj
                row_max = new_max

            out[h, i_start:i_end] = row_out / row_sum[:, None]
            stats["hbm_writes"] += block_rows * head_dim

    return out, stats


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Compare all three on the same input
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
sample_tokens = np.array([BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in docs[0]])[:block_size]
seq_len = len(sample_tokens)

logits_std, stats_std = inference_forward(sample_tokens, standard_attention)
logits_online, stats_online = inference_forward(sample_tokens, online_softmax_attention)
logits_tiled, stats_tiled = inference_forward(sample_tokens, tiled_attention)

diffs = [
    ("online softmax", np.max(np.abs(logits_std - logits_online)), 1e-10),
    ("tiled", np.max(np.abs(logits_std - logits_tiled)), 1e-6),
]
print(f"sequence length: {seq_len}")
for label, diff, tol in diffs:
    print(f"max diff (standard vs {label + '):':<16s} {diff:.2e}")
    assert diff < tol, f"{label} diverged: {diff}"
print("All three produce identical output (within floating-point tolerance).\n")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Memory operation comparison
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("--- memory operation counts (HBM reads + writes) ---\n")

for name, stats, note in [
    ("Standard attention", stats_std, "writes N*N attention matrix to HBM, reads it back for softmax"),
    ("Online softmax", stats_online, "never stores N*N matrix (processes one key at a time)"),
    (
        "Tiled (FlashAttention, block_size=4)",
        stats_tiled,
        "reads K/V in blocks (amortizes HBM access), vectorized compute",
    ),
]:
    r, w = stats["hbm_reads"], stats["hbm_writes"]
    print(f"{name}:\n  HBM reads: {r:>6d}  writes: {w:>6d}  total: {r + w:>6d}")
    print(f"  Key insight: {note}\n")

N, d, nh = seq_len, head_dim, n_head
print(
    f"Summary:\n"
    f"  Across all heads, standard writes {2 * nh * N * N:>5d} score/probability elements (O(n_head*N^2))\n"
    f"  Across all heads, tiled and online write only {nh * N * d:>5d} output elements (O(n_head*N*d))\n"
    f"  Per head at N=2048, d=128: 2*N^2 = {2 * 2048 * 2048 / 1e6:.1f}M vs N*d = {2048 * 128 / 1024:.0f}K elements"
)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# GPU memory hierarchy context
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("""
--- GPU memory hierarchy (why this matters) ---

  Level         Size        Bandwidth       What lives here
  ──────────    ────────    ────────────    ───────────────────────────
  Registers     ~few KB     fastest         Current computation
  SRAM (L1)     ~20 MB      ~19 TB/s       FlashAttention keeps Q,K,V blocks here
  HBM (VRAM)    ~80 GB      ~2 TB/s        Standard attention writes N*N matrix here
  CPU DRAM      ~TBs        ~50 GB/s        Model doesn't fit in GPU? Swap here

Standard attention writes the full N*N attention matrix to HBM — 10x slower
memory. FlashAttention tiles the computation so it stays in SRAM, never
materializing the full matrix. Same math, ~2-4x faster on real hardware.""")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference — generate names using tiled attention
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
print("\n--- inference (tiled attention) ---")
for sample_idx in range(20):
    generated = [BOS]
    sample = []
    for _ in range(block_size):
        logits, _ = inference_forward(np.array(generated), tiled_attention)
        logits = logits[-1] / temperature
        probs = softmax_np(logits.reshape(1, -1))[0]
        token_id = rng.choice(vocab_size, p=probs)
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        generated.append(token_id)
        sample.append(uchars[token_id])
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {''.join(sample)}")

labs.title: Paged KV Cache (PagedAttention)
labs.tagline: Virtual memory paging applied to KV caches.
labs.description:
PagedAttention applies the operating system's virtual memory concept to KV caches. Instead of allocating one contiguous block per sequence, KV data is stored in fixed-size pages that can be anywhere in physical memory.

This solves the fragmentation problem: when sequences have different lengths and lifetimes, contiguous allocation wastes memory on internal fragmentation. Paging eliminates this waste, enabling ~2-4× more concurrent sequences in the same memory budget.

This is the core innovation behind vLLM. The lab implements it with the same pure-Python scalar autograd as Lab 01, making the memory management logic explicit and inspectable.
learning_objectives: Implement virtual memory paging for KV cache management
Analyze memory fragmentation in contiguous vs. paged allocation
Explain how PagedAttention enables 2-4x more concurrent sequences

exercises.prompt: Change the page size from the default to half and double. How does this affect memory fragmentation and the number of page table lookups?
exercises.hint: Smaller pages reduce internal fragmentation but increase page table overhead. Print the total allocated vs. actually used memory for each page size.

exercises.prompt: Simulate 10 concurrent sequences of varying lengths, then free half of them. Print the memory map before and after freeing to visualize fragmentation.
exercises.hint: Track which pages are allocated to which sequences. After freeing, contiguous allocation leaves gaps while paged allocation can reuse any freed page.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — PagedAttention edition.

Same architecture as the pure-Python version (01), but demonstrating two
KV cache strategies for inference: contiguous (wasteful) and paged (efficient).
PagedAttention is the core innovation in vLLM -- it applies the operating
system's virtual memory paging concept to KV caches, achieving near-perfect
memory utilization.

Zero dependencies. Pure Python. The algorithms are pure data structures.

Based on "Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with
PagedAttention" (Kwon et al., 2023), https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.06180, the
core innovation behind vLLM. The concept directly parallels virtual memory
paging in operating systems, as described in standard texts like "Operating
System Concepts" (Silberschatz et al.). This lab implements the memory
management side of PagedAttention (block tables, on-demand allocation,
copy-on-write prefix sharing) -- not the fused PagedAttention CUDA attention
kernel itself. The pure-Python scalar implementation makes the data structure
logic fully transparent.
"""

import math
import os
import random

random.seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model — reuse Lab 01's Value autograd + GPT architecture
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head


# fmt: off
class Value:
    __slots__ = ('_children', '_local_grads', 'data', 'grad') # Python optimization for memory usage

    def __init__(self, data, children=(), local_grads=()):
        self.data = data                # scalar value of this node calculated during forward pass
        self.grad = 0                   # derivative of the loss w.r.t. this node, calculated in backward pass
        self._children = children       # children of this node in the computation graph
        self._local_grads = local_grads # local derivative of this node w.r.t. its children

    def __add__(self, other):
        other = other if isinstance(other, Value) else Value(other)
        return Value(self.data + other.data, (self, other), (1, 1))

    def __mul__(self, other):
        other = other if isinstance(other, Value) else Value(other)
        return Value(self.data * other.data, (self, other), (other.data, self.data))

    def __pow__(self, other): return Value(self.data**other, (self,), (other * self.data**(other-1),))
    def log(self): return Value(math.log(self.data), (self,), (1/self.data,))
    def exp(self): return Value(math.exp(self.data), (self,), (math.exp(self.data),))
    def relu(self): return Value(max(0, self.data), (self,), (float(self.data > 0),))
    def __neg__(self): return self * -1
    def __radd__(self, other): return self + other
    def __sub__(self, other): return self + (-other)
    def __rsub__(self, other): return other + (-self)
    def __rmul__(self, other): return self * other
    def __truediv__(self, other): return self * other**-1
    def __rtruediv__(self, other): return other * self**-1

    def backward(self):
        topo = []
        visited = set()
        def build_topo(v):
            if v not in visited:
                visited.add(v)
                for child in v._children:
                    build_topo(child)
                topo.append(v)
        build_topo(self)
        self.grad = 1
        for v in reversed(topo):
            for child, local_grad in zip(v._children, v._local_grads):
                child.grad += local_grad * v.grad


def matrix(nout, nin, std=0.08):
    return [[Value(random.gauss(0, std)) for _ in range(nin)] for _ in range(nout)]


state_dict = {
    "wte": matrix(vocab_size, n_embd),
    "wpe": matrix(block_size, n_embd),
    "lm_head": matrix(vocab_size, n_embd),
}
for i in range(n_layer):
    for name in ("attn_wq", "attn_wk", "attn_wv", "attn_wo"):
        state_dict[f"layer{i}.{name}"] = matrix(n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f"layer{i}.mlp_fc1"] = matrix(4 * n_embd, n_embd)
    state_dict[f"layer{i}.mlp_fc2"] = matrix(n_embd, 4 * n_embd)
params = [p for mat in state_dict.values() for row in mat for p in row]
print(f"num params: {len(params)}")


def linear(x, w):
    return [sum(wi * xi for wi, xi in zip(wo, x)) for wo in w]


def softmax(logits):
    if isinstance(logits[0], Value):
        max_val = max(val.data for val in logits)
        exps = [(val - max_val).exp() for val in logits]
    else:
        max_val = max(logits)
        exps = [math.exp(l - max_val) for l in logits]
    total = sum(exps)
    return [e / total for e in exps]


def rmsnorm(x):
    scale = (sum(xi * xi for xi in x) / len(x) + 1e-5) ** -0.5
    return [xi * scale for xi in x]


def relu(x):
    if isinstance(x[0], Value):
        return [xi.relu() for xi in x]
    return [max(0.0, xi) for xi in x]


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# KV Cache Managers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


class ContiguousKVCache:
    """Standard KV cache: pre-allocate max_seq_len slots per layer."""

    def __init__(self, max_seq_len, n_layers, dims):
        self.max_seq_len = max_seq_len
        self.n_layers = n_layers
        self.dims = dims
        self.k_cache = [[[0.0] * dims for _ in range(max_seq_len)] for _ in range(n_layers)]
        self.v_cache = [[[0.0] * dims for _ in range(max_seq_len)] for _ in range(n_layers)]
        self.length = 0
        self.allocated = n_layers * max_seq_len * dims * 2
        self.label = "contiguous"

    def new_sequence(self, seq_id=0):
        self.length = 0

    def append(self, seq_id, layer, k, v):
        self.k_cache[layer][self.length] = k
        self.v_cache[layer][self.length] = v
        if layer == self.n_layers - 1:
            self.length += 1

    def get_kv(self, seq_id, layer):
        return self.k_cache[layer][: self.length], self.v_cache[layer][: self.length]

    def utilization(self):
        used = self.length * self.n_layers * self.dims * 2
        return used / self.allocated if self.allocated > 0 else 0


BLOCK_SIZE_TOKENS = 4


class PagedKVCache:
    """
    Paged KV cache (PagedAttention / vLLM concept).
    Allocate fixed-size blocks on demand. A block table maps logical positions
    to physical block IDs — just like page tables in an operating system.
    """

    def __init__(self, total_blocks, n_layers, dims, block_size_tokens=BLOCK_SIZE_TOKENS):
        self.block_size = block_size_tokens
        self.n_layers = n_layers
        self.dims = dims
        self.total_blocks = total_blocks
        self.k_blocks = [[[0.0] * dims for _ in range(block_size_tokens)] for _ in range(total_blocks)]
        self.v_blocks = [[[0.0] * dims for _ in range(block_size_tokens)] for _ in range(total_blocks)]
        self.free_blocks = list(range(total_blocks))
        self.refcounts = {i: 0 for i in range(total_blocks)}
        self.block_tables = {}
        self.seq_lengths = {}
        self.alloc_events = []
        self.label = "paged"

    def _alloc_block(self):
        if not self.free_blocks:
            raise RuntimeError("Out of physical blocks!")
        blk = self.free_blocks.pop()
        self.refcounts[blk] = 1
        return blk

    def new_sequence(self, seq_id):
        self.block_tables[seq_id] = {li: [] for li in range(self.n_layers)}
        self.seq_lengths[seq_id] = 0

    def append(self, seq_id, layer, k, v):
        length = self.seq_lengths[seq_id]
        logical_block = length // self.block_size
        slot_in_block = length % self.block_size
        if logical_block >= len(self.block_tables[seq_id][layer]):
            phys_id = self._alloc_block()
            self.block_tables[seq_id][layer].append(phys_id)
            self.alloc_events.append(
                f"  seq {seq_id}, layer {layer}, logical block {logical_block} -> physical block {phys_id}"
            )
        phys_id = self.block_tables[seq_id][layer][logical_block]
        # Copy-on-write: clone shared block before writing
        if self.refcounts[phys_id] > 1:
            new_id = self._alloc_block()
            self.k_blocks[new_id] = [row[:] for row in self.k_blocks[phys_id]]
            self.v_blocks[new_id] = [row[:] for row in self.v_blocks[phys_id]]
            self.refcounts[phys_id] -= 1
            self.block_tables[seq_id][layer][logical_block] = new_id
            phys_id = new_id
        self.k_blocks[phys_id][slot_in_block] = k
        self.v_blocks[phys_id][slot_in_block] = v
        if layer == self.n_layers - 1:
            self.seq_lengths[seq_id] = length + 1

    def get_kv(self, seq_id, layer):
        length = self.seq_lengths[seq_id]
        ks, vs = [], []
        for pos in range(length):
            logical_block = pos // self.block_size
            slot = pos % self.block_size
            phys_id = self.block_tables[seq_id][layer][logical_block]
            ks.append(self.k_blocks[phys_id][slot])
            vs.append(self.v_blocks[phys_id][slot])
        return ks, vs

    def free_sequence(self, seq_id):
        for layer_blocks in self.block_tables[seq_id].values():
            for blk in layer_blocks:
                self.refcounts[blk] -= 1
                if self.refcounts[blk] == 0:
                    self.free_blocks.append(blk)
        del self.block_tables[seq_id]
        del self.seq_lengths[seq_id]

    def utilization(self):
        used_blocks = self.total_blocks - len(self.free_blocks)
        if used_blocks <= 0:
            return 0.0

        occupied_slots = set()
        for seq_id, length in self.seq_lengths.items():
            for layer in range(self.n_layers):
                for pos in range(length):
                    logical_block = pos // self.block_size
                    slot = pos % self.block_size
                    phys_id = self.block_tables[seq_id][layer][logical_block]
                    occupied_slots.add((phys_id, slot))

        return len(occupied_slots) / (used_blocks * self.block_size)

    def share_prefix(self, src_seq_id, dst_seq_id, prefix_len):
        """Copy-on-write prefix sharing: point dst's block table at src's physical blocks."""
        self.new_sequence(dst_seq_id)
        n_shared_blocks = -(-prefix_len // self.block_size)  # ceiling division
        shared = 0
        for layer in range(self.n_layers):
            for b in range(min(n_shared_blocks, len(self.block_tables[src_seq_id][layer]))):
                phys_id = self.block_tables[src_seq_id][layer][b]
                self.block_tables[dst_seq_id][layer].append(phys_id)
                self.refcounts[phys_id] += 1
                shared += 1
        self.seq_lengths[dst_seq_id] = prefix_len
        return shared


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# GPT forward pass (unified for training and inference)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


def gpt(token_id, pos_id, cache, seq_id=0, W=None):
    if W is None:
        W = state_dict
    x = [t + p for t, p in zip(W["wte"][token_id], W["wpe"][pos_id])]
    x = rmsnorm(x)
    for li in range(n_layer):
        x_res = x
        x = rmsnorm(x)
        q, k, v = (linear(x, W[f"layer{li}.attn_w{n}"]) for n in ("q", "k", "v"))
        cache.append(seq_id, li, k, v)
        all_k, all_v = cache.get_kv(seq_id, li)
        x_attn = []
        for h in range(n_head):
            hs = h * head_dim
            q_h = q[hs : hs + head_dim]
            k_h = [ki[hs : hs + head_dim] for ki in all_k]
            v_h = [vi[hs : hs + head_dim] for vi in all_v]
            scores = [sum(q_h[j] * k_h[t][j] for j in range(head_dim)) / head_dim**0.5 for t in range(len(k_h))]
            weights = softmax(scores)
            head_out = [sum(weights[t] * v_h[t][j] for t in range(len(v_h))) for j in range(head_dim)]
            x_attn.extend(head_out)
        x = linear(x_attn, W[f"layer{li}.attn_wo"])
        x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_res)]
        x_res = x
        x = rmsnorm(x)
        x = linear(x, W[f"layer{li}.mlp_fc1"])
        x = relu(x)
        x = linear(x, W[f"layer{li}.mlp_fc2"])
        x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_res)]
    return linear(x, W["lm_head"])


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training (identical to Lab 01, but using ContiguousKVCache)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
learning_rate, beta1, beta2, eps_adam = 0.01, 0.85, 0.99, 1e-8
m = [0.0] * len(params)
v = [0.0] * len(params)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)
    cache = ContiguousKVCache(block_size, n_layer, n_embd)
    cache.new_sequence()
    losses = []
    for pos_id in range(n):
        token_id, target_id = tokens[pos_id], tokens[pos_id + 1]
        logits = gpt(token_id, pos_id, cache)
        probs = softmax(logits)
        losses.append(-probs[target_id].log())
    loss = (1 / n) * sum(losses)
    loss.backward()
    lr_t = learning_rate * (1 - step / num_steps)
    for i, p in enumerate(params):
        m[i] = beta1 * m[i] + (1 - beta1) * p.grad
        v[i] = beta2 * v[i] + (1 - beta2) * p.grad**2
        m_hat = m[i] / (1 - beta1 ** (step + 1))
        v_hat = v[i] / (1 - beta2 ** (step + 1))
        p.data -= lr_t * m_hat / (v_hat**0.5 + eps_adam)
        p.grad = 0
    if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.data:.4f}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference setup
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
W = {name: [[v.data for v in row] for row in mat] for name, mat in state_dict.items()}


def generate(cache, seq_id=0, max_len=block_size, temperature=0.5):
    cache.new_sequence(seq_id)
    token_id, sample = BOS, []
    for pos_id in range(max_len):
        logits = gpt(token_id, pos_id, cache, seq_id=seq_id, W=W)
        probs = softmax([l / temperature for l in logits])
        token_id = random.choices(range(vocab_size), weights=probs)[0]
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        sample.append(token_id)
    return sample, cache


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Compare contiguous vs paged KV cache
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- contiguous vs paged KV cache ---\n")

cache_c = ContiguousKVCache(block_size, n_layer, n_embd)
random.seed(100)
sample_c, _ = generate(cache_c)
name_c = "".join(uchars[t] for t in sample_c)
used_c = cache_c.length * n_layer * n_embd * 2
print(f"contiguous: '{name_c}' ({cache_c.length} tokens)")
print(f"  allocated {cache_c.allocated} slots, used {used_c} ({cache_c.utilization():.0%} slot utilization)")

total_blocks = 32
paged = PagedKVCache(total_blocks, n_layer, n_embd)
random.seed(100)
sample_p, _ = generate(paged, seq_id=0)
name_p = "".join(uchars[t] for t in sample_p)
print(f"paged:      '{name_p}' ({paged.seq_lengths[0]} tokens)")
print(
    f"  {total_blocks - len(paged.free_blocks)} blocks allocated on demand "
    f"({paged.utilization():.0%} slot utilization)"
)
assert name_c == name_p, f"Outputs differ: {name_c} vs {name_p}"
print("  -> identical output, paged allocates only what's needed")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Prefix sharing — shared system prompt
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- prefix sharing (shared system prompt) ---\n")

prefix_cache = PagedKVCache(total_blocks=64, n_layers=n_layer, dims=n_embd)
prefix_tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in "an"]
prefix_cache.new_sequence("prefix")
for pos_id, token_id in enumerate(prefix_tokens):
    gpt(token_id, pos_id, prefix_cache, seq_id="prefix", W=W)

prefix_len = prefix_cache.seq_lengths["prefix"]
used_blk = prefix_cache.total_blocks - len(prefix_cache.free_blocks)
print(f"prefix 'an' cached: {prefix_len} tokens, {used_blk} blocks")

n_shared = 3
for i in range(n_shared):
    shared = prefix_cache.share_prefix("prefix", f"req_{i}", prefix_len)
    print(f"  req_{i}: shared {shared} blocks (zero-copy)")
print(f"blocks: {used_blk} total — sharing adds zero new allocations")
print(f"without sharing: {used_blk * (1 + n_shared)} blocks ({used_blk} x {1 + n_shared})")

print("""
At production scale (Llama 3 70B, 8K context, 100 requests):
contiguous needs ~200 GB, paged needs ~30-60 GB, prefix sharing saves
another 30-50%. This is why vLLM achieves 2-4x higher throughput.
""")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Inference — generate names (using paged KV cache)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("--- inference (paged KV cache) ---")
final_cache = PagedKVCache(total_blocks=128, n_layers=n_layer, dims=n_embd)
for sample_idx in range(20):
    random.seed(42 + sample_idx)
    sample, _ = generate(final_cache, seq_id=f"sample_{sample_idx}")
    name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in sample)
    print(f"sample {sample_idx + 1:2d}: {name}")
    final_cache.free_sequence(f"sample_{sample_idx}")

23.text: Paged attention borrows an operating-systems idea, but it still lives inside ordinary transformer math, so the numerical pieces remain familiar.

24.text: Use the same names corpus so the story stays on memory layout and cache management rather than drifting into a different task.

25.text: This lab mixes model behavior with cache-allocation behavior, so controlling randomness keeps the paging story readable.

27.text: A stable run matters here because we want to watch page allocation and sharing as a deterministic systems story, not as a moving target.

32.text: Locate the shared corpus. The model is familiar; the novelty is how its KV history gets laid out in memory.

33.text: Fetch the corpus if needed so the lab remains runnable end to end, including the cache-allocation demonstrations.

34.text: Only bring in the downloader if the dataset is missing. The real systems lesson starts after the model begins allocating KV storage.

36.text: A small corpus is enough here because the important scaling problem is not training size, but how many requests and prefixes the cache must hold efficiently.

37.text: Fetch the names dataset from the URL and save it to the local data directory.

39.text: Read the file line by line, stripping whitespace. Each line is one name that becomes a training document.

41.text: Show how many names we loaded so we can verify the dataset is intact.

43.text: Collect all unique characters from the dataset and sort them to build a deterministic character-to-ID vocabulary.

46.text: Display the character vocabulary size including the special BOS/EOS token.

51.text: Each token maps to a 16-dimensional vector. Small but sufficient for learning character-level name patterns in this demo.

52.text: Split the embedding dimension across four attention heads for multi-head attention.

53.text: Single layer, keeps training fast for the paging demo.

59.text: Same Value autograd engine from Lab 01, exact copy. Each node is a scalar that tracks its computation graph for automatic differentiation.

60.text: __slots__ for memory efficiency, especially matters here because we'll have thousands of Value nodes during training.

62.text: Constructor: scalar data, parent nodes, and local derivatives.

63.text: The scalar computed during the forward pass. Gradients flow backward from the loss to update this value during training.

65.text: Child nodes in the computation graph.

68.text: Define addition between two Value nodes, tracking both operands for backpropagation.

70.text: New node: sum of data, both local grads are 1.

72.text: Define multiplication between two Values using the product rule: d(a*b)/da = b and d(a*b)/db = a, tracked for backprop.

73.text: Wrap raw Python numbers into Value nodes so arithmetic works uniformly in the graph.

74.text: Product rule: d(a*b)/da = b, d(a*b)/db = a.

76.text: Raise a Value to a constant power, with the derivative following the power rule.

77.text: Natural logarithm with derivative 1/x. Essential for computing cross-entropy loss, which is -log(predicted probability).

78.text: Exponential function, unique in that it's its own derivative. Used inside softmax to convert logits to positive values.

79.text: ReLU: gradient is 1 if positive, 0 if negative.

80.text: Negate a Value by multiplying it by -1, reusing the multiplication operator.

81.text: Support expressions like 3 + value by forwarding to the normal add method.

82.text: Subtract two Values by adding the negation, reusing existing operators.

83.text: Handle number - value by negating the Value and adding, reusing existing operators to keep the autograd graph simple.

84.text: Handle number * value by delegating to the Value's own multiplication, ensuring the computation graph tracks both operands.

85.text: Rewrite division as multiplication by the inverse power, keeping the autograd graph simple by reusing __pow__ and __mul__.

86.text: Support expressions like 3 / value by forwarding to the inverse-power path.

89.text: Sort all nodes so each is processed after everything that depends on it. This ordering makes backpropagation a simple reverse walk.

90.text: Keep a set of already-visited nodes to avoid processing any node twice when the graph has diamond-shaped shared subexpressions.

91.text: Depth-first search that builds the topological ordering by visiting children before parents, matching the reverse flow of gradients.

92.text: If we have already processed this node, skip it to avoid duplicate traversal.

93.text: Add this node to the visited set so we do not process it again.

94.text: Recurse into this node's children first, ensuring they appear earlier in the ordering and receive gradients before this node.

95.text: Recurse into each child node to ensure they appear before this node in the ordering.

96.text: After all children are processed, append this node so it comes last in topological order.

98.text: The gradient of the loss with respect to itself is 1 by definition. This seed value propagates backward through the entire graph.

99.text: Walk the sorted nodes in reverse: from the loss back toward parameters, applying the chain rule at each step.

100.text: Chain rule: multiply local grad by parent grad.

104.text: Weight matrix factory: creates a 2D list of small random Value nodes.

108.text: Set up the nested dictionary that holds all weight matrices: embeddings, per-layer attention projections, and MLP weights.

109.text: Token embeddings: map token IDs to vectors.

110.text: Position embeddings: map positions to vectors.

111.text: Separate output head (no weight tying in this lab).

112.text: Close the initial state dict after adding embedding and output head weights. Per-layer weights are added in the loop below.

113.text: For each transformer layer, allocate the four attention projections (Q, K, V, output) and the two MLP weight matrices.

114.text: Compact loop over the four attention projections.

117.text: MLP second layer: contract 64 → 16.

118.text: Flatten all parameters into a single list for the optimizer.

119.text: Display the total number of trainable scalar parameters so we can verify the model size matches our expectations.

122.text: Matrix-vector multiply: dot product of each weight row with input.

123.text: Returns a vector of dot products.

126.text: Dual-mode softmax: works with both Value nodes (training) and plain floats (inference).

127.text: Type dispatch: check if we're in autograd mode.

128.text: Max subtraction for numerical stability (Value mode).

129.text: Raise e to each shifted logit. The prior max-subtraction keeps the largest exponent at 0, preventing numerical overflow.

132.text: Use Python's built-in math.exp for fast exponentiation in plain float mode.

133.text: Sum all exponentiated values to get the normalizing constant. Dividing each exp by this total yields probabilities summing to 1.

134.text: Divide each exponentiated value by the total, producing a proper probability distribution where all values sum to 1.

138.text: Calculate the inverse root-mean-square in one shot: square all elements, average, add epsilon, raise to the -0.5 power.

139.text: Multiply each element by the computed scale factor, bringing the vector to roughly unit magnitude while preserving its direction.

142.text: Dual-mode ReLU: dispatches to Value.relu() or plain max.

143.text: Detect whether the input is a Value node (training with autograd) or a plain float (inference), to call the right implementation.

144.text: Value mode: use the tracked relu for gradient computation.

145.text: Plain float mode: simple max(0, x).

153.text: Contiguous KV cache: the traditional approach. Pre-allocates max_seq_len slots per layer upfront, regardless of actual sequence length. Simple but wasteful, like malloc'ing the maximum possible size for every request.

156.text: Constructor: specify max length, layers, and dimension.

157.text: Remember the maximum sequence length so we know how many slots to pre-allocate in the contiguous KV buffer.

158.text: Track how many transformer layers need separate K and V caches, since each layer maintains its own attention history.

159.text: The dimensionality of each key and value vector, determining how many floats each cache slot holds.

160.text: Pre-allocate full K cache: n_layers × max_seq_len × dims. All slots allocated even if most stay empty.

161.text: Pre-allocate full V cache: same shape, same waste.

162.text: Track how many positions are actually filled.

163.text: Total allocated slots, this number stays fixed regardless of actual usage, which is the waste PagedAttention solves.

164.text: A string tag to identify this cache type in printed output and comparisons.

166.text: Reset for a new sequence, just moves the length counter back to zero.

167.text: "Free" memory by resetting the pointer. The pre-allocated arrays stay in memory.

169.text: Append a new K,V pair to the cache at the current position.

170.text: Write K vector to the next open slot.

171.text: Write V vector to the same slot.

172.text: Only increment length after the last layer writes, since all layers share the same position counter.

173.text: Move the write pointer forward by one position so the next token's KV pair goes into the next slot.

175.text: Retrieve all cached K,V pairs up to the current position.

176.text: Return slices, only the filled portion, not the empty pre-allocated tail.

178.text: Report what fraction of pre-allocated memory actually holds real data, exposing the waste inherent in fixed-size allocation.

179.text: Count how many KV slots are actually filled with real data from the sequence.

180.text: Ratio of used to allocated. For short sequences in a large buffer, this is often just 10-20%.

183.text: Block size for paged allocation: 4 tokens per block. Like a page size in virtual memory, the fundamental unit of allocation.

186.text: PagedKVCache: the core innovation behind vLLM. Instead of pre-allocating max_seq_len per sequence, allocate small fixed-size blocks on demand. A block table maps logical positions to physical block IDs, exactly like OS page tables map virtual addresses to physical frames.

193.text: Constructor: takes total physical blocks available, layers, dimensions, and block size.

194.text: Record how many tokens fit in each physical block, which determines the granularity of memory allocation and waste.

195.text: Store the number of transformer layers this cache must track KV pairs for.

196.text: The number of floats per key or value vector, used to size each slot within a physical block.

197.text: Total physical blocks in the pool.

198.text: Physical K storage: one block = block_size_tokens × dims. Blocks are the atomic allocation unit.

199.text: Physical V storage: same shape as K blocks.

200.text: Free block list, like the OS free frame list. Blocks are allocated from here on demand and returned when sequences finish.

201.text: Block tables: maps (seq_id, layer) → list of physical block IDs. This IS the page table.

202.text: Dictionary mapping each active sequence ID to how many tokens it has cached so far, used for slot addressing.

204.text: A string tag to identify this cache type in printed output and comparisons.

207.text: Out of blocks = out of memory. In production vLLM, this triggers request preemption.

208.text: Raise an error if the pool is exhausted.

209.text: Pop from the free list and return the physical block ID.

210.text: Allocate one physical block from the free list.

211.text: Initialize a new sequence's page table, one empty block list per layer.

212.text: Create the page table: each layer gets its own list of physical block mappings.

214.text: Every new sequence starts empty with zero cached tokens. Blocks will be allocated on demand as tokens arrive.

215.text: Append K,V to the paged cache, the hot path that makes paging work.

216.text: Current sequence length determines which logical block and slot.

219.text: If this logical block doesn't have a physical block yet, allocate one. This is the demand paging, blocks are allocated only when first touched, not upfront.

220.text: Allocate a physical block from the free pool.

221.text: Map: logical block → physical block in the page table.

222.text: Record the allocation event in the log so we can trace exactly when and why each physical block was claimed.

223.text: Which logical block this position falls in (integer division).

225.text: Look up the physical block ID for this logical block.

226.text: Write K vector into the physical block at the right slot.

227.text: Write V vector into the same slot.

228.text: Increment length only after the last layer.

229.text: Increment the sequence position counter so the next token's KV pair lands in the correct slot.

231.text: Reconstruct the full K,V history by walking the page table, translating logical positions to physical locations.

232.text: Look up how many tokens this sequence has cached so far, which tells us how many KV pairs to gather.

235.text: Logical block index for this position.

236.text: Position modulo block_size gives the slot offset within the block, since each block holds block_size consecutive tokens.

237.text: Translate logical → physical via the page table. This indirection is the price of paging, but it's O(1).

238.text: Read K vector from the physical block.

239.text: Read V vector from the physical block.

240.text: Reassemble the full K and V history by walking the page table to translate each logical position into a physical block and slot.

242.text: Free a sequence's blocks back to the pool, like deallocating pages in an OS.

243.text: Collect all physical block IDs from every layer's page table and return them to the free pool for reuse by other sequences.

244.text: Extend the free list, blocks are immediately available for other sequences.

246.text: Delete the length entry for this sequence since it no longer exists in the cache after deallocation.

248.text: Report what fraction of the physical block pool is currently in use, which tracks actual memory consumption much more tightly than contiguous.

250.text: Ratio: with paging, this tracks actual usage closely. No wasted pre-allocation.

253.text: Prefix sharing: the killer feature of paged caches. Multiple sequences share the same physical blocks for a common prefix, zero-copy, just duplicate the page table entries.

254.text: Set up a fresh page table for the destination sequence so it can start receiving shared block references.

255.text: Ceiling division: how many blocks the shared prefix spans.

256.text: Track how many physical blocks are being shared, so we can report the memory savings from prefix sharing.

257.text: Iterate over each transformer layer to copy its block table entries.

258.text: For each block in the prefix...

259.text: Look up which physical block holds this part of the source sequence's prefix in the page table.

260.text: Point the destination's page table at the SAME physical block. No data copy, both sequences read from identical memory.

262.text: Set the destination sequence's cached length to match the prefix, since it inherits all those positions from the source.

263.text: Report the total number of physical blocks that were shared, letting the caller calculate the memory savings.

264.text: Increment the shared block counter each time we point the destination at an existing physical block.

271.text: Unified GPT forward pass: processes one token at a time using any cache implementation. The cache abstraction means the model code doesn't know or care whether it's paged or contiguous.

272.text: Default to the training state dict if no weights provided.

273.text: If no separate inference weights are passed in, use the training state dict. This lets the same function work for both phases.

279.text: Compute Q, K, V projections in one compact expression using a generator.

280.text: Store K,V in the cache, the cache handles the allocation strategy transparently.

281.text: Retrieve all historical K,V pairs, the cache translates logical to physical positions if paged.

282.text: Empty list that will collect each head's output before concatenation into the full multi-head attention result.

283.text: Process each attention head independently, since heads operate on separate slices of the embedding and don't interact.

284.text: Compute the starting index for this head's slice within the full embedding vector.

285.text: Slice out this head's query from the full projection vector, isolating the dimensions this head is responsible for.

288.text: Scaled dot-product attention against all cached keys.

294.text: Snapshot before the MLP transforms it. The residual connection will add this back so the MLP can only refine the representation.

295.text: Normalize activations before the feed-forward network so the MLP weights see well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

298.text: Contract the activated hidden state back to the embedding dimension via the second MLP weight matrix.

299.text: Add the MLP output to the pre-MLP snapshot, completing the second residual connection in this transformer layer.

300.text: Map the final hidden state to a score for every token in the vocabulary, producing the logits used for next-token prediction.

301.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer layers.

302.text: Snapshot the current representation before attention modifies it. The residual connection adds this back to preserve the original signal.

303.text: Append this head's output to the growing list. Once all heads finish, these slices get concatenated into a full vector.

304.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model's embedding dimension.

305.text: Expand the hidden state to four times the embedding dimension via the first MLP weight matrix.

306.text: Adam optimizer settings: learning rate, momentum decay rates (beta1 for mean, beta2 for variance), and epsilon to prevent division by zero.

307.text: Buffer for the exponential moving average of each parameter's gradient (first moment), initialized to zero for all parameters.

311.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step with teacher forcing.

312.text: Pick the next training name by cycling through the shuffled dataset with a modulo index.

314.text: Limit the sequence to the maximum context window the model can handle.

315.text: Create a contiguous cache for this training step. Training always uses contiguous, paging is an inference optimization.

316.text: Reset the contiguous cache to empty for this training step's sequence, clearing the position counter to zero.

317.text: Collect the cross-entropy loss at each token position. We'll average them to get the training signal for this step.

318.text: Feed each token one at a time in sequence order, caching KV pairs, matching how inference will run with the cache.

319.text: Input and target for this position.

320.text: Forward pass through the model with the cache.

321.text: Turn raw logit scores into probabilities via softmax so we can compute the cross-entropy against the correct next token.

322.text: Cross-entropy loss: negative log probability of the correct next token.

324.text: Compute gradients of the loss with respect to every parameter via backpropagation.

325.text: Reduce the learning rate linearly toward zero as training progresses, allowing fine-grained convergence at the end.

327.text: Update the running average of gradients (first moment). This smooths out noisy per-step gradients for more stable updates.

330.text: Correct the second moment estimate for the same zero-initialization bias, ensuring accurate variance estimates early in training.

331.text: Apply the Adam update rule to move this parameter one step toward lower loss.

332.text: Zero out this parameter's gradient so it does not accumulate across training steps.

333.text: Print the training loss at regular intervals so we can monitor convergence.

334.text: Display the current training step and loss value so we can monitor convergence during the training loop.

335.text: Update the running average of squared gradients (second moment). This adapts the learning rate per-parameter based on gradient history.

336.text: Correct the first moment estimate for initialization bias, since starting at zero makes early estimates too small.

339.text: Strip autograd wrappers: extract plain floats for fast inference.

342.text: Generate a name using any cache implementation. The cache parameter abstracts the allocation strategy completely.

343.text: Reset the cache for a fresh sequence, clearing any previously stored KV pairs and resetting internal pointers.

344.text: Start with BOS, empty sample buffer.

346.text: Forward pass using plain-float weights and the provided cache.

348.text: Draw one token from the probability distribution to decide the next character.

349.text: The BOS token doubles as an end-of-sequence signal. When the model predicts it, the name is complete and generation stops.

351.text: Add the newly generated token to the output list, building the name character by character.

352.text: Return both the generated token list and the cache object so the caller can inspect memory utilization and allocation patterns.

360.text: Allocate a contiguous cache sized for the full context window, pre-reserving all slots even though most will stay empty.

361.text: Lock the random seed before generation so both cache strategies see identical sampling decisions for a fair comparison.

365.text: Print the generated name and token count.

366.text: Show allocated vs. used slots, the waste is visible here.

369.text: Allocate a paged cache backed by the same total memory capacity, but using on-demand block allocation instead of upfront reservation.

370.text: Same seed ensures identical random choices.

372.text: Convert the generated token IDs back to characters so we can display the name and visually compare it against the contiguous version.

373.text: Display the name generated with the paged cache and its token count for comparison.

374.text: Show blocks allocated on demand, much less waste than contiguous.

375.text: Assert outputs match, the cache strategy must not affect model output, only memory efficiency.

376.text: Verify that paged and contiguous caches produce the exact same output. The allocation strategy must be invisible to the model's computation.

377.text: Generate a name using the contiguous cache to establish the baseline for memory usage and output comparison.

378.text: 32 physical blocks for the paged cache pool.

384.text: Tokenize a short prefix "an", simulates a shared system prompt.

387.text: Forward pass stores K,V in the cache at each position.

388.text: Run each prefix token through the model to populate the KV cache.

389.text: Look up the prefix sequence's length in the cache to verify all tokens were stored correctly before sharing.

390.text: Count how many blocks the prefix occupies.

391.text: Print how many tokens and physical blocks the prefix occupies, showing the baseline cost that sharing will amortize.

393.text: Spawn three new sequences that will share the prefix's physical blocks, demonstrating zero-copy KV reuse.

395.text: Share the prefix's physical blocks with the new sequence, zero data copying, just page table duplication.

396.text: Report how many blocks were shared (should match the prefix's block count).

397.text: Show that sharing adds zero new block allocations.

labs.title: Soft Thinking
labs.tagline: Pass a probability-weighted blend forward, preserving information.
labs.description:
Standard autoregressive models collapse the output distribution to a single token via argmax or sampling, then embed that token for the next step. Soft thinking instead computes a weighted average of all token embeddings, weighted by the output probabilities.

This "concept token" preserves the model's uncertainty: if the model is 60% sure of 'a' and 40% sure of 'e', the concept token is a blend of both embeddings. No information is discarded. The model can then use this richer signal for subsequent predictions.

The tradeoff: concept tokens are more expensive to compute (a matrix multiply with the full vocabulary) and the model must learn to interpret blended inputs.
learning_objectives: Implement probability-weighted concept tokens as model input
Explain how soft tokens preserve uncertainty across generation steps
Evaluate the compute-information tradeoff of concept tokens

exercises.prompt: Apply a temperature of 0.5 to the probability distribution before computing concept tokens. How does sharpening the distribution affect generation quality?
exercises.hint: Lower temperature makes concept tokens closer to hard one-hot tokens. Compare the generated names with temperature 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 for concept token computation.

exercises.prompt: Print the entropy of the concept token distribution at each generation step. Does the model become more confident as it generates more characters?
exercises.hint: Compute -sum(p * log(p)) for the distribution at each step. Lower entropy means the model is more certain about the next token.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — Soft thinking edition.

Same architecture as the PyTorch version (03), but with soft decoding at
inference time. Instead of collapsing to a single token at each step, soft
thinking passes a "concept token" -- a probability-weighted blend of all
token embeddings -- to the next step. The full distribution flows forward,
preserving information that hard decoding discards.

  Hard:  logits -> sample -> embed(token)            -> next input
  Soft:  logits -> softmax(logits/T) @ embed_table   -> next input

Based on "Soft Thinking: Unlocking the Reasoning Potential of LLMs in Continuous
Concept Space" (Zhang et al., 2025), https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.15778. The
concept token computation follows the paper's formulation. Note that this is an
inference-only technique -- the model is trained with standard teacher forcing on
hard tokens, which creates a train-test distribution mismatch that Lab 18 (soft
training) addresses.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (same as Lab 03, with inputs_embeds support for concept tokens)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head
device = "cpu"


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)
        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx=None, inputs_embeds=None):
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx) if idx is not None else inputs_embeds
        assert tok_emb is not None, "provide idx or inputs_embeds"
        T = tok_emb.shape[1]
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=tok_emb.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Shared utilities (also used by Lab 18)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
num_steps = 1000


@torch.no_grad()
def generate(model, mode="hard", soft_temp=1.0, temperature=0.5):
    """Generate a name using hard (discrete) or soft (concept token) decoding."""
    model.eval()
    tokens, entropies = [], []
    embeds = model.wte(torch.tensor([[BOS]], device=device))

    for _ in range(block_size):
        logits = model(inputs_embeds=embeds)[0, -1]

        # Sample a discrete token (for output and stopping)
        probs = F.softmax(logits / temperature, dim=-1)
        token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        tokens.append(token_id)

        # Track entropy: how spread is the distribution?
        entropies.append(-(probs * probs.clamp(min=1e-10).log()).sum().item())

        # Next input: discrete embedding or concept token
        if mode == "hard":
            next_emb = model.wte(torch.tensor([[token_id]], device=device))
        else:
            # Concept token: probability-weighted blend of ALL token embeddings
            soft_probs = F.softmax(logits / soft_temp, dim=-1)
            next_emb = (soft_probs @ model.wte.weight).view(1, 1, -1)

        embeds = torch.cat([embeds, next_emb], dim=1)[:, -block_size:]

    return tokens, entropies


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training & Inference (runs only when executed directly)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
if __name__ == "__main__":
    model = MicroGPT().to(device)
    print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

    # Training — identical to Lab 03
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)

    for step in range(num_steps):
        doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
        tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
        targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

        logits = model(input_ids)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
        for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
            pg["lr"] = lr_t
        optimizer.step()
        if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
            print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

    # Inference — hard vs. soft decoding at different temperatures
    print("\n--- soft thinking comparison ---\n")
    n_samples = 20
    max_H = math.log(vocab_size)

    for mode, soft_temp, label in [
        ("hard", 1.0, "hard (standard decoding)"),
        ("soft", 0.5, "soft T=0.5 (mild blend)"),
        ("soft", 1.0, "soft T=1.0 (moderate blend)"),
        ("soft", 2.0, "soft T=2.0 (diffuse blend)"),
    ]:
        print(f"{label}:")
        all_H = []
        for i in range(n_samples):
            toks, ents = generate(model, mode, soft_temp)
            name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in toks)
            avg_H = sum(ents) / len(ents) if ents else 0
            all_H.append(avg_H)
            if i < 10:
                print(f"  {i + 1:2d}: {name:<15s} entropy {avg_H:.2f}/{max_H:.2f}")
        print(f"  -> mean entropy: {sum(all_H) / len(all_H):.2f}/{max_H:.2f}\n")

    print(f"""--- what's happening ---

Standard (hard) decoding collapses the model's rich output distribution to a
single token ID at every step. The next step sees only one embedding — all
information about what the model "almost said" is discarded.

Soft thinking preserves this information:

  concept_token = softmax(logits / T) @ embedding_table

The concept token is a {n_embd}-dimensional vector blending all {vocab_size} token
embeddings, weighted by their probability. It lives in the same embedding space
as regular tokens but encodes the model's full uncertainty.

Temperature (T) controls the softness:
  T -> 0:  concept token = argmax embedding (hard, no benefit)
  T = 1:   standard softmax (moderate blending)
  T -> inf: uniform weights (noise — all tokens equally blended)

The entropy column shows how "spread" each distribution is. Higher entropy
means more tokens contribute to the concept token — richer information, but
higher risk of out-of-distribution drift. Max entropy = ln({vocab_size}) = {max_H:.2f}.

This is training-free — no model weights change. The Soft Thinking paper
(Zhang et al., 2025) shows this improves reasoning in large models by
+2.5 pass@1 while using 22% fewer tokens.
""")

21.text: Soft thinking is about uncertainty that stays alive instead of being collapsed immediately, so entropy becomes part of the story rather than just a side metric.

22.text: Same names dataset again. The lab is changing the representation passed between steps, not the task itself.

23.text: We need repeatable runs because this lab compares two very different decoding philosophies on the same underlying model.

25.text: PyTorch provides the baseline model; the new idea is that the next-step input might be a probability-weighted concept, not a single hard token.

26.text: The transformer itself barely changes. What changes is what kind of object gets fed back into it after each prediction.

27.text: Softmax is the star here because it turns logits into the probability distribution that will either be collapsed away or preserved as a blended concept token.

29.text: Lock both modes to the same random baseline so the hard-vs-soft comparison is interpretable.

30.text: PyTorch's RNG controls both initialization and sampling, so it has to be fixed too.

35.text: Construct the dataset file path relative to this script's location so it works regardless of the working directory.

36.text: If the names dataset doesn't exist locally yet, download it on the fly so the script is self-contained.

42.text: Read the dataset file where each line contains a single name for training.

43.text: Randomize the order of training names so the model doesn't memorize alphabetical patterns from the dataset.

46.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset, mapping each to a deterministic token ID from 0 to n-1.

47.text: The BOS token marks the beginning and end of each name sequence during training and generation.

54.text: Small model, same architecture as Lab 03, but with one crucial addition: the forward pass accepts pre-computed embeddings, not just token IDs.

55.text: Split the embedding dimension across four heads for multi-head attention.

56.text: A single transformer layer keeps the model small and training fast for this demo.

57.text: Maximum sequence length of 16 tokens, matching the longest names in the dataset.

58.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the full embedding.

59.text: Run everything on CPU since this small model does not need GPU acceleration.

62.text: RMSNorm, same as all previous labs.

67.text: Scale the input by its inverse root-mean-square, bringing activations to roughly unit magnitude without learned parameters.

74.text: Q, K, V, and output projections.

79.text: Run the complete attention computation: project inputs to Q/K/V, score, mask future positions, blend values, and project output.

80.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input shape.

81.text: Reshape the query projection into separate heads so each head independently attends to its own slice of the embedding.

82.text: Project to keys and reshape into multi-head format, same as queries above.

83.text: Project to values and reshape into multi-head format, same as queries above.

84.text: Compute Q dot K transposed, scaled by sqrt(head_dim). The scaling prevents softmax from saturating as dimensionality grows.

85.text: Causal mask, no peeking at future tokens.

86.text: Set future positions to negative infinity so softmax gives them zero attention weight.

89.text: Mix information across heads by projecting the concatenated output back to embedding dimension.

92.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information.

96.text: Project the expanded representation back to the model's embedding dimension, compressing the MLP's "thinking space" output.

99.text: Apply ReLU activation between the two linear layers, introducing the needed nonlinearity.

102.text: One transformer block combining attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections.

105.text: RMSNorm before attention ensures the Q/K/V projections see inputs with consistent scale, stabilizing training.

106.text: The multi-head causal self-attention sublayer that lets tokens attend to prior positions.

107.text: RMSNorm before the MLP ensures the feed-forward weights see well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

108.text: The feed-forward network sublayer that processes each token independently after attention.

111.text: Add the attention output to the original input via a skip connection, so this layer can only refine, not overwrite.

112.text: Add the MLP output via a second skip connection, preserving gradient flow and ensuring the layer is an incremental refinement.

116.text: The model, now with a key difference from earlier labs.

119.text: Token embedding table, this does double duty in soft thinking: it maps token IDs to embeddings AND is the table we blend against to create concept tokens.

120.text: Learned position vectors that tell the model where each token sits in the sequence. Added to token embeddings before the transformer.

121.text: RMSNorm applied to the combined embeddings before the transformer stack, stabilizing the first layer's input scale.

122.text: The stack of transformer blocks that form the core of the model's processing pipeline.

124.text: Walk every submodule and apply the weight initialization function so all learned parameters start with well-scaled random values.

128.text: Small normal init for all weights.

131.text: The critical change: forward accepts either token IDs OR pre-computed embeddings. This is what enables soft thinking, we can feed in concept tokens (weighted blends) instead of discrete token embeddings.

132.text: If idx is provided, look up embeddings normally. Otherwise, use the pre-computed inputs_embeds directly.

133.text: Safety check: must provide one or the other.

134.text: Figure out how long the input sequence is from whichever input format was provided (token IDs or raw embeddings).

135.text: Position embeddings work the same either way.

136.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embeddings before feeding them into the transformer stack.

137.text: Feed the embeddings through each transformer block in sequence, progressively refining the representations.

139.text: Map the final hidden states to a score for every vocabulary token, producing the logits for next-token prediction.

148.text: No gradients during generation, pure inference.

149.text: The generation function: supports both hard (standard) and soft (concept token) decoding. This is where the magic happens.

151.text: Disable dropout and other training-specific behaviors. Not strictly needed here (no dropout), but good practice for generation.

152.text: Track generated tokens and per-step entropy (measures how uncertain the model is).

153.text: Start with the BOS token embedding, same for both modes.

156.text: Forward pass using embeddings (not token IDs), this is how concept tokens flow through the model.
