159.text: Divide logits by the sampling temperature and apply softmax to control how peaked or spread out the token probabilities are.

160.text: Sample a discrete token, needed for output text and stopping condition, regardless of mode.

161.text: BOS doubles as end-of-sequence. When sampled, it means the name is complete and generation should stop.

163.text: Append the chosen token to the output list, building up the generated name one character at a time.

166.text: Shannon entropy of the distribution: measures how spread out the model's beliefs are. Low entropy = confident, high entropy = uncertain. We'll compare this across decoding modes.

169.text: Hard decoding: collapse to a single token, throw away the distribution. Standard approach.

170.text: Look up the single selected token's embedding, all information about alternatives is lost.

172.text: Soft decoding: preserve the full distribution as a "concept token."

173.text: Soft temperature controls how blended the concept token is. Lower T = more peaked (closer to hard). Higher T = more spread (more tokens contribute).

174.text: THE key line: softmax(logits/T) @ embedding_table. This creates a weighted average of ALL token embeddings, where the weights are the model's probabilities. The concept token lives in the same embedding space as regular tokens, but encodes the full uncertainty of what the model "wanted to say."

176.text: Append the new embedding (hard or soft) and truncate to context window. The model now sees concept tokens as part of its history.

184.text: Only run training and inference when executed directly, Lab 18 imports from this file.

185.text: Create the model, move it to the device, and print its parameter count for verification.

189.text: Adam optimizer, same settings as Lab 03.

191.text: Standard next-token prediction training: the model sees ground-truth tokens as input and learns to predict each next one.

192.text: Pick one training example per step, cycling through the shuffled dataset.

194.text: Truncate the token sequence to fit within the model's positional embedding table and context window.

196.text: Prepare the input tensor containing all tokens except the last one in the sequence.

199.text: Forward pass with token IDs (standard training, no soft tokens yet).

200.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the correct next tokens at each position.

202.text: Reset all parameter gradients to zero before computing the new backward pass.

203.text: Compute gradients of the loss with respect to every model parameter via backpropagation.

204.text: Scale the learning rate down linearly toward zero as training progresses, for smoother convergence near the end.

205.text: Write the decayed learning rate into the optimizer so the next parameter update uses the reduced step size.

207.text: Apply the computed gradients to update all model weights one step toward lower loss.

212.text: Compare hard vs. soft decoding at different soft temperatures.

213.text: Generate 20 names for each configuration, enough samples to see meaningful differences in behavior between decoding modes.

214.text: Maximum possible entropy: ln(vocab_size). This is the "uniform distribution" ceiling.

216.text: Test four configurations: hard baseline plus three soft temperatures.

217.text: Hard decoding (standard), mild soft, moderate soft, and very diffuse soft.

222.text: Print which configuration is being tested (hard vs. soft, and at what temperature) so the output is easy to interpret.

225.text: Generate one name, collecting entropy at each step.

227.text: Average entropy across generation steps, how uncertain was the model on average?

229.text: Show first 10 names with their entropy.

230.text: Display name and entropy relative to maximum. Higher entropy in soft mode shows the distribution is spreading, the concept tokens carry more information forward.

231.text: Average entropy across all samples, the summary statistic.

labs.title: Soft Training
labs.tagline: Use concept tokens during training too.
labs.description:
Soft thinking (Lab 17) uses concept tokens at inference but trains with hard (one-hot) tokens. This creates a train-test mismatch: the model never sees blended inputs during training.

Soft training fixes this with a scheduled curriculum. Early in training, inputs are mostly hard tokens. As training progresses, the model gradually receives more concept tokens as input. By the end, it's fully trained on its own soft outputs.

The curriculum schedule (linear ramp from 0% to 100% soft inputs) is critical — starting with all-soft inputs from the beginning doesn't work because the model's early predictions are random noise.
learning_objectives: Implement a curriculum schedule transitioning from hard to soft inputs
Explain the train-test mismatch problem and how soft training solves it
Design curriculum schedules for different convergence behaviors

exercises.prompt: Change the curriculum schedule from linear to exponential (e.g., soft_ratio = min(1, exp(step/total - 1))). Does faster or slower transition to soft inputs work better?
exercises.hint: Exponential schedules spend more time in the hard-token regime early on. Compare final loss and generation quality against the linear schedule.

exercises.prompt: Start training with 100% soft inputs from step 1 (no curriculum). Verify that training fails or produces poor results, confirming why the curriculum is needed.
exercises.hint: Without curriculum, early soft tokens are random noise since the model has not learned yet. Watch the loss curve to see if it converges at all.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — Soft training edition.

Builds on Lab 17 (soft thinking): instead of only using concept tokens at
inference time, this version also uses them during training. A curriculum
gradually replaces ground-truth token embeddings with the model's own soft
predictions, closing the train-test gap that limits inference-only soft thinking.

  Standard training:  input = embed(ground_truth_token)
  Soft training:      input = (1-mix) * embed(ground_truth) + mix * concept_token
                      where mix increases from 0 -> 1 during training

Extends the concept from "Soft Thinking" (Zhang et al., 2025),
https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.15778 by also using concept tokens during training.
The curriculum schedule (linear ramp from hard to soft inputs) is inspired by
"Scheduled Sampling for Sequence Prediction with Recurrent Neural Networks"
(Bengio et al., 2015), https://arxiv.org/abs/1506.03099. Note that soft training
is an educational extension exploring how to close the train-test gap -- the
original Soft Thinking paper focuses on inference-time techniques.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (same as Lab 17 — supports both token IDs and raw embeddings)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head
device = "cpu"


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)
        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx=None, inputs_embeds=None):
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx) if idx is not None else inputs_embeds
        assert tok_emb is not None, "provide idx or inputs_embeds"
        T = tok_emb.shape[1]
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=tok_emb.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Generation — hard (discrete) or soft (concept token) decoding
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
num_steps = 1000
temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
soft_temp = 1.0


@torch.no_grad()
def generate(model, mode="hard", soft_temp=1.0):
    """Generate a name using hard (discrete) or soft (concept token) decoding."""
    model.eval()
    tokens, entropies = [], []
    embeds = model.wte(torch.tensor([[BOS]], device=device))

    for _ in range(block_size):
        logits = model(inputs_embeds=embeds)[0, -1]

        probs = F.softmax(logits / temperature, dim=-1)
        token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
        if token_id == BOS:
            break
        tokens.append(token_id)

        entropies.append(-(probs * probs.clamp(min=1e-10).log()).sum().item())

        if mode == "hard":
            next_emb = model.wte(torch.tensor([[token_id]], device=device))
        else:
            soft_probs = F.softmax(logits / soft_temp, dim=-1)
            next_emb = (soft_probs @ model.wte.weight).view(1, 1, -1)

        embeds = torch.cat([embeds, next_emb], dim=1)[:, -block_size:]

    return tokens, entropies


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training — standard vs. soft input mixing
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def train_model(model, name, soft_mix=False):
    """Train with standard teacher forcing, or with soft input curriculum."""
    print(f"\n--- training {name} ({sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())} params) ---")
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)

    for step in range(num_steps):
        doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
        tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)

        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
        targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)

        if soft_mix and n > 1:
            mix = step / num_steps  # curriculum: 0 -> 1

            # Model's soft predictions (detached — no gradient through this path)
            with torch.no_grad():
                pred_logits = model(input_ids)
                soft_embeds = F.softmax(pred_logits / soft_temp, dim=-1) @ model.wte.weight

            # BOS stays ground truth; rest blended with concept tokens
            # soft_embeds[:, i] predicts position i+1, so shift left by one
            gt_embeds = model.wte(input_ids)
            mixed = torch.cat([gt_embeds[:, :1], (1 - mix) * gt_embeds[:, 1:] + mix * soft_embeds[:, :-1]], dim=1)

            logits = model(inputs_embeds=mixed)
        else:
            logits = model(input_ids)

        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        lr_t = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
        for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
            pg["lr"] = lr_t
        optimizer.step()

        if (step + 1) % 10 == 0 or step == 0:
            extra = f" | mix {mix:.2f}" if soft_mix and n > 1 else ""
            print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}{extra}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Train both models from the same initial weights (fair comparison)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
standard_model = MicroGPT().to(device)
soft_model = MicroGPT().to(device)
soft_model.load_state_dict(standard_model.state_dict())

train_model(standard_model, "standard")
train_model(soft_model, "soft-trained", soft_mix=True)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Compare: standard-trained vs. soft-trained, hard vs. soft decoding
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- comparison: standard-trained vs. soft-trained ---\n")
n_samples = 20
max_H = math.log(vocab_size)

configs = [
    (standard_model, "standard-trained", "hard", 1.0),
    (standard_model, "standard-trained", "soft", 1.0),
    (soft_model, "soft-trained", "hard", 1.0),
    (soft_model, "soft-trained", "soft", 1.0),
]

with torch.no_grad():
    for model_obj, model_name, mode, st in configs:
        model_obj.eval()
        print(f"{model_name} + {mode} decoding:")
        all_H = []
        for i in range(n_samples):
            toks, ents = generate(model_obj, mode, st)
            name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in toks)
            avg_H = sum(ents) / len(ents) if ents else 0
            all_H.append(avg_H)
            if i < 10:
                print(f"  {i + 1:2d}: {name:<15s} entropy {avg_H:.2f}/{max_H:.2f}")
        print(f"  -> mean entropy: {sum(all_H) / len(all_H):.2f}/{max_H:.2f}\n")

print("""--- what's happening ---

Standard training uses teacher forcing: the model always sees perfect
ground-truth embeddings as input. At inference with soft decoding (Lab 17),
it encounters concept tokens — weighted blends it never trained on.
This train-test mismatch limits soft decoding's effectiveness.

Soft training fixes this with a curriculum:

  input = (1 - mix) * embed(ground_truth) + mix * concept_token
  mix: 0 -> 1 over training (start standard, end fully soft)

Each training step has two forward passes:
  1. Standard forward (detached) -> logits -> compute concept tokens
  2. Mixed-input forward -> loss -> backprop

The concept token at position i comes from the model's prediction at
position i-1, replacing the ground-truth embedding that would normally
occupy that slot. Position 0 (BOS) always stays ground truth.

This is scheduled sampling with soft tokens: the model gradually learns
to work with continuous inputs, closing the distribution gap between
training and soft inference.
""")

22.text: Soft training is about closing the gap between what the model sees during training and at soft-decoding time, so the math here supports that uncertainty-aware training signal.

23.text: Keep the data fixed. This lab is about the training curriculum, not about changing the problem.

24.text: We want to compare ordinary teacher forcing against gradual soft-input training, so the random parts of the run need to stay pinned down.

26.text: PyTorch gives us the normal training loop; the twist is that the model will increasingly train on its own blended concepts instead of only pristine ground truth.

27.text: The architecture is not the novelty here. The novelty is what kind of inputs the architecture learns to expect during training.

28.text: Softmax matters twice now: once for the usual prediction distribution, and again for turning that distribution into a concept-token training signal.

30.text: Lock the training run down so the effect of the soft-input curriculum is visible instead of getting lost in randomness.

31.text: PyTorch's own RNG has to match too, otherwise the comparison with hard-only training would start from different models.

36.text: Construct the path to the dataset file relative to this script's location so it works from any working directory.

37.text: If the names dataset isn't cached locally yet, download it on the fly so the script is self-contained.

38.text: Only import the URL library when we actually need to download, keeping startup fast.

40.text: The URL pointing to Karpathy's makemore names dataset hosted on GitHub.

41.text: Fetch the names dataset from the URL and save it to the local data directory.

43.text: Read the file line by line, stripping whitespace. Each line is one name that becomes a training document.

45.text: Show how many names we loaded so we can verify the dataset is intact.

47.text: Collect all unique characters and sort them to create a deterministic character-to-ID mapping for tokenization.

50.text: Display the character vocabulary size including the special BOS/EOS token.

55.text: Each token maps to a 16-dimensional vector. Small but sufficient for learning character-level name patterns.

56.text: Split the embedding dimension across four heads for multi-head attention.

57.text: A single transformer layer keeps the model small and training fast. This lab focuses on the training method, not model depth.

63.text: RMSNorm: rescale by inverse root-mean-square. Simpler and faster than LayerNorm.

64.text: Dimension unused but kept for API consistency.

65.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

66.text: Tiny constant added inside the square root to prevent division by zero when the input vector is near-zero.

68.text: Run the RMSNorm computation, scaling the input by its inverse root-mean-square.

72.text: Standard left-to-right attention that prevents each position from seeing future tokens, identical to earlier labs.

73.text: No size parameters, uses module-level constants (simpler since both models are identical).

74.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

75.text: Linear projection that transforms input embeddings into query vectors for attention.

76.text: Linear projection that transforms input embeddings into key vectors for attention.

77.text: Linear projection that transforms input embeddings into value vectors for attention.

78.text: Mix information across heads by projecting the concatenated output back to embedding dimension.

80.text: Run the full attention computation: project, score, mask, weight, and combine values.

81.text: Unpack batch, sequence length, and embedding dimension.

82.text: Project to queries and reshape into heads.

83.text: Project to keys and reshape into multi-head format, same as queries above.

84.text: Project to values and reshape into multi-head format, same as queries above.

85.text: Compute Q dot K transposed, scaled by sqrt(head_dim), producing raw attention scores before masking and softmax.

86.text: Causal mask: prevent attending to future positions.

88.text: Normalize attention scores via softmax over the key dimension, converting raw scores into a probability distribution.

89.text: Weighted value sum, reshape back.

90.text: Mix information across heads by projecting the concatenated output back to embedding dimension.

93.text: The position-wise feed-forward network that processes each token independently through expand-ReLU-contract.

95.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

96.text: Linear layer that expands the hidden state to four times the embedding dimension.

97.text: Linear layer that contracts the expanded representation back to the embedding dimension.

99.text: Run the input through the expand-ReLU-contract sequence and return the result.

103.text: Transformer block: attention + MLP with residual connections.

104.text: No constructor parameters needed since the model dimensions are module-level constants.

105.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

106.text: RMSNorm applied before the attention sublayer in the pre-norm transformer style.

107.text: The causal self-attention sublayer that lets each token gather information from all previous positions in the sequence.

108.text: RMSNorm applied before the MLP sublayer in the pre-norm transformer style.

109.text: The feed-forward network sublayer that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

111.text: Run input through attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections.

112.text: Add attention output to the input via a skip connection, preserving gradient flow and the original representation.

113.text: Add the MLP output to the input via a residual connection, preserving gradient flow.

114.text: Pass the updated hidden states back to the caller. These become input for the next transformer block or final projection.

117.text: The full model, same architecture as Lab 17. The key difference: forward accepts either token IDs or raw embeddings, which is what enables soft training.

118.text: No size parameters since both models share the same architecture.

119.text: Standard PyTorch module initialization, calling the parent class constructor.

120.text: Learned dense vectors for each token, mapping discrete IDs into continuous embedding space for the transformer.

121.text: Learned position vectors that tell the model where each token sits. Added to token embeddings before the transformer.

122.text: RMSNorm applied after combining token and position embeddings, stabilizing the first transformer layer's input scale.

123.text: A sequence of transformer blocks stacked together, each refining the token representations through attention and MLP sublayers.

125.text: Walk every submodule and apply the weight initialization function so all parameters start with well-scaled random values.

128.text: The function that initializes weight matrices with small random values.

129.text: Only init Linear and Embedding layers.

130.text: Initialize weights from a narrow Gaussian (std=0.08). Small initial values prevent activations from exploding early in training.

132.text: Dual-input forward pass: accepts either idx (token IDs for standard training) or inputs_embeds (pre-computed embeddings for soft training). This flexibility is what makes the curriculum possible.

133.text: If token IDs given, look them up; otherwise use the raw embeddings directly. This single branch is the gateway for soft training.

134.text: Verify that the caller provided either token IDs or pre-computed embeddings as input.

135.text: Determine the sequence length from the embedding tensor shape, since we need it for position embedding lookup.

136.text: Fetch learned position vectors for each slot in the sequence so the model knows token ordering.

138.text: Feed the normalized embeddings through each transformer block, progressively building richer token representations.

139.text: Run one transformer block, updating the hidden representations for this layer.

140.text: Map the final hidden states to a score for every vocabulary token, producing the logits for next-token prediction.

146.text: Train for 1000 steps, enough for this small character-level model to converge on basic name patterns.

147.text: Controls the sharpness of the sampling distribution during generation. Temperature 0.5 balances diversity and coherence.

148.text: Soft temperature for concept token computation, 1.0 means full softmax probabilities (no sharpening).

151.text: Turn off gradient computation for the entire generation phase, since we don't need backpropagation during inference.

152.text: Generate a name using either hard (discrete) or soft (concept token) decoding.

154.text: Switch the model to evaluation mode so batch norm and dropout behave deterministically during generation.

155.text: Track generated tokens and per-step entropies.

159.text: Forward pass using embeddings (not token IDs), this allows soft concept tokens as input.

162.text: Draw one token from the probability distribution to decide the next character.

164.text: End-of-sequence detected, so stop generating further tokens for this name.

165.text: Append the chosen token to the output list, building up the generated name character by character.

167.text: Shannon entropy of the distribution, measures how uncertain the model is. Low entropy = confident predictions; high entropy = the model is unsure, and soft decoding helps most here.

169.text: Hard decoding: look up the discrete token's embedding. Standard approach, picks one token and commits to it.

171.text: The else branch: if soft decoding is enabled, create a concept token instead of looking up a single embedding.

172.text: Re-softmax the logits at the soft temperature to control how spread out the concept token's probability weights are.

173.text: The concept token: probability-weighted average of ALL token embeddings. Instead of picking one token, this continuous blend carries information about the model's full belief distribution. A soft "a" that's 30% "b" encodes richer context than a hard "a" alone.

177.text: Package the generated token IDs and per-step entropy values into a return tuple for analysis and comparison.

183.text: Training function: supports both standard teacher forcing and soft input mixing via the soft_mix flag.

185.text: Print model name and parameter count.

188.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step with teacher forcing.

189.text: Pick the next training name by cycling through the shuffled dataset with a modulo index.

191.text: Limit the sequence to the maximum context window the model can handle.

196.text: Soft mixing branch: only when enabled and sequence is long enough.

197.text: The curriculum schedule: mix goes from 0 (pure ground truth) to 1 (pure concept tokens) linearly over training. This is the key insight, gradual transition avoids the shock of suddenly switching from perfect teacher inputs to noisy self-generated ones.

200.text: First forward pass (detached): get the model's current predictions without tracking gradients. These predictions become the concept tokens for the second forward pass.

201.text: Standard forward pass on ground-truth inputs.

202.text: Convert logits to soft concept tokens: softmax gives probabilities, matrix multiply with embedding weights gives the weighted average embedding. This is the same concept token computation used in Lab 17's soft decoding, but now applied during training.

206.text: Ground-truth embeddings for the input sequence.

207.text: The blending: position 0 (BOS) always stays ground truth. For all other positions, mix between ground truth and the concept token from the previous position's prediction. Early in training (mix≈0), this is mostly ground truth. Late (mix≈1), mostly concept tokens, matching what the model will see at inference time.

209.text: Second forward pass: model processes the blended input and produces logits for the loss.

211.text: Standard path: just use token IDs when soft mixing is off.

217.text: Reduce the learning rate linearly toward zero as training progresses, allowing fine-grained convergence near the end.

218.text: Write the decayed learning rate into the optimizer's parameter group so the next update uses the reduced value.

219.text: Write the decayed learning rate into the optimizer's parameter group.

220.text: Apply the computed gradients to update all model weights one step toward lower loss.

222.text: Print the training loss at regular intervals so we can monitor convergence.

223.text: Include the mix ratio in the log when soft training, shows the curriculum progressing.

224.text: Print step, loss, and optional mix ratio.

230.text: Instantiate the baseline model that will be trained with standard teacher forcing and no concept token mixing.

231.text: Instantiate the soft-trained model that will learn with a curriculum of gradually increasing concept token mixing.

232.text: Copy weights so both start from the exact same initialization. This ensures any difference in output quality is due to the training method, not random init.

234.text: Train with standard teacher forcing (always ground-truth inputs).

235.text: Train with soft mixing curriculum (gradually replace ground truth with concept tokens).

241.text: Generate 20 names per configuration, enough samples to see meaningful entropy and quality differences.

242.text: Maximum possible entropy (uniform distribution), used to normalize entropy readings.

244.text: Set up the four test configurations: two models (standard vs. soft-trained) times two decoding modes (hard vs. soft).

245.text: Standard model with hard (discrete) decoding, the classic baseline.

246.text: Standard model with soft decoding, Lab 17's approach, applied to a model that never saw concept tokens during training.

247.text: Soft-trained model with hard decoding, tests whether soft training helps even with standard decoding.

248.text: Soft-trained model with soft decoding, the ideal combo: trained on concept tokens, generates with them.

249.text: End of the configuration list. Each entry specifies a model, decoding mode, and descriptive label for the comparison.

251.text: Turn off gradient computation for the entire generation phase to save memory and compute during inference.

253.text: Switch this configuration's model to evaluation mode for deterministic behavior during generation.

256.text: Generate the specified number of name samples for this configuration, collecting output and entropy data.

257.text: Generate one name with the specified mode and temperature.

259.text: Average entropy for this sample. Lower is better, means the model is more confident in its predictions.

260.text: Append this sample's average entropy to the list so we can compute the overall mean across all samples.

261.text: Show the first 10 generated names with their entropy scores for a quick visual comparison of quality.

262.text: Show name, entropy relative to maximum. The soft-trained + soft decoding combo should show the lowest entropy.

263.text: Print mean entropy across all samples, the definitive metric for comparing configurations.

labs.title: Disaggregated Serving
labs.tagline: Prefill and decode on separate workers.
labs.description:
LLM inference has two phases with very different compute profiles: prefill (process the entire prompt, compute-bound) and decode (generate tokens one at a time, memory-bound).

When both run on the same GPU, long prefills block short decodes, causing latency spikes. Disaggregated serving splits them onto separate workers: prefill workers process prompts and send KV caches to decode workers via a transfer mechanism.

This eliminates head-of-line blocking and lets you scale each phase independently. The lab simulates this with threads and queues, showing the scheduling and KV cache transfer logic used by production systems like Mooncake and DistServe.
learning_objectives: Implement prefill/decode disaggregation with thread-based workers
Analyze the compute profiles of prefill vs. decode phases
Design a scheduling strategy that eliminates head-of-line blocking

exercises.prompt: Add a third worker type that handles both prefill and decode. Compare the latency distribution against the disaggregated setup with 10 concurrent requests.
exercises.hint: A combined worker processes requests sequentially, so long prefills block short decodes. Measure p50 and p99 latency for both architectures.

exercises.prompt: Simulate a burst of 50 requests arriving at once. How does the queue depth grow for prefill vs. decode workers, and which becomes the bottleneck?
exercises.hint: Add logging to print queue sizes at each step. Prefill is compute-bound and usually slower per request, so its queue should grow faster under burst load.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — Disaggregated serving edition.

Same model as lab 03/12, but demonstrating disaggregated (split) inference:
the prefill phase (process the prompt, compute-bound) and the decode phase
(generate tokens one at a time, memory-bound) run on separate workers.

Production systems like Splitwise (Microsoft), DistServe, and TetriInfer
use this to avoid head-of-line blocking: a long prompt prefill on a shared
GPU stalls all the decode requests waiting behind it. Disaggregation lets
each phase run on hardware optimized for its bottleneck.

Based on "DistServe: Disaggregating Prefill and Decoding for Goodput-optimized
Large Language Model Serving" (Zhong et al., 2024),
https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.09670, and "Splitwise: Efficient generative LLM
inference using phase splitting" (Patel et al., 2024),
https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.18677. Also relevant: "TetriInfer: Disaggregated
LLM Inference on Heterogeneous GPUs" (2024). This lab simulates disaggregation
with threads and queues -- production systems transfer KV caches over
NVLink/RDMA between physical GPU workers.
"""

import math
import os
import random
import threading
import time
from collections import deque
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 16  # embedding dimension
n_head = 4  # number of attention heads
n_layer = 1  # number of layers
block_size = 16  # maximum sequence length
head_dim = n_embd // n_head  # dimension of each head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x, kv_cache=None):
        B, T_new, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T_new, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T_new, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T_new, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        if kv_cache is not None:
            k = torch.cat([kv_cache[0], k], dim=2)
            v = torch.cat([kv_cache[1], v], dim=2)
        T_total = k.shape[2]
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T_new, T_total, device=x.device, dtype=torch.bool), diagonal=T_total - T_new + 1)
        att = F.softmax(att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf")), dim=-1)
        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T_new, C)
        return self.wo(out), (k, v)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x, kv_cache=None):
        attn_out, new_cache = self.attn(self.norm1(x), kv_cache=kv_cache)
        x = x + attn_out
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x, new_cache


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx, past_caches=None, start_pos=0):
        _, T = idx.shape
        x = self.norm_in(self.wte(idx) + self.wpe(torch.arange(start_pos, start_pos + T, device=idx.device)))
        new_caches = []
        for i, layer in enumerate(self.layers):
            x, new_cache = layer(x, kv_cache=past_caches[i] if past_caches else None)
            new_caches.append(new_cache)
        return self.lm_head(x), new_caches

    def prefill(self, idx):
        return self.forward(idx)

    def decode_step(self, token_id, past_caches, pos):
        return self.forward(torch.tensor([[token_id]]), past_caches, pos)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
num_steps = 1000

for step in range(num_steps):
    doc = docs[step % len(docs)]
    tokens = [BOS] + [uchars.index(ch) for ch in doc] + [BOS]
    n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)
    input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
    targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)
    logits, _ = model(input_ids)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = 1e-2 * (1 - step / num_steps)
    optimizer.step()
    if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
        print(f"step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps:4d} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")

model.eval()
torch.set_grad_enabled(False)  # inference only from here

# Part 1: Measure prefill vs decode profiles
print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("PART 1: PREFILL vs DECODE — different compute profiles")
print("=" * 70)


def timed(fn, n_runs=50):
    for _ in range(5):
        fn()
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(n_runs):
        fn()
    return (time.perf_counter() - t0) / n_runs


def measure_profile(plen):
    idx = torch.tensor([[BOS] + [random.randint(0, vocab_size - 2) for _ in range(plen - 1)]])
    n_dec = min(5, block_size - plen)
    t_pre = timed(lambda: model.prefill(idx))
    _, init_caches = model.prefill(idx)

    def _decode():
        past, pos = init_caches, plen
        for _ in range(n_dec):
            _, past = model.decode_step(BOS, past, pos)
            pos += 1

    t_dec = timed(_decode) if n_dec > 0 else 0.0
    c = 2 * n_head * head_dim * n_layer
    return t_pre, t_dec, c * plen**2, sum(c * (plen + s) for s in range(n_dec))


print(
    f"\n{'Prompt':>8s} | {'Prefill (ms)':>12s} | {'Decode 5tok':>14s} | {'Prefill FLOPs':>13s} | {'Decode FLOPs':>12s}"
)
print("-" * 72)
for prompt_len in [2, 4, 8, 12]:
    tp, td, fp, fd = measure_profile(prompt_len)
    print(f"  {prompt_len:6d} | {tp * 1000:10.3f}  | {td * 1000:12.3f}  | {fp:13,} | {fd:12,}")

print("""
Prefill processes T tokens in ONE pass (compute-bound, high parallelism).
Decode processes 1 token per step (memory-bound, reads all weights each time).
On real GPUs, prefill saturates compute; decode wastes it waiting on memory.
""")

# Part 2: Simulate colocated vs disaggregated serving
print("=" * 70)
print("PART 2: COLOCATED vs DISAGGREGATED SERVING")
print("=" * 70)

temperature = 0.5  # in (0, 1], control the "creativity" of generated text, low to high
PREFILL_COST_MS = 0.5  # ms per prompt token (simulated)
DECODE_COST_MS = 0.3  # ms per decode step (simulated)


@dataclass
class Request:
    id: int
    prompt: list
    arrival_time: float
    max_decode_steps: int = 10
    first_token_time: float = 0.0
    finish_time: float = 0.0
    generated_tokens: list = field(default_factory=list)


def make_workload(n_requests=12, seed=999):
    rng = random.Random(seed)
    reqs = []
    for i in range(n_requests):
        plen = rng.randint(8, 12) if rng.random() < 0.3 else rng.randint(2, 3)
        prompt = [BOS] + [rng.randint(0, vocab_size - 2) for _ in range(plen - 1)]
        reqs.append(Request(id=i, prompt=prompt, arrival_time=i * 0.3e-3, max_decode_steps=block_size - plen))
    return reqs


def sim_prefill(prompt_tokens):
    logits, caches = model.prefill(torch.tensor([prompt_tokens]))
    time.sleep(len(prompt_tokens) * PREFILL_COST_MS / 1000)
    return logits, caches


def sim_decode(token_id, caches, pos):
    logits, caches = model.decode_step(token_id, caches, pos)
    time.sleep(DECODE_COST_MS / 1000)
    return logits, caches


def sample_token(logits):
    return torch.multinomial(F.softmax(logits[0, -1] / temperature, dim=-1), 1).item()


# Strategy 1: Colocated — one worker does both prefill and decode (FIFO)
def serve_colocated(requests):
    results, clock = [], time.perf_counter()
    for req in requests:
        dt = clock + req.arrival_time - time.perf_counter()
        if dt > 0:
            time.sleep(dt)
        logits, caches = sim_prefill(req.prompt)
        req.first_token_time = time.perf_counter() - clock
        tok, pos, gen = sample_token(logits), len(req.prompt), []
        for _ in range(req.max_decode_steps):
            if tok == BOS or pos >= block_size:
                break
            gen.append(tok)
            logits, caches = sim_decode(tok, caches, pos)
            tok, pos = sample_token(logits), pos + 1
        req.generated_tokens, req.finish_time = gen, time.perf_counter() - clock
        results.append(req)
    return results


# Strategy 2: Disaggregated — separate prefill and decode workers
def serve_disaggregated(requests):
    decode_queue, lock = deque(), threading.Lock()
    results, results_lock = [], threading.Lock()
    prefill_done, clock = threading.Event(), time.perf_counter()

    def prefill_worker():
        for req in requests:
            dt = clock + req.arrival_time - time.perf_counter()
            if dt > 0:
                time.sleep(dt)
            logits, caches = sim_prefill(req.prompt)
            req.first_token_time = time.perf_counter() - clock
            with lock:
                decode_queue.append((req, caches, sample_token(logits), len(req.prompt)))
        prefill_done.set()

    def decode_worker():
        active = []
        while True:
            with lock:
                while decode_queue:
                    req, caches, tok, pos = decode_queue.popleft()
                    active.append((req, caches, tok, pos, []))
            if not active:
                if prefill_done.is_set():
                    break
                time.sleep(0.1e-3)
                continue
            still_active = []
            for req, caches, tok, pos, gen in active:
                if tok == BOS or len(gen) >= req.max_decode_steps or pos >= block_size:
                    req.generated_tokens, req.finish_time = gen, time.perf_counter() - clock
                    with results_lock:
                        results.append(req)
                else:
                    gen.append(tok)
                    logits, caches = sim_decode(tok, caches, pos)
                    still_active.append((req, caches, sample_token(logits), pos + 1, gen))
            active = still_active

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=f) for f in (prefill_worker, decode_worker)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()
    return sorted(results, key=lambda r: r.id)


# Run both strategies
print("\nWorkload: 12 requests (mix of short and long prompts)\n")
workload = make_workload()
for req in workload:
    print(f"  req {req.id:2d}: {'LONG ' if len(req.prompt) > 5 else 'short'} prompt ({len(req.prompt):2d} tokens)")

print("\n--- Colocated (single worker) ---")
torch.manual_seed(2000)
results_coloc = serve_colocated(make_workload())

print("\n--- Disaggregated (prefill + decode workers) ---")
torch.manual_seed(2000)
results_disagg = serve_disaggregated(make_workload())

# Part 3: Compare results
print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("PART 3: RESULTS")
print("=" * 70)

print(
    f"\n{'Req':>4s} {'Prompt':>6s}  {'TTFT coloc':>10s} {'TTFT disagg':>11s} {'Speedup':>8s}  {'Name coloc':>12s}  Name disagg"
)
print("-" * 80)
for rc, rd in zip(results_coloc, results_disagg):
    plen = len(rc.prompt)
    speedup = rc.first_token_time / rd.first_token_time if rd.first_token_time > 0 else 0
    name_c = "".join(uchars[t] for t in rc.generated_tokens) or "(empty)"
    name_d = "".join(uchars[t] for t in rd.generated_tokens) or "(empty)"
    tag = " LONG" if plen > 5 else ""
    print(
        f"  {rc.id:2d}  {plen:2d}tok{tag:>5s}"
        f" {rc.first_token_time * 1000:6.1f} ms"
        f" {rd.first_token_time * 1000:8.1f} ms"
        f"  {speedup:5.1f}x"
        f"  {name_c:>12s}  {name_d}"
    )

avg_c = sum(r.first_token_time for r in results_coloc) / len(results_coloc)
avg_d = sum(r.first_token_time for r in results_disagg) / len(results_disagg)
print(f"\n  Avg TTFT:  colocated {avg_c * 1000:.1f} ms  |  disaggregated {avg_d * 1000:.1f} ms  ({avg_c / avg_d:.1f}x)")
t_c = max(r.finish_time for r in results_coloc)
t_d = max(r.finish_time for r in results_disagg)
print(f"  Total:     colocated {t_c * 1000:.1f} ms  |  disaggregated {t_d * 1000:.1f} ms")

# Part 4: Why disaggregation matters
print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("WHY DISAGGREGATED SERVING MATTERS")
print("=" * 70)
print("""
The two phases of LLM inference have different hardware profiles:
  Prefill: compute-bound (T tokens in parallel, O(T^2)). Wants more ALUs, FP8.
  Decode:  memory-bound (1 token/step, reads all weights). Wants high BW, HBM3e.

PROBLEM — colocated serving (one GPU does both):
  [====PREFILL req0====][dec 0][dec 0]...[==PREFILL req1==][dec 1]...
                                          ^ decode 1 blocked by prefill 1!

SOLUTION — disaggregate prefill and decode onto separate workers:
  Prefill GPU:  [==PREFILL 0==][==PREFILL 1==][PREFILL 2]
  Decode GPU:   [dec 0][dec 0][dec 1][dec 0]...  <- starts right after handoff

Benefits: lower TTFT, better throughput, reduced tail latency, cost-efficient
(fewer compute GPUs for prefill, more memory-BW GPUs for decode).

Production systems:
  - Splitwise (Microsoft, ISCA'24): 1.4x throughput via prefill/decode split
  - DistServe (OSDI'24): goodput-based placement across workers
  - TetriInfer (2024): prefill/decode on different SMs within one GPU
  - Mooncake (Moonshot AI, 2024): KV cache transfer over RDMA

The KV cache transfer is the key engineering challenge. For Llama 3 70B at
8K context, ~1 GB transfers over NVLink (900 GB/s) in ~1.1 ms — negligible
vs. the prefill time it unblocks.
""")

23.text: Disaggregated serving keeps the transformer math the same; the real change is where prefill and decode work happen and how they stop blocking each other.

24.text: Same tiny names task, so the systems lesson is not hidden behind a change in data or model complexity.

25.text: This lab simulates a workload, so reproducibility matters both for the training run and for the request-arrival pattern we want to analyze.

26.text: Threading is the star here because the lab is really about systems architecture: separate workers let prefill and decode stop fighting over the same execution lane.

27.text: Latency is the whole point. We care about time-to-first-token and throughput because serving architecture is judged by user-visible delay, not by elegance.

28.text: The queue is the handoff point between the two phases, where a finished prefill becomes decode-ready state instead of blocking later requests.

29.text: Each request carries its own prompt, timing, and decode state, because serving is really about how many such requests can coexist without stepping on each other.

31.text: PyTorch runs the actual model so the serving simulation still feels like real inference, even though the central lesson is scheduling rather than modeling.

32.text: Neural network building blocks like Linear and Embedding that make up the transformer's layers.

33.text: Functional API providing softmax, cross-entropy, and ReLU without module overhead.

35.text: Fix seeds for reproducible workload generation.

41.text: Construct the dataset file path relative to this script's location so it works from any working directory.

42.text: If the names dataset file doesn't exist locally, download it on the fly so the script is self-contained.

48.text: Read the dataset file where each line contains a single name for training.

49.text: Randomize training order so the model doesn't memorize alphabetical patterns from the dataset.

52.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters across all names, mapping each to a deterministic token ID.

53.text: The BOS token marks the beginning and end of each name sequence during training and generation.

54.text: Total vocabulary size: all unique characters plus the special BOS/EOS token.

60.text: Small model config, enough to demonstrate the serving architecture.

61.text: Split the embedding dimension across four heads for multi-head attention.

62.text: A single transformer layer keeps the model small and training fast for this demo.

63.text: Maximum sequence length of 16 tokens, matching the longest names in the dataset.

64.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the full embedding.

72.text: Scale the input by its inverse root-mean-square, bringing activations to roughly unit magnitude without learned parameters.

76.text: Self-attention WITH KV cache support, the distinction between prefill and decode lives here.

84.text: Accepts an optional KV cache: None for prefill (process all tokens), populated for decode (only new token's K/V are computed).

85.text: T_new is the full prompt length during prefill, but just 1 during decode.

86.text: Project queries for the new tokens only.

87.text: Project to keys for the new input tokens, same shape transformation as queries.

88.text: Project to values for the new input tokens, same shape transformation as queries.

89.text: If we have cached K/V from previous steps, concatenate the new K/V onto them.

90.text: Concatenate new keys with cached keys, the cache grows by one token each decode step.

91.text: Concatenate new values with cached values, growing the history by one token per decode step.

92.text: Total sequence length including cached history.

93.text: Attention scores: new queries attend to ALL keys (cached + new).

94.text: Causal mask adapted for the asymmetric case: T_new query rows vs T_total key columns. During decode, this is just 1 row attending to the full history.

95.text: Mask and softmax in one line.

96.text: Weighted values, reshape to (B, T_new, C).

97.text: Return output AND the updated cache, the caller decides whether to keep it.

100.text: The standard feed-forward network: expand, activate, and contract back to embedding dimension.

103.text: Expand the hidden state to four times the embedding dimension through the first linear layer.

104.text: Contract the activated representation back to the embedding dimension through the second linear layer.

106.text: Apply ReLU activation between the two linear layers, introducing the needed nonlinearity.

110.text: Transformer block that threads KV caches through.

113.text: Normalize activations before the attention projections so Q/K/V computations see inputs with consistent scale.

114.text: The attention sublayer with optional KV cache: during prefill it processes all tokens, during decode just one new token.

115.text: RMSNorm before the MLP ensures the feed-forward weights see well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

116.text: The feed-forward network sublayer that processes each token independently after attention.

118.text: Block forward: attention with cache, MLP, return updated cache.

120.text: Add the attention output to the original input via a skip connection, preserving the representation and enabling gradient flow.

121.text: Add the MLP output via a second residual connection, letting the feed-forward network only refine rather than replace.

122.text: Return both the updated hidden states and the new KV cache, so the next layer or decode step can continue from here.

125.text: Full GPT with explicit prefill and decode methods, the foundation for disaggregated serving.

128.text: Table mapping each token ID to a dense vector in the embedding space.

129.text: Learned position vectors that tell the model where each token sits. Added to token embeddings before the transformer.

130.text: RMSNorm on the combined embeddings before the transformer, stabilizing the first layer's input scale.

131.text: The stack of transformer blocks that form the core of the model's processing pipeline.

132.text: Final linear layer that projects hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for next-token prediction.

133.text: Walk every submodule and apply the weight initialization function so all parameters start with well-scaled random values.

137.text: Initialize all weights from a small Gaussian distribution to prevent exploding activations at start.

140.text: Forward pass with KV cache support. start_pos offsets the position embeddings, during decode, positions continue from where prefill left off.

142.text: Position embeddings offset by start_pos, during decode step 5, this produces position embedding for position 5, not 0.

143.text: Collect updated caches from each layer.

145.text: Retrieve the cached KV state for this layer from the previous step (or None for the first pass during prefill).

146.text: Save this layer's updated KV cache so the next decode step can reuse the computed keys and values.

147.text: Return the predicted logits plus all updated layer caches, giving the caller everything needed to continue generation.

149.text: Prefill is the wide, heavy phase: process the whole prompt at once, saturate compute, and prepare the KV state that decode will later consume token by token.

150.text: Just a forward pass with no prior cache, all tokens processed together.

152.text: Decode is the opposite shape: one new token, lots of KV reads, very little arithmetic. That mismatch is exactly why colocating it with prefill wastes hardware.

153.text: Single token forward pass with position offset.

159.text: Run on CPU since the model is small enough that GPU overhead would not help.

160.text: Create the model, move it to the device, and display its parameter count for verification.

163.text: Adam optimizer, same settings as all labs.

164.text: Train for 1000 steps, enough for this small model to learn basic character-level name patterns from the dataset.

166.text: Standard next-token prediction training loop, processing one name per step with teacher forcing.

167.text: Pick one training example per step, cycling through the shuffled dataset.

169.text: Truncate the token sequence to fit within the model's positional embedding table and context window.

170.text: Prepare the input tensor containing all tokens except the last one in the sequence.

171.text: Prepare the target tensor containing all tokens except the first, shifted by one position.

172.text: Forward pass, ignoring caches during training.

173.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the correct next tokens at each position.

174.text: Reset all parameter gradients to zero before computing the new backward pass.

175.text: Compute gradients of the loss with respect to every model parameter via backpropagation.

176.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate over the course of training for smoother convergence toward the end.

177.text: Set the learning rate to a linearly decayed value based on how far along training we are.

178.text: Apply the computed gradients to update all model weights one step toward lower loss.

182.text: Switch to eval mode for inference.

183.text: Disable gradient tracking globally, everything from here is inference.

191.text: Timing utility: warms up with 5 runs, then times 50 runs for stable measurement.

192.text: Warmup runs, let caches and JIT settle.

193.text: Execute the function once during warmup to trigger any lazy initialization or JIT compilation.

194.text: Record the wall-clock start time so we can measure total execution duration.

195.text: Execute the target function 50 times to get a stable average, smoothing out per-run timing noise.

197.text: Compute the average wall-clock time per run by dividing total elapsed time by the number of runs.

200.text: Measure the compute profile difference between prefill and decode at various prompt lengths.

201.text: Generate a random sequence of token IDs to simulate a prompt of the specified length for benchmarking.

202.text: Decode up to 5 tokens after the prompt.

203.text: Time the prefill phase: ALL prompt tokens processed in one pass.

204.text: Run a prefill forward pass on the prompt and capture the resulting KV caches that decode will build upon.

206.text: Time the decode phase: generate tokens one at a time.

208.text: Each decode step processes one token.

209.text: One token forward pass with KV cache.

212.text: Measure how long the autoregressive decode phase takes, separate from the prefill measurement.

214.text: Prefill FLOPs scale as O(T^2), quadratic in prompt length. Decode FLOPs scale linearly per step but add up.

221.text: Test with increasing prompt lengths to show how prefill scales quadratically while decode stays relatively flat.

222.text: Run the profiling function at each prompt length and collect the timing results for the comparison table.

223.text: Print timing and FLOPs for this prompt length.

236.text: Controls how peaked the sampling distribution is. Lower temperature means more deterministic output; higher means more variety.

237.text: Simulated prefill cost: 0.5ms per prompt token. On real GPUs, this would be actual compute time.

238.text: Simulated decode cost: 0.3ms per step. Cheaper per-step than prefill because it's just one token.

242.text: A serving request: prompt, timing metadata, and generated tokens.

243.text: A numeric ID that uniquely identifies this request in the serving system.

245.text: When this request arrives in the system.

246.text: Upper limit on how many tokens to generate per request, bounding the decode phase for this simulation.

247.text: Time-to-first-token: the key latency metric for user experience.

249.text: List that collects the tokens generated for this request during the decode phase.

252.text: Create a realistic workload: mix of short prompts (fast prefill) and long prompts (slow prefill). The long prompts are the problem, they block everything in colocated serving.

255.text: Loop through the specified number of requests, creating each with a random prompt and staggered arrival time.

256.text: 30% chance of a long prompt (8-12 tokens), these are the "heavy" requests that cause head-of-line blocking.

257.text: Fill the prompt with random token IDs drawn from the vocabulary to simulate a realistic request payload.

258.text: Stagger arrival times by 0.3ms, simulating real request arrivals.

262.text: Simulate a prefill operation: run the actual model AND sleep to simulate GPU time.

263.text: Real forward pass to get logits and KV caches.

264.text: Sleep proportional to prompt length, longer prompts take longer to prefill.

268.text: Simulate a decode step: real model computation + simulated GPU time.

269.text: Real single-token forward pass with KV cache.

274.text: Sample one token from the model's output distribution.

275.text: Scale the logits by temperature, apply softmax, and sample one token. This is the actual character generation step.

279.text: Colocated serving: ONE worker does both prefill AND decode. Processes requests FIFO, a long prefill blocks ALL subsequent requests.

281.text: Handle one request at a time in FIFO order: prefill its prompt, then decode all tokens before moving to the next.

282.text: Wait until the request's arrival time.

283.text: Sleep if the request hasn't arrived yet.

285.text: Prefill the prompt, if this is a LONG prompt, all subsequent requests wait.

286.text: Record time-to-first-token, this is terrible for requests stuck behind long prefills.

287.text: Sample the first token and start decoding.

288.text: Decode loop: generate tokens one at a time.

289.text: Stop on BOS (end token) or context limit.

291.text: Append each newly generated token to this request's output list, building the name character by character.

292.text: One decode step with KV cache.

293.text: Sample next token and advance position.

300.text: Disaggregated serving: SEPARATE prefill and decode workers connected by a queue. Prefill doesn't block decode. The key insight: while one worker prefills a long prompt, the other worker continues decoding in-flight requests.

301.text: Thread-safe queue connects prefill worker to decode worker.

302.text: Results collection with lock for thread safety.

303.text: Signal when prefill is done; wall-clock baseline.

305.text: The prefill worker owns the wide prompt-processing phase, then hands finished KV state across the queue instead of hanging around to decode tokens itself.

307.text: Wait for each request's arrival time.

310.text: Prefill the prompt, runs independently of any ongoing decoding.

311.text: Record time-to-first-token, now only depends on THIS request's prefill, not previous requests.

312.text: Push the prefilled request state onto the thread-safe queue so the decode worker can pick it up without data races.

313.text: Hand off the prefilled state (KV caches, first token, position) to the decode worker.

314.text: Signal that all prefills are done.

316.text: The decode worker lives on the other side of that handoff, interleaving many in-flight requests while the prefill worker keeps chewing through new prompts.

317.text: Dictionary of requests currently in the decode phase, keyed by ID. The decode worker processes all of these each iteration.

318.text: Keep running until prefill is done and all decodes finish.

319.text: Poll the queue for any newly prefilled requests that the prefill worker has handed off for decoding.

320.text: Drain the queue, grab all available prefilled requests.

321.text: Unpack the handoff: request, KV caches, first sampled token, position.

322.text: Add to active set with an empty generated tokens list.

323.text: If nothing active and prefill not done, briefly wait.

324.text: Test whether the prefill worker has signaled completion, so the decode worker knows when no more requests are coming.

328.text: Process one decode step for each active request.

330.text: Evaluate stopping conditions for each active request: did it generate BOS (end token) or hit the maximum length?

331.text: Request finished: record timing and results.

332.text: Acquire the results lock before writing, since the main thread reads results after both workers finish.

335.text: Continue generating: append token, decode one step, sample next.

336.text: One decode step with KV cache.

338.text: Filter out finished requests so we only continue decoding the ones that still have tokens left to generate.

340.text: Spin up one thread for the prefill worker and one for the decode worker, which will run concurrently.

341.text: Start both workers, they run in parallel.

342.text: Launch both threads to begin parallel execution of the prefill and decode workers.

343.text: Block until both worker threads complete, ensuring all requests have finished processing before returning results.

344.text: Join the second worker thread. After this returns, all generation work is done and results are ready.

345.text: Sort results by request ID for clean comparison.

349.text: Show the workload configuration: how many requests, their prompt lengths, and arrival pattern for context.

350.text: Generate the test workload of requests with mixed prompt lengths and staggered arrival times.

352.text: Label long vs short prompts, long ones are the troublemakers.

355.text: Run the colocated strategy where a single worker handles both prefill and decode in series.

359.text: Run the disaggregated strategy where separate prefill and decode workers operate in parallel.

360.text: Same seed ensures same model outputs.

371.text: Compare each request between the two strategies.

372.text: Extract how many tokens were in this request's prompt for the comparison table.

373.text: TTFT speedup: how much faster was disaggregated for this request?

374.text: Decode generated tokens back to characters.

375.text: Decode the disaggregated-generated tokens back to characters for the side-by-side name comparison.

385.text: Average TTFT across all requests, the headline metric.

386.text: Compute the average time-to-first-token across all requests in the disaggregated strategy.

387.text: Print the TTFT comparison. Disaggregated should show significantly lower average TTFT, especially for requests that arrived after long prompts.

388.text: Total wall-clock time for all requests to complete.

389.text: Compute the total wall-clock time for the disaggregated strategy to complete all requests.

390.text: Show the total completion time for both strategies side by side to quantify the end-to-end throughput difference.

labs.title: Self-Improving Model
labs.tagline: The model generates data, scores it, and retrains on its own best outputs.
labs.description:
A model that improves itself through iterative self-training. Each round: generate candidate names, score them with a verifiable quality function, keep only the best, and retrain on the mix of real data plus self-generated data.

This is the core pattern behind STaR (Self-Taught Reasoner), self-rewarding language models, and Karpathy's autoresearch project. The key insight: keeping only verified good outputs prevents the collapse that naive self-training causes. A bigram-statistics scorer acts as the verifier, making the lab fully self-contained — no external LLM needed.

Watch the quality metric improve across rounds as the model bootstraps from its own best generations.
learning_objectives: Implement the generate-score-filter-retrain self-improvement loop
Explain why filtering is essential to prevent self-training collapse
Compare self-improved output quality against the baseline

exercises.prompt: Remove the quality filter and keep ALL self-generated names for retraining. Does the model still improve, or does it collapse?
exercises.hint: Without filtering, the model retrains on its own mistakes, amplifying errors. Watch the quality metric — it should degrade rather than improve.

exercises.prompt: Change the mix ratio from 30% self-generated to 80% self-generated data. How does this affect the speed and stability of improvement?
exercises.hint: More self-generated data means faster adaptation but higher risk of distribution drift. Track quality over rounds for both settings.

labs.code:
"""
microGPT — Self-improving model edition.

A tiny character-level GPT that iteratively improves itself by generating
candidate outputs, scoring them with a verifiable quality function, and
retraining on its own best generations alongside the original data.

This implements a simplified form of filtered self-training, closest in spirit
to "STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning with Reasoning" (Zelikman et al., 2022,
https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.14465), which keeps only verified-correct
self-generated rationales. Karpathy's autoresearch project
(https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch) applies the same
generate-evaluate-keep loop to hyperparameter optimization. Broader related
work includes "Self-Rewarding Language Models" (Yuan et al., 2024,
https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.10020), which uses a learned self-judge rather
than a fixed scorer, and "SPIN" (Chen et al., 2024,
https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.01335), which uses distribution-matching self-play.

This lab uses a fixed handcrafted scorer (bigram statistics) as the verifier,
which is simpler than the learned judges in Self-Rewarding LMs or the
distribution-matching in SPIN. No external LLM is needed.
"""

import math
import os
import random

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dataset & Tokenizer
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
input_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "..", "..", "data", "input.txt")
if not os.path.exists(input_path):
    import urllib.request

    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/makemore/refs/heads/master/names.txt"
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, input_path)

docs = [l.strip() for l in open(input_path).read().strip().split("\n") if l.strip()]
random.shuffle(docs)
print(f"num docs: {len(docs)}")

uchars = sorted(set("".join(docs)))
char_to_id = {ch: i for i, ch in enumerate(uchars)}
BOS = len(uchars)
vocab_size = len(uchars) + 1
print(f"vocab size: {vocab_size}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Quality scorer — the "verifier" that decides which self-generations to keep
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# We build a bigram model from the REAL training data. This serves as a
# ground-truth quality signal: generated names that match real-name statistics
# score higher. In STaR, the verifier checks logical correctness; here it
# checks "does this look like a real name?"


def build_bigram_scorer(names):
    """Build a log-probability scorer from bigram statistics of real names."""
    counts = {}
    for name in names:
        seq = [BOS] + [char_to_id[ch] for ch in name] + [BOS]
        for a, b in zip(seq, seq[1:]):
            counts[(a, b)] = counts.get((a, b), 0) + 1
    # Add-1 (Laplace) smoothing: P(b|a) = (count(a,b) + 1) / (count(a) + V)
    total_per_context = {}
    for (a, _), c in counts.items():
        total_per_context[a] = total_per_context.get(a, 0) + c
    log_probs = {}
    for (a, b), c in counts.items():
        log_probs[(a, b)] = math.log((c + 1) / (total_per_context[a] + vocab_size))
    # Default for unseen bigrams: use per-context denominator when available
    default_lp_per_context = {a: math.log(1 / (t + vocab_size)) for a, t in total_per_context.items()}
    # Fallback for completely unseen contexts
    default_lp_unknown = math.log(1 / vocab_size)
    return log_probs, default_lp_per_context, default_lp_unknown


def score_name(name, log_probs, default_lp_ctx, default_lp_unk):
    """Score a generated name by average bigram log-probability with length penalty."""
    if len(name) < 3 or len(name) > 15:
        return -10.0  # reject too-short or too-long names
    seq = [BOS] + [char_to_id.get(ch, 0) for ch in name if ch in char_to_id] + [BOS]
    total = 0.0
    for a, b in zip(seq, seq[1:]):
        total += log_probs.get((a, b), default_lp_ctx.get(a, default_lp_unk))
    avg_lp = total / len(seq)
    # Penalize names far from the typical length (mean ~6 chars in the dataset)
    length_penalty = -0.1 * abs(len(name) - 6)
    return avg_lp + length_penalty


bigram_lp, default_lp_ctx, default_lp_unk = build_bigram_scorer(docs)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Model (same architecture as lab 03)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_embd = 32
n_head = 4
n_layer = 1
block_size = 16
head_dim = n_embd // n_head


class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, _dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.eps = eps

    def forward(self, x):
        return x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)


class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wq = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, n_head, head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(head_dim)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        att = F.softmax(att, dim=-1)
        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)


class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(n_embd, 4 * n_embd, bias=False)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(4 * n_embd, n_embd, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc2(F.relu(self.fc1(x)))


class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.mlp = MLP()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class MicroGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.wte = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.wpe = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.norm_in = RMSNorm(n_embd)
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([Block() for _ in range(n_layer)])
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(module):
        if isinstance(module, (nn.Linear, nn.Embedding)):
            nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.08)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok_emb = self.wte(idx)
        pos_emb = self.wpe(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.norm_in(tok_emb + pos_emb)
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x)
        return self.lm_head(x)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training and generation helpers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
device = "cpu"
temperature = 0.5


def train_on_data(model, train_docs, num_steps, base_lr=1e-2):
    """Train the model on a list of name strings."""
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=base_lr, betas=(0.85, 0.99), eps=1e-8)
    total_loss = 0
    for step in range(num_steps):
        doc = train_docs[step % len(train_docs)]
        tokens = [BOS] + [char_to_id[ch] for ch in doc] + [BOS]
        n = min(block_size, len(tokens) - 1)
        input_ids = torch.tensor([tokens[:n]], device=device)
        targets = torch.tensor([tokens[1 : n + 1]], device=device)
        logits = model(input_ids)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        lr_t = base_lr * (1 - step / num_steps)
        for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
            pg["lr"] = lr_t
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()
        if (step + 1) % 200 == 0 or step == 0:
            print(f"  step {step + 1:4d} / {num_steps} | loss {loss.item():.4f}")
    return total_loss / num_steps


@torch.no_grad()
def generate_names(model, n_samples=100):
    """Generate n_samples names from the model."""
    model.eval()
    names = []
    for _ in range(n_samples):
        tokens = [BOS]
        for _ in range(block_size):
            idx = torch.tensor([tokens[-block_size:]], device=device)
            logits = model(idx)
            logits = logits[0, -1] / temperature
            probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            token_id = torch.multinomial(probs, 1).item()
            if token_id == BOS:
                break
            tokens.append(token_id)
        name = "".join(uchars[t] for t in tokens[1:])
        if name:
            names.append(name)
    model.train()
    return names


def evaluate_quality(names):
    """Compute average quality score for a list of names."""
    scores = [score_name(n, bigram_lp, default_lp_ctx, default_lp_unk) for n in names]
    return sum(scores) / len(scores) if scores else -10.0


# ===========================================================================
# Phase 1: Initial training on real data
# ===========================================================================
print("=" * 60)
print("PHASE 1: Initial training on real data")
print("=" * 60)

model = MicroGPT().to(device)
print(f"num params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())}")

initial_steps = 800
avg_loss = train_on_data(model, docs, initial_steps)
print(f"initial training: {initial_steps} steps, avg loss {avg_loss:.4f}")

# Generate baseline samples
baseline_names = generate_names(model, 200)
baseline_quality = evaluate_quality(baseline_names)
print(f"baseline quality: {baseline_quality:.4f}")
print(f"baseline samples: {', '.join(baseline_names[:10])}")
